review_text: In one word: "Exceptional"  It has been around ten years since I have played Axiom Verge 1, so I am not going to try to compare the two since it has been so long for me.  On its own, the game is excellent and near the apex of the Metroidvania genre.  This is less "Metroidvania" and more "Super Metroid."  The game borrows very liberally from the old SNES classic.  Even so, it still has a unique flair to it that you won't see in any other game.  It's a shame that this game didn't get near the attention that the original did.  It is excellent in every respect.
Assim, interessante, mas o 1 é melhor.

我不满意的几个点： 一开始就有登山镐，但攀岩技能居然中期才能获取； 近战武器登山镐，后来换成铜锄头，可初始敌人就有电棒，这合理吗； 远程武器是回旋镖，笑死，敌人各种霰弹枪，狙击枪，导弹，激光； 机器人的武器居然是溜溜球你敢信，我真的无语； 还有各种能力、技能都装在陶罐里，过于传统了，关键打碎之后就像是召唤了阿拉丁一样，一股烟雾冒出来…… 游戏的BGM，怎么说呢，听着有股咖喱味，不知道是不是我心理作用……  优点： 地图巨大，各种卡关需要不同获取不同技能后才能通过，重度探索党可以狂喜； 辅助设置里可以降低敌人伤害和提升自身攻击力，非常人性化；

The controls are an improvement over the original Axiom Verge and the weapon selection is better designed - instead of loads of useless weapons, there's a limited number of them. Unfortunately, that's pretty much where the improvements end. The game is overall just not as interesting: the level designs, the story, the enemies, the bosses - it all feels like just a worse game. The fundamental switch from the ranged-first combat of the original to melee-first combat makes it play like a completely different game. The hacking concept is neat, but ultimately just a gimmick that a number of other games do better.

Holds up well next to AV1, just like the first game the writing feels stiff and I found myself focusing more on the gameplay than the story. Overall a good metroidvania.

(Данный обзор написан после прохождения первой и второй части подряд)  Насчёт второй части. Когда я начинал играть, я сразу понял... ну игра тоже хорошая... ну я не думал, что игра вообще другая! Почти всё, что было в первой части было улучшено или изменено, либо вообще заменено полностью.  Начнём с того, что вернулось из первой, но было подвергнуто улучшению, или изменению: 1. Взаимодействие с окружением и противниками - В первой был разрушитель адреса, к-рый позволял легально ломать логику некоторых элементов игры. Например: В одной из локаций были враги, что стреляли лазером, но при изменении, они начинали не только атаковать своих соратников, но и ломать некоторые стены, к-рые не можете ломать вы! И это только один пример такого! Во второй части, было заменено на поле, к-рое могло устраивать взлом. Во первых оно удобнее, за счёт области вокруг игрока, а не строго в сторону наведения. Во вторых... элементов взаимодействия с врагами и окружением стало меньше. В игре буквально одного врага можно было использовать как платформу, и то для в этом мало толку. Теперь в основном только для переманивания врагов на свою сторону или открытия дверей. 2. Графика и звуковые эффекты, ну и меню (о визуале потом) - Графика (в техническом плане) стала лучше, чем в первой части. По ощущениям более аккуратные цвета, особенно линии на границе. Банально не так выбивается из взгляда и выглядит более цельно (весь обзор это имхо). Звуковые эффекты те же... всё! Меню почти те же, но к примеру меню карты улучшено. Самое главное ты видишь структуру комнаты и это очень удобно в исследовании. 3. Механика дрона, почти такая-же но гораздо продуманнее и лучше.    Наверное это всё что я смог выделить здесь а дальше...  Абсолютно по другому: 1. Боевая система - Во первых: вы теперь ближник (дальний бой остался в виде бумеранга, но он почти бесполезен), а значит был убран контактный урон. Честно хорошо, что так сделали. Во вторых: были изменены боссы, конкретно боссов... их нет, ну кроме финального, всех остальных нет. Теперь на локациях можно встретить очень больших противников. Их убийство необязательно, но советуется, если хотите прокачаться. 2. Прокачка - В прошлой части её не было. Основным улучшением были предметы и оружие, теперь же есть ещё и прокачка. Ты можешь прокачать ХП, боёвку, взлом (необходимо, если будете зачищать вообще всё в игре.) и дрон. Предметом прокачки выступают фляги апокалипсиса. Их можно найти по пути, в секретках, а также выбить с недо боссов.  3. Исследование - Настолько перелопачен, что я не могу его назвать тем же, поэтому он в этом списке. Структура карты теперь другая, так что убрали импровизированный фаст тревел по голове русалки (не спрашиваете). Вместо этого для быстрого перемещения можно просто телепортироваться, через точки сохранения. Самое главное изменение, был добавлен разлом. Альтернативное измерение, к-рое также можно исследовать и нужное для прохождения в те или иные места, в к-рые невозможно попасть из реального мира. Проход только дроном. 4. Визуальный стиль и музыка - Абсолютно не тоже самое, что в первой части. Враги - это не обитатели Судры (или Шудры?), а какие-то роботы, на службе какого-то ИИ (Сами играете короче). Также очевидно больше нет (таком количестве) Чего-то неземного. Большая часть локаций, это заброшенные города, леса и горы. а также пещеры,  к-рые выглядят как... пещеры. То есть большая часть игры стала выглядеть более приземлённой, нежели в первой части. Ну и музыка. Честно слабее чем в первой части, но всё же можно найти прикольные треки, а ещё они теперь другие. Если в первый в основном было какой-то техно. То тут у нас этника с вокалом и какими-то восточными мотивами, что тоже неплохо.  В остальной очень достойная игра! И опять-же вообще не похожа на первую часть!

After finishing the first Axiom Verge, I was pretty excited to jump into the sequel. I’d had both games sitting in my library for a while. But even before starting, something about Axiom Verge 2 felt off. The screenshots already gave me doubts. It’s not just the change in art style (although that’s definitely part of it). The game looked more like Starbound now than the original Axiom Verge, and there’s also this weird “mobile-like” feel to the UI. Early on it’s not too noticeable, but as you unlock more tools, your screen starts getting cluttered with icons instead of the clean minimalism the first game had. The change of protagonist also hits harder than I expected. The first game had really strong themes around identity, and the ending pretty clearly hinted that Trace would return. Instead, we get a completely different character, and it just doesn’t carry the same weight. On top of that, I couldn’t help but remember how long this game was locked to Epic Games Store, which didn’t exactly boost my confidence going in. The overall atmosphere is very different too. The original leaned heavily into biopunk, while the sequel goes for something more dreamy and “cozy.” Again, Starbound is probably the closest comparison. It’s not that the atmosphere is bad, audio, visuals, and gameplay still work together well, but the shift feels too drastic. Personally, I don’t think moving away from the tone of the first game was a good decision. The visuals are simpler and clearer, but also less interesting. The game just feels more generic. In the original, you were crawling through strange alien corridors, occasionally reaching the surface. Here, you’re mostly in very familiar earthy environments: snowy landscapes, research stations, mines, that kind of thing. The storytelling took a hit too. In the first game, you’re thrown in with almost no context, and the mystery slowly unfolds as you play. In the sequel, everything is explained to you upfront. Who the main character is, what her motivation is, what’s going on. There’s barely any intrigue. You even get a “guide” character who constantly explains things. Yes, the first game had something similar, but that character was mysterious and barely communicated with you. Here, it feels like the game refuses to let you figure anything out on your own. I stopped caring about notes and lore pretty quickly, which is the opposite of how I played the first game, where I actively hunted for every piece of story. Exploration also feels weaker. Now you have a compass that basically tells you where to go at all times. In the first game, figuring out your path was a core part of the experience, that’s the foundation of metroidvanias. I only got truly lost once in the entire first game, but it felt natural. Here, you always know where to go, which makes everything feel more linear. It quickly turns into “just follow the marker.” The game also shifts away from sci-fi into something closer to mysticism and esotericism. Enemy design (still robots, but now with Sumerian influences), environments, and even the music lean heavily into these almost Middle Eastern motifs. It’s not necessarily bad, but it feels disconnected from what made the original interesting. Honestly, aside from some familiar sounds and UI elements, I don’t really understand why this is called Axiom Verge 2. There are some good ideas. The proto-stealth system, where enemies don’t notice you immediately, is interesting. Some drone sections are fun, fast travel between save points is a great addition, and hacking is more flexible now, you can choose how you want to affect objects. But none of that really saves the experience for me. At some point, I just lost the desire to keep playing. The first game felt like a true standout, a unique, memorable project. This, on the other hand, feels generic and unfocused. Maybe the developer wanted to do something different, and that’s fine, but then it probably shouldn’t have been called Axiom Verge 2. As a sequel, it just doesn’t deliver. I can’t really recommend it. If you’re absolutely starving for metroidvanias, you might still find something here. But honestly, you’re better off just playing the original again.

Review que é só uma série de vários pensamentos sem uma ordem específica:  À primeira vista, a impressão é que, diferente do primeiro jogo (que tinha bastante inspiração em Metroid), desta vez a filosofia parece ser algo entre um Castlevania e Zelda. O mapa não é mais tão claustrofóbico quanto antes e a atmosfera é mais aberta.  A comparação com Zelda se dá pelo fato de que, mesmo com uma filosofia parecida entre os estilos de design de exploração com "lock and key", aqui os upgrades costumam ser muito mais ferramentas para prosseguir na área, que, diga-se de passagem, acabaram ficando bem mais lineares, sem muito backtracking importante, já que não existe nada muito único para se voltar atrás que não seja apenas ponto de upgrade ou fragmentos de vida. Também, na temática de ser mais aberto, o jogo possui alguns bosses opcionais espalhados pelo mapa para você enfrentar.  A comparação com Castlevania também ocorre porque, de início, a impressão no começo é que, como agora você possui ataques com armas melee e ranged, com estatísticas de dano e velocidade de ataque (em vez de uma arma de longo alcance única), é que esse elemento seria algo central. Porém, isso não se faz tão presente no jogo pois, tirando as duas primeiras armas que você adquire no começo, no mapa inteiro existem apenas ~4 armas de verdade para adquirir, realmente não entendi a lógica de se dar ao trabalho de fazer parecer que isso seria algo integral do jogo.  Parando um pouco com as reclamações: mesmo com uma certa linearidade na progressão do mapa, o jogo acaba compensando com a mecânica de "Breach", que é basicamente um segundo mapa sobreposto que você consegue acessar para interagir com o mundo normal, burlando barreiras e outros obstáculos. A mecânica de hacking também teve um bom avanço em comparação ao primeiro (onde era apenas um dos tipos de tiro da arma); aqui ela faz parte do loop de gameplay tanto para combate quanto para exploração. Também foi feita de uma forma que consegue ter um meio-termo: não é obrigatório focar nela, mas o jogo também não te pune se você não quiser interagir com a mecânica. Vale dizer que, se você não é fã do estilo de narrativa não intrusiva (de ser jogado no meio dos acontecimentos sem muito contexto e deixar pra você entender oque esta acontecendo), aqui a história segue um estilo mais normal, com a personagem tendo um objetivo claro desde o começo e a narrativa tendo um começo claro. Ainda assim, o jogo não poupa na apresentação de conceitos e nomes confusos.  Como considerações finais, eu ainda recomendo se você for fã de metroidvania. A experiência de exploração com o segundo mapa é bem legal, mas se estiver atrás de algo parecido com o primeiro, pode não ser sua praia. Também, para quem gosta de conquistas, o jogo possui todos os desafios padrão do gênero, desde pegar todos os coletáveis até speedrun. Ponto bônus por igual ao primeiro, ter uma conquista incomum te pedindo para fazer uma run low%.  P.S. eu nunca joguei um zelda então posso estar fazendo comparação sem sentido

Axiom Verge 2 is a Metroidvania that is close, but not quite there. It’s all the more disappointing since it’s definitely a step down from the original.  [h3]Story[/h3]  This is the one thing that’s on par with the original. Both had boring and uninteresting stories. There’s vague intrigue about an advanced/ancient civilization, but there’s too much talking & explanation for any world building to really get you wondering about the possibilities. Characters just explain the intrigue away with any sort of nuance. These characters additionally have little to no personality and are simply a means to progress the plot. “Go here and read the computer terminal” the plot. Save the universe - the end. Its very dull for how colorful the world is.  [h3]Gameplay[/h3]  This is what’s the most disappointing about this game. In Axiom Verge you had all these cool guns that had all these niche uses, which made you cycle weapons all the time to counter all types of enemies. This game has, for some inexplicable reason, switched to melee combat. It’s not even interesting combat, mainly due to lack of difficulty. There’s also the issue that a lot of melee combat Metroidvanias have, which is where the weapon with the biggest AoE is the best weapon.   You do get some tech options in combat in this game, and they’re overpowered as hell. There’s this invincibility nuke that you get in the beginning of the game that just nukes all enemies & bosses. Those bosses, by the way, have been completely gutted when compared to the first game. First game, challenging bosses that block the story path. In this game, you have optional world bosses that you can just jump on top of and AoE nuke three times. There are two story “bosses” in this game, but you get resurrections in these fights. ?????? This INCLUDES the final boss. What the ♥♥♥♥ is the point in fighting a boss if you can’t die? What’s the point in playing a game where I can’t lose? There is a lore reason, but ♥♥♥♥ your lore reason. If your story or lore makes your game boring, then it ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sucks.  The boring nature of this game is further compounded by the excessive use of checkpoints. Even when you die in this game, the checkpoints are so close together, and the enemies rarely block you. No deaths have any tension, no enemy has any pressure, no boss any presence.  [h3]Exploration[/h3]  The only real thing that this game has going for it is the exploration. Environments pop, enemies are visually unique, and there is really striking pixel art animation. The combination of abilities stands out from other Metroidvanias’ movesets, which makes moving around the map and collecting everything quite fun. This is especially underlined by the game’s two world mechanic, while not perfect (they thematically clash & don’t overlap when it’s the least convenient), it is cool to have to think across two worlds when trying to find the next collectible.   There also isn’t exactly ‘flow’ movement, as it’s a bit stiff. Jumping around is awkward at times, and there are some cheesy movement options. You also have to cycle abilities a lot, and it’s done really poorly. I feel like they could’ve used more buttons on the controller to lower the amount of menuing, but it’s not the biggest issue.  [h3]Conclusion[/h3] If you’re drawn in by the amazing pixel art in the trailer or loved the original Axiom Verge, just beware. Unless you’re exclusively driven by exploration, I’d avoid this since the gameplay and story are completely dull.

A great metroidvania with excellent music, progression, weapons and atmosphere. The combat can get a bit repetitive, and is more melee-focused this time, which is very different from the first game. The exploration with the multi-world system is by far the best part of this game.  My only real issue with the first game were the bosses, and they are, unfortunately, even worse in this game. They are not challenging, fun or creative, but that's not the focus of the game.

Axiom Verge 2 is ultimately an uneven experience. The game truly shines in its satisfying Metroidvania-style world, which rewards exploration and discovery. Unfortunately, the story feels nonsensical at times and somewhat disconnected from the first game, making it difficult to stay invested. The audio design is also mixed. While the game leans into a retro-inspired sound, some effects can come across as unpleasant. That said, the soundtrack features several genuinely memorable tracks, particularly those in the Breach, the parallel dimension you explore with your drone. Despite its flaws, I still enjoyed the game overall, mainly for its exploration, and would recommend it to the fans of the Metroidvania genre.
3/10  Firstly, the gameplay has nothing to do with the first game.  Secondly, it's boring.

I really liked the first Axiom Verge but sadly this sequel isn't proving to be as enjoyable in too many fundamental ways. Where the first game had slick combat, this is clunky. Where it had a nicely judged path of progression, this is tedious.  The original, and this sequel, are Metroid-alikes, with a mixture of action and exploration that is gated off by obstacles that require finding new abilities. Axion Verge wasn't the most inspired take on this well-trod path, but it had some neat touches, enjoyable game-play and fairly unique presentation.  Immediately noticeable about this sequel is that it looks drab and bland, whereas the first was quite distinctive in its style. If that was the worst thing about it then this would be a positive review, but alas, the music isn't as good and the game-play is more than a bit whiffy.  Melee attacks are the emphasis here and it is nowhere near as satisfying as the ranged weapons you had before. Combat doesn't feel good or like it has much impact to your strikes, and there's a mild stealth element that I would applaud them for if it actually worked particularly well.   It doesn't though and enemies sometimes become aware of you too quickly. Doing a ton of ranged damage as they run away, while you flail about with a naff short-range attack or your limited and ineffective ranged weapon (of which you have one to use)  Exploration is the saving grace, with a nice sense of progression and platforming challenges. Right up until the point where it starts to have some annoyingly long-winded passages of traversal and dead-ends that take ages to backtrack through.  Having traipsed from one end of the map to the other with no joy, it's easy to forget that there are parts of the environment that are currently fruitless diversions. In some ways that's the nature of the genre, but at the same time  it can be quite dull having to go the long way around just to get stuck in another example and then trudge back from that too.  Ultimately I'm stuck and not enjoying it all that much to want to scour the entire map for the umpteenth time looking for what might be a hidden but necessary path to progress. The combat is naff, it's not too great to look at and the music isn't as engaging as it was before (though it is still the best thing about this release)  It isn't an awful game, but it's a poor sequel to a pretty good title and I'm just not feeling much love for this one. If you have more patience, or are happy to make excuses for iffy design by looking up clues in a guide to avoid the tedium, then you might like this more than I did. Otherwise it's mediocre as an action title and can be irritating when it comes to the exploration side of it.

Over all, a great iteration of Axiom Verge. While I too played it for about an hour and didn't feel it, I eventually returned and had a great time with it. I think it’s a bit unfair to just want more Axiom Verge, and what it does do is pretty cool and interesting. The background storytelling and visuals, they feel pretty frash mixing high-sifi HRGigerisms and Mesopotamian stuff, wish a nice contrast between hightech and pleasant foliage and rock. I think a big part of search action games are the vibs, veg as it is. And if you are open, you can enjoy a lot of vibs including this one.   I won’t not talk about what I didn’t like though, which is mostly the encounter design, I remember this being a bit of a problem in the first game, but with the more melee focused combat, I think it is a bit worse.  That all said, I still enjoyed looking around and figuring things out. I think they got the sauce, and I am looking forward to the 3rd one.

Sin haber jugado a la primera parte, me atrevo a decir que se trata de un gran metroidvania que construye un mundo bastante extraño pero en el que merece la pena perderse y que referencia a clásicos del género. Muy recomendable para fans del género.

The first game was a reskinned Super Metroid and I really liked it. This is a completely different game. There are a few references so maybe it’s the same universe? I really like this game. It was a bit slow at first but I couldn’t stop playing it. I had 4 hours one day and 8 hours the next day. I never play games for 8 hours in one day.   About halfway through I got stuck and had a hard time finding a guide to say what the missing thing is. I didn’t have any idea where to go next. I beat the rest of the game without a guide then I went for 100% with a guide for what I missed.   I beat the game in about 8-9 hours and about 4 hours to get 100%. Getting 100% was a pain. A lot of games might have a small marker where you got something. All this game has is if the area is cleared or not. I finished with 79% and had to check every item in each area till it was cleared. Even with a guide I had trouble finding some things. A bunch of times I went “How the crap would anyone ever find this or know you need to do that?”   The drone has too little health at the start of the game. I was going down so fast while using it. Once the drone has some good health it’s really good. Just a bit of a problem early on.   Great game, really fun just don’t go for all items like I did. Some of the achievements look pretty stupid so I won’t be doing them.

Contrary to what seems to be a popular opinion, I much preferred Axiom Verge 2 to the original after playing them back-to-back.  First, this game has everything I look for in a metroidvania: interesting exploration, lots of secrets, and a progression full of 'aha' moments.  It also has plenty of clever ideas, such as the dual-world system, which definitely reaches its full potential by the end of the game, or the hacking system, albeit limited, which I wish we could see more often. The list goes on.  Compared to the first game now, the environments and soundtrack are much diverse and pleasant. The controls, while they might take a little time to get used to, end up being more fun. No more double tapping, or anything clunky of the sort! Finally, some people criticize the limited range of weapons compared to AV1. Personally, I found the variety of weapons in AV1 to be artificial, and only ever used 3 or 4 of them at most while playing on hard mode (necessity wasn't a factor here then). As for the melee combat in AV2, it was neither good nor bad. Not that it's the main appeal of the game anyway.  In short, this game is definitely recommended to any fan of the genre!

I really enjoyed Axiom Verge 1, so I had to scoop up the sequel. It sat in my backlog for sometime, but I'm glad I got around to it.  I didn't really care for the story at all, I was more in it for a fun platforming metroidvania, and they really nailed the exploration mechanics - the switching between the four-legged mini spider-like robot and the human[oid] was unique and fun.   When it comes to metroidvanias like this, I often give up on them if the exploration becomes dragged out over time or if they lack a lot of direction, but I think they did a good job with compromising the two - they don't hold your hand as much as I personally like, but it really didn't go too long without finding a path or item I needed to progress. I did have to look up a walkthrough near the end, but I was able to enjoy most of the game without it.  I think both AV 1&2 both warrant your time if you're looking for a good metroidvania at a good {on-sale] price - but I just think there are other games of the genre I would rather play instead and I find to be just better overall.  Score - 7.4 / 10

A nice and well-received sequel, this game has more difficult "regular" enemies than its predecessor in the form of mini-bosses and some nice extensions to the overarching story and lore introduced in the original game. Similarly to the original, it was a very enjoyable metroidvania platformer game for its gameplay mechanics governing battle, exploration, and solving puzzles regarding how to defeat enemies or reach secret locations. I appreciated the story just as much as the story of the original, which I mean for the reader to understand as very high praise in light of how often different parts of a sequel (especially the story) will usually end up feeling like a rather simple re-imagining of the original with very little expansion or variation. It did not feel derivative, and felt like its story and gameplay shined on their own and came together well to make a very enjoyable game. I would even recommend players to play both games, especially if they are fans of the metroidvania platformer genre!

So it took me about 2 playthroughs to finally enjoy this game. Basically, I got stuck early on my first playthrough and I gave up, came back literal years later for my second, and now I just beat it a few hours ago writing this.  Look. I get why people are a bit disappointed, but I've seen a *lot* of hyperbole thrown around - "This game is ♥♥♥♥", "Worst game ever", etc, I know "Worst game ever" is thrown around a lot but, still. Am I a bit disappointed? Yes, honestly, a little. This game's actual *combat* isn't as good as the first, and I chalk that up mostly to the boss fights. There's one ACTUAL "Bossfight" right at the end, and the rest are entirely optional minibosses that can usually be avoided quite easily, and most of them aren't hard.  The early game is a bit of a slog, and that's largely because you're weak and, honestly, your weapons kinda suck. IT gets better as you get further, until you eventually end up with a fair few upgrades, statistically and weapon-wise, under your belt. In a way, I think that makes me appreciate the stronger weapons you find a lot more.  However, what this game lacks in its combat, I think it makes up with its exploration, atmosphere, and general mood. A lot of the art is fantastic, so much better than it was in the first game. The music is also, absolutely fantastic. It possesses such an incredibly unique mood and it *fits* how this game feels perfectly. The story is, also, I reckon, pretty good. I'm not sure I'm as pulled by the protagonist's story as much as I was in the first game, but there's a lot of elements to Indra's story, and the story of the world and culture you're constantly walking through, that are interesting. In parts, the story of the world is really interesting and cool to discover, and some of it explains parts of 1's story more.  This games strengths are in its exploration. It has a lot more fun with the drone and how you actually navigate, there's a lot more done with Breaches, etc. It feels pretty good to pull a portal to another dimension to you, discover an entire new section of a Breach-World, and exit said breach to end up in an entirely closed off part of the main world you're in, and finding a nice little power up. This game absolutely does that and general backtracking (thank god for the teleport) better than Axiom Verge 1. The hacking in this game is... unfortunately not as interesting as glitching reality and enemies in 1, but still. It's occasionally interesting, sometimes does fun things, and usually lets you sabotage enemies or make certain parts of the game easier if you need it - and that's fine.  I don't think it's bad, no I think it's pretty good actually. Whether or not I like 1 more is a hard question, I absolutely have fond memories of 1, but I liked the *combat* in that a lot more and I feel like people are often driven to think of their fondess with a game purely in the "Did I have fun shooting bad mens" category. This game at least has a power-curve that makes me appreciate how powerful I get towards the end game, but Trace starts off with a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gun that disrupts an entity's being until it ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ explodes because the fabric of reality around it is so unstable. Indra has to beat robots to death with an icepick.  Also I think the writing is alright. I like its story and characters. But, really, if I had to pick the BEST thing this game has? The art direction, the music, and the feel. Those are stellar. The music, good lord I enjoy it. And the vocal performances from Mayssa Karaa are wonderful.

Truly baffled by this game. It looks and feels completely unrelated to the first game, not even the name makes sense as the story never mentions it. I actually didn't have any fun.  Remember the cool gun you get on the first game with all these wacky fire modes? Gone. All you have is an axe and a boomerang. Melee sucks. Ranged sucks. Melee is very short range and lots of enemies seem to stay away from you. The boomerang is very slow and requires you to wait it to return before you can throw again. There are almost no upgrades, and the few that exist feel the same.  That said, there isn't a lot of enemy variation. Most seem to be blobs that walk slowly or some drone that hovers above you. Bosses are few and look more like big enemies. Even the final boss is pathetic and all you do is press a button 4 times. A huge downgrade from the boss of the first game.  Story seems unrelated to first game, maybe I missed something, because nothing seems to be related to the characters of the first, including the cliffhanger. One small note is related to the story of the first game, marking this as a sequel, but actions make it seem like this is a prequel. Music is better than the first, but gets REALLY annoying as there are giberish lyrics and they drive you crazy when you are looking for the right way to go.  Most of the game is either climbing or using the drone. There is too much climbing, almost every room requires a climbing skill, and there is no double jump to reduce that. Near the end of the game you get a flight power, but it uses so much energy that you might be able to use it for 5 seconds before you run out. You are also required to use the drone power, one of the few things returning from the first game, to walk around. A lot of rooms require the drone, as it has a different set of climbing tools.  One annoying mechanic is having to cross between two different maps. While you can move from map B to A with a power, going from A to B requires finding a portal. Lots of rooms and powerups require you to move between these maps to find routes and locks. And map B forces you to use the drone, so its more forced drone gameplay.  The game is very confusing on where to go. While it gives you a marker, you most often than not have to take a detour to get a skill, or bypass a locked door. I got stuck on a part with no way to progress because the game decides to change the rules without saying so. In the first game, there is some glitched stuff that you can't destroy until you find a special upgrade. Here you also find some purple stuff that can't be destroyed, but only in the real world, as you can destroy it on the virtual world. That is never elaborated, and if you tried to destroy the purple stuff and didn't manage to, you would have no reason to try again on the other dimension.  The entire story is also rather short, maybe 4 to 6 hours long if you know where to go. Almost no upgrades aside skill points, which only give you more health or damage. The only useful upgrade is higher level hacking and longer hack distance, as some doors require it. 100% completion is a chore, as the maps lack the Metroid mechanic that tells you an item is on that tile. All you get is a small checkmark saying if you got all items in an entire zone. Achievements also have a lot of padding. Destroy 1000 plants? Why?? You don't even find plants outside two or so zones, and there are maybe 10 or so scattered.  Overall, a game that removes everything the first one had and replaces with worse mechanics, non-existent story, lame power ups, boring fights and lots of climbing. Just skip it.

Mild recommendation to avoid. I played through Axiom Verge 1 fully on the Switch, and gave this one 90 minutes.  It's a very classic-feeling Metroidvania. I slowly picked up upgrades, new abilities, the drone, found a mini-boss, and, well ... none of it is very exciting. It's very exploration focused, which should be a plus, but combat, ugh. It feels terrible and clunky. I had to ask myself, do I really want to be swinging this ice-pick and throwing this awkward boomerang another 10 hours while I have dozens of games in my steam queue? And the answer is, no.  If it was the only game I owned, I would play it. It's not bad, but I don't care to see more.

Эта игра крайне противоречива. И хоть я склоняюсь ближе к положительной оценке, тут есть огромное количество проблем. Проходил на Steam Deck Offline из-за чего поломался счётчик времени в игре. Данный проект ощущается крайней экспериментальным. Большой акцент на ближний бой, отсутствие боссов как таковых (кроме финального), увеличение важности дрона вплоть до половины геймплея, система взлома и некоторые незначительные мелочи. На самом деле то, что полноценных боссов заменили на просто сильных врагов, выступающих не преградой, а банкой с прокачкой я понял только во второй половине, поскольку мир изучать интересно. Лучше первой части. Система взлома наконец является полезной механикой, а не странной способностью для открытия дверей. Ну, так ощущалось в начале. Но после середины игры взлом очень просаживается ввиду не таких уж полезных функций. Противники стали намного умнее, а их роль в комнате ощущается продуманнее. Очень хорошей находкой является система Разлома. По сути альтернативной картой, схожей с основной. Упразднили коды и теперь секретные двери открываются движением персонажа. Казуально, хотя коды в первой игре местами были совсем умудрёнными.

Axiom Verge 2 stands up strong alongside its predecessor, and I don't think people gave it a fair chance. While it's a stark departure from the first game, I found the exploration and combat a lot more fun and engaging throughout. Unlike the first game, this isn't trying to be a modern take on Metroid 1, and it's much stronger for it.  The writing is a bit stiff, just like in the original, but I was more emotionally invested in the player character than Trace from the first game. The worldbuilding is also really strong and I loved learning more about the wider multiverse beyond what we saw in Axiom Verge 1.  I do think the first game had a stronger visual identity, but this game is still quite pretty to look at, with really well done art and animations.  As for the ending, I think it was fine. [spoiler]This game is a prequel to Axiom Verge 1, not a sequel, so the lingering plot threads it hints at in the ending are all stuff we see the results of in Axiom Verge 1, so I didn't have a problem with there being some ambiguity as to what happens next.[/spoiler]  Definitely recommend, just don't expect it to be another Metroid clone.

I have mixed views on this game compared to the first but would overall recommend it.  Still great but just in a different way.  Unlike the first game, there is more of an emphasis on stealth and exploration as opposed to gunning everything down like you do in the first game.  In fact, most of your weapons are melee and your only ranged weapons are boomerangs that to horrible damage (except the final one), so unless you need to, it is best to just avoid enemies if you can.   This is easy to do since there are indicators telling you their FOV and if you have been spotted.   So sort of like Metal Gear Solid in that regard.  You can also hack enemies which is pretty cool to give yourself an advantage if you do need to fight.    Pros:  - Worlds are massive.  Took me about 16 hours to beat the game and almost 20 to get everything.    - Tons of power-ups to collect in a borderline open-world game.  Some are tough to find, but fair.  There was only one that I needed to look up how to find via a walkthrough,   - Pixel art and music are amazing.    - There is a new sill tree system that is used which i actually kind of like.     Cons:  - Story was kind of meh.  Also you play as a cranky old lady.  lol.   - Since there is more emphasis on exploration rather than combat, I find myself using the drone a lot more than the protagonist because it is smaller and more mobile.    - Not a whole lot of replay value once you do get everything.   There is no hard mode but there is a damage modifier in the options menu.  Also no randomizer option which would have been an amazing addition.

I loved Axiom Verge (AV1), as it really felt like a 2D Metroid game - a true spiritual successor.  Unfortunately Axiom Verge 2 (AV2) falls short of AV1 in my opinion and I'm not sure if call it a Metroidvania. I wish I could give it a middling review.   If you're looking for a Metroidvania then I don't recommend this one.   If you're looking for a pseudo-openworld, pseudo-metroid experience you might like this one.   I have chosen to rate this positively because I don't think it bad enough for a negative review; the mechanics that are here work well and it has some interesting lore which might be all people want to know to decide if they want to buy it. Whether or not it's like 2D Metroid may be of no concern.   My review focuses on explaining why I don't think this is a Metroidvania and is mostly a comparison to the 2D Metroid games, because that's what I hoped this game would be like.  AV1 felt very tight as it's environment was mainly inside a facility. AV2s map is very open as it's mostly outside but this leads to the mechanics feeling a bit floaty and the world feeling unstructured. In AV1 it wasn't always obvious where to go but after backtracking a while you'd come across an area you couldnt access before but now have the item to do so. As AV2s map is so open I kept getting lost and couldn't remember which areas had inaccessible doors to return too.   AV1s pickups worked like a Metroid game where they upgraded either the weapon or health. AV2 has health pickups but now it also has containers that give you ability points to upgrade via a skill tree. I didn't like this for a Metroidvania because having limited pick ups adds to the streamlined feeling and every upgrade has weight.   The ability tree needed me to save up for certain abilities so I didnt feel I was getting stronger with every pick up making them fell less impactful and more like just collectibles. Also there were a lot of options which again makes this game feel less like a tight Metroidvania and more like an open world, I was never sure which upgrades would be the most beneficial to me right now. The tree felt like it was included because every other game has one rather than because it's actually necessary for this game.   Towards the end of AV1 there's an item which greatly increases speed and you can zoom throughout the map. Not so for AV2, which makes the character feel slow. I find this an odd decision considering it's a more open map and could really benefit from a speedy item. I collected all the items in the first game but I couldn't be bothered here as my character feels so lethargic.   AV2 appears to only have one mandatory boss with all the others being optional. Again this adds to feeling that this game is very loose and unsctructed. None of the bosses I fought were a challenge - I defeated most of them on my first try by just spamming attacks.   According to the achievements I have missed one boss but I've got no motivation to face it. Also you can't die to the final mandatory boss, or you can, but you respawn in the room and continue the battle; the fight does not reset which is not challenging.    Overall, I didn't enjoy AV2 anywhere near as much as AV1 and that's because AV2 is, in my opinion, not really a Metroidvania which is what I wanted from it.

Поиграл немного в этот продукт. Не знаю, смешанные чувства, может я наигрался в первый, который мне очень понравился. Тут же швах полный по сеттингу, скучнейшие роботы, боссы которых зачем-то можно скипать? Зачем они нужны? Мне не понятно.  Игра из метроидвании стала скучной бродилкой с ужасным левелдизайном, когда непонятно куда надо идти и без быстрых переещений между точками возрождений, это основной минус игры. Очень нехватает возможности телепортироваться между "чашами", чтобы преодолеть огромные скучные локации.  Первая часть позволяла тебе обходить не особо шустрых врагов и была отличная хаб локация в центре, которая позволяля перемещаться в любую локацию по желанию довольно быстро.  Тут отняли возможность сражаться в дальним боем, (бумеранг не в счет он ужасен), борьба исключительно киркой, сражаться против огнестрельных противников ужасно неудобно. Ладно бы можно было приноровиться, но из-за того что приходится постоянно бегать по огромным картам туда сюда, с ними еще меньше желания сражаться и убивать время на уничтожение, теряния хп, смерти.  В общем игра крайне унылая и душная, будто специально растягивает свой геймплей.   Добавили элемент РПГ, зачемто, я не против, но чувствую долго буду прокачиваться, пока на самом деле почувствую себя какимто сильным и мне не будет душно убивать противников.   Зачемто добавили стелс механику... Вот это я вообще не понял, мне предлагает игра либо долгие сражения, либо долгий стелс по обходу противников? Причем его почемуто нельзя прокачивать, чтобы тебя враги не так быстро замечали, хотя если они заметят они все равно к тебе идут, в общем я не понял этого прикола, это какойто совершенно лишний костыль.   Самое неприятное это смена сеттинга, когда из непонятно Гигеровской вселенной попадаешь на заснеженный пляж с роботами или под воду к роботам, одни придурошные унылые роботы. Скучно, очень скучно.  Основные враги воры Казахи. Союзники сверхтехнологичные Индусы, смешно наверное, ну да ладно. Когда в прошлой игре ты вообще не понимал кому можно верить, а кому нет. Вообще сюжет прошлой игры был на голову выше, он тебя заставлял разобраться что там происходит и главный герой вызывал симпатию.  А тут изначально ты играешь за абсолютно негативного персонажа, который является мультимиллиардером и эксплуатирует работяг, увольняет их недоплачивает, и в одиночку! без охраны, без наймитов идет куда-то за своей дочкой, самый унылый сюжет который можно было придумать. Я уж не говорю о том что это пожилая женщина, допустим пожилая Лара Крофт, тут можно сделать поблажку. В такого персонажа просто невозможно поверить, даже глупо для сказки.   В общем я все плачу о том какая шикарная была первая часть... Может когда нибудь вернусь, попробую допройти и поменяю свое мнение, но сейчас желания играть точно нет.

It's unfortunate that people seemed to have disliked this game compared to the first Axiom Verge. I think a lot of things about this game show clear improvement in the gameplay design and great maturity in the dev's aesthetic/style, in ways where the first game was mostly in the shadow of its Metroid inspirations.  The exploration in particular was implemented excellently, in not only the quality of life features implemented around it (better mapping, save point fast travel being present, having map markers & waypoints available that allow for rough guidance without spoiling the "answer"), but also in terms of the way the world and rooms were structured, that allow for the player to be able to uncover rooms of the map in a funneling manner, rather than large stretches of retracing steps & manual backtracking for padding.   In a place where the 1st game failed in this regard, I think Axiom Verge 2 does much better in minimizing moments where the next place of progress is arbitrary located with respect to where the player found the ability that unlocks that progress. As an example, the progress block that is gated by the drone hover is unblocked by funneling the neighboring area, which is consistent with how the map design of this game drives the player to explore anyway, whereas in the first game such an ability might have been located on the other side of the map, and with no proper fast travel.   This being said, there were still some moments in Axiom Verge 2 where it was unclear what map locations the player was supposed to have taken note of as something that can be unblocked by a progression ability later, such as with the destructible blocks and with hidden breach portals. I don't think a player can reasonably have foresight to preemptively mark each one they come across to come back to later, as it isn't signposted in the best way for the player to draw the connection. To fix this in a future game, I think the dev can take a page out of Metroid Dread's book, where certain points of interest or potential progress walls are marked ??? on the map, until the player has an ability that can meaningfully interact with them. This can still be done in a way that doesn't spoil the natural exploration the player is to do, while still being natural to intuit. Regardless though, this is a testament to the thoughtfulness and creativity of the progression abilities themselves in this game, since they aren't your typical "double jump" or "ground pound" type of deal.  Other noteworthy things about this game: the music is absolutely fantastic, even over the first game's already great music. I can't think of a single track that I did not like, nor one that did not pair well with the theming of its respective zone or the game overall. The spritework, backgrounds, and presentation of the story I think are all stellar improvements from the first game as well. The story itself still leans pretty heavily into crazy scifi territory, but definitely easier to follow than the 1st game. Perhaps one thing I would mention is that it was sometimes hard to distinguish platforms you could land on with platforms that were merely decorative. I did not realize until pretty late into my playthrough that there is a "platform contrast" accessibility option, which I am thankful is in the game.  There were some abilities and combat options that left a little to be desired, especially with the RC boomerang and combat boomerang, which weren't too useful or interesting. It would have been cool to see the melee weapon get further add-ons in a similar vein to the multitude of weapon options the 1st game had, but given the primitive (and annoying) nature of the enemies, and the lack of bosses, it felt like it wasn't as much of a focus as the exploration.  One of the coolest things about this game is the systems around entering/exiting the breach, and manipulating the portals' locations to do so. It felt very Zelda-esque in the way it was handled, and also reminded me of Castlevania Harmony of Dissonance a little bit. It is definitely something the developer can expand upon further, as I felt the ideas that were present in Axiom Verge 2 were suppressed slightly. Maybe this was a good call though, since once its potential registered in my brain, I was afraid it would either overshadow other aspects of the game, or be utilized in a manner too dull to be worth its inclusion. In hindsight, for what was required to beat the game, it was just the right balance.  I would overall recommend this game. It's a big step up from the first game, and is one of the better metroidvanias in an indie space saturated with endless Hollow Knight-likes and others.
我怎么觉得二代还不如一代呢，比起一代操作手感游玩体验的确提升了很多，但是引导比一代还离谱，拿到新能力你最起码在主线需要使用的地方提醒一下啊，游玩时间里面起码又一半时间在迷路真是无语了

I'll start by saying even though I don't recommend this game, it's not necessarily a bad game. Once I got past a strong standing "Where do I go, what am I supposed to be doing?" stage or two, I did enjoy the time I had playing this. My biggest problem is that its admittedly inferior to its predecessor in varied degrees and ways.  Doesn't feel like there's as much to collect or explore. Combat is so, very much, weaker. Story feels rushed. It's not bad, but feels kinda distracted and jumpy. It feels like a well written essay, with lots of thought put into it, then hastily shortened because of a forgotten word limit. I can kinda say that for the rest of the game, too. Lastly, final boss is lackluster and ending feels abrupt. I was surprised to see the ending cutscene and credits only after the 5 or so hours I spent on my playthrough, and apparently there's more content in the ending if you beat it in less than 4!  I cannot suggest to anyone to play Axiom Verge 2.  I can, however, excitedly recommend Axiom Verge 1 to anyone. Go play that instead. I'm likely to boot up a new run soon after posting this.

Хорошая игра, не хуже первой части. Исследовать мир стало интереснее, первая часть тоже награждала за исследование, узлами силы или здоровья, но чаще всего выдавала тебе фрагменты усилений, которых нужно целых 5 штук, чтобы увеличить характеристику, что отбивало желание куда то лезть. Если точка твоего интереса находилась далеко, чаще всего нужно было потратить минут 5 чтобы дойти до неё и увидеть, что ты получил 1 фрагмент и тебе нужно ещё всего лишь 4, до которых также нужно идти, а какой кайф было понять, что у тебя ещё нет нужной способности, чтобы пройти дальше и ты зря сюда шёл, а ведь потом ещё нужно возвращаться в локацию, которую нужно исследовать чтобы продвинуть сюжет, а быстрого перемещения нет, именно из за этого я при прохождении первой части довольно быстро потерял интерес к изучению мира. Также стоит отметить одно довольно странное решение первой части, в которой разраб почему то по приколу решил некоторые секреты спрятать в случайной точке локации, за случайным блоком, разбить который можно либо опять же случайно, либо проверяя все стены на своём пути на предмет секретов. Вторая часть в этом плане намного лучше. Она награждает игрока выдавая новое оружие или флаконы которые заменили узлы и которые тратятся на увеличение хп героини или её бота, их урона, скорости атаки и тд. Мне например хотелось прокачать способности по взлому врагов, из за чего я часто шёл исслледовать мир в поисках флаконов. Во многом на это также повлияло то, что во второй части появилось быстрое перемещение из любой точки карты к алтарям и я в любой момент могу телепортироваться хоть на другой конец карты, если там есть открытый алтарь и пойти исследовать его, или если там нужна способность которой у меня ещё нет, я просто телепортируюсь назад.  Во второй части довольно большой акцент сделан на бота, который был и в первой части, но там он использовался по большей части, чтобы добраться до мест, в которые герой не мог попасть сам. Во второй части это тоже есть, но также можно полноценно проходить за него игру, он гораздо мобильнее главной героини благодаря крюку, из за маленького размера ему проще уклоняться от атак, а также он обладает неплохим уроном в ближнем бою, я даже сумел победить за него несколько боссов, которые всё ещё как и в первой части проходятся за одну две попытки, но в этой части стали поинтереснее, так как их можно взломать и например отключить им какое то оружие. Из минусов бота у него нет дальних атак, а также меньше здоровья, чем у главной героини, но несмотря на это, за него приятно играть, и я большую часть игры прошёл за него, поскольку героиня по сюжету получает способность в любой момент превращаться в бота и обратно.  Графика стала лучше, локации выглядят живее, музыка тоже неплоха, но всё же это не уровень первой части. В первой части музыка и графика создавали поистине непередаваемые чувства. Первая часть происходит на обломках некогда великой цивилизации, кроме гг есть всего два персонажа, которые изредка будут общаться с главным героем, говоря что делать дальше, большую же часть времени главный герой один и БОЖЕ как же прекрасно саундтрек передаёт это чувство одинокого путешествия по некогда живому миру. Каким то магическим образом минималистичная графика в сочетании с музыкой умудрились создать поистине запоминающиеся локации, у меня до сих пор мурашки по всему телу, когда я слушаю саундтрек первой части. Одним из самых запоминающихся треков для меня стал Cellular Skies. Большая часть игры происходит под землёй или в помещениях, они порой довольно большие и высокие, но всё же это помещения, ты видишь потолок над собой, ну или не видишь, но понимаешь что он есть. Именно поэтому мне так запомнился Cellular Skies, который играет в надземных локациях мира. Ты видишь небо на заднем плане, слышишь этот трек, и чувствуешь свободу, обожаю его. Остальная часть саундтрека ни чем не хуже, но я хотел рассказать именно про него.  Вторая часть тоже происходит на обломках некогда великой цивилизации, но другой, не той что в первой части. И как я писал выше, саундтрек неплохой, он тоже создаёт атмосферу путешествия и исследования остатков некогда великого мира, но другую. Во второй части есть несколько других персонажей с которыми можно общаться, они живут в поселениях людей, которые как и гг попали в этот мир, также есть персонажи которые общаются с гг во время прохождения из за чего пропадает атмосфера одиночества из первой части. Локации по большей части светлые и яркие, многие из них под открытым небом, что создаёт атмосферу приключенческого фильма про попадание на другую планету, и это не плохо, но мне всёже больше нравится мрачная атмосфера одиночества из первой части. Вторая часть лучше первой в плане исследования мира, у неё есть своя, неплохая атмосфера лёгкого приключенческого фильма и я спокойно могу рекомендовать её к прохождению, как и первую часть.

Honestly, I had a lot of fun, but it just wasn't for me I feel. It gets a bit experimental sometimes and even if I love it, some parts overstay it's welcome or don't push the ideas further enough to be interesting.  What hurts it the most to me, is how linear the game is. Aside from a few short moments, it can be a really A to B moment for the mayority of the playthough unless you want to go out of your way to maybe search for more lore, upgrades or weapons (which I didn't really feel the need to do).  This also I feel ends up affecting a lot of the really cool ideas the game haves and encapsulated them so that it can be as linear as possible, not having room to grow and experiment. Again this is how I feel about it after just finishing it and I might change a bit my opinion once I let it rest.  I'm glad Thomas took the chance to experiment and do something different for the sequel. Even if I feel it wasn't for me as much as the first game, I had a lot of fun and I'm really glad I got to finally play it! Can't wait to see what else they will make in the future.

Surprisingly excellent metroidvania with how people talked about it. I thought the general consensus from fans of the first game was disappointment, and I can see why, it has a very different focus. That game is very combat-heavy, with traditional bosses, an interesting variety of enemies, and a comical amount of weapons. This game has no bosses that I would call very 'fun' and combat situations are satisfying enough but usually go the same way every time. What makes this game a better experience than the first (in my opinion, though I would like to revisit it as it's been a while) is the exploration. The various abilities you get are extremely creative and lend themselves well to the huge, interconnected world that's been created. The lore and narrative are just as convoluted as the first game: if you like sifting through lots of notes to build a picture of an obviously well-thought out world, I've heard these games are pretty special at that, but I might save that rabbit hole for a later date.

Great game with a very interesting setting, it was quite satisfying to play. It is important to note, however, that it is NOT more of the first game, as far as gameplay goes; no run-and-gun stuff, one must either evade enemies or plan one's attacks carefully(at least, early on) and remember that the hacking ability is your friend.

Too many dead ends on the map, there are 'breach' areas to explore with no way of knowing why your there in the first place.  So you'll find yourself going to the same dead ends looking for something you missed, and entering the same breach areas for the same reason.  Eventually you get fast travel, which really doesn't help since you generally will have to clue where to go anyway, but at least it reduces how much time you waste wandering around aimlessly.   I played for about 10 hours and just got too frustrated, and simply wasn't having fun anymore. And I'm not interested in playing a game where I'm CONSTANTLY looking at a guide trying to figure out how to proceed.  This game badly needs to mark areas, or at least the breaches, as 'complete' so you don't keep wasting time going back.  I have what I *think* is a quest marker on my map, which does absolutely no good since I can't even get close to it no matter which direction I try to approach from.  What a mess, and metroidvanias are my second favorite genre.  Axiom Verge 2 makes a great first impression, then over time, gets extremely annoying.  Others seem to enjoy it, but buy it at a discount if you decide to give it a go.
