Axiom Verge 1 was a more-than-competent 'Metroid-Clone' with the occasional weird but interesting idea here and there. A really good Metroidvania. More of the same with Axiom Verge 2 would have been totally fine and that's what a lot of people expected. But the developper tried something different. Some people were disappointed because if the formula was already good, why change it ?  The two big complaints seems to be the melee-focused combat system and the absence of real bosses encounters. Here's what I think about those:  You don't use a fun scifi gun obliterating everything in front of you in an instant in Axiom Verge 2, the game is melee focused and the occasional projectile isn't really satisfying to use. If you wanted to mod AV2 into a prehistoric metroidvania where you control a caveman wielding a club and throwing rocks, you wouldn't need to change a lot of things other than the skins. This sounds bad for a scifi game but it really isn't because the game fully embraces its anachronistic nature. A distant past is mixed with futuristic vibes in every aspect of the game. If you like that, I think the 'limited combat' becomes a logical part of it.  The bosses are just existing in the world and you can simply run past them. And if you fight them, they feel like normal ennemies but bigger and with more HP. No special music, no real phases. To re-use my caveman-game analogy, they feel like some megafauna chilling somewhere you might encounter and decide wether or not you want to kill them. This wasn't an issue for me as I just accepted it's part of the unusual world and atmosphere of the game.  A forced boss encounter to progress is a very usual 'video game thing' it isn't needed, we're just used to it. That said it feels weird as it goes against the genre's conventions. Zero big epic boss battles in a metroidvania might of course be a flaw for some.    This game doesn't look, sound or feel like any other metroidvania, it's weird, unfamiliar but it is made with the same talent as Axiom Verge 1. To me it's not worse, it's different and weird. If you're OK with that you'll have at least a good time exploring this world. But if that unfamiliarity speaks to you it might even become your favorite of the two. This was the case for me anyway.  It reminded me of Shadow of the Beast, on Amiga, one of those very hard sidescrollers from the late 80s, early 90s. I pushed through that game frustrating difficulty and bad controls because of its weirdness, I wanted to see the next ennemies, hear the next music, see how everything came together. Axiom Verge 2 isn't difficult, the controls are good and there aren't any glaring issue but I had a very similar feeling playing it and if it reminded me of such an old game it's because that particular feeling of wonder isn't something that a lot of game were able to create. So yeah, this was an enjoyable and memorable game.  So yes, it's an easy recommendation for me but it's different enough from the first game that your enjoyment of Axiom Verge 1 pretty much indicates nothing about what you might think of this game.

Terrible. The first game was amazing in every way but after playing this one, I have to say it is a huge let down. The first one was absolutely perfect, down to every minute detail: The map designs, OST, enemy designs, weapons, abilities, and hidden areas. This sequel (if you can call it that) feels like they either sold the rights to someone random who tried to capitalize on it, or their magic ran out. It is nothing like the first one, to the point where I couldn't stomach playing for more than 40 min. Don't waste your money on it. Somehow the sequel is absolute trash juice compared to the first one.

This game feels like it was made before AV1. I didn't dislike it but there were so many times where I felt like poor choices were made to differentiate the two games. I knew that 1 was an homage to Metroid but after seeing the more original 2 struggle with it's attempt to be more original with design choices, it made me retroactively consider 1 to be more derivative.  Story wise I very much enjoyed the lore which gives a lot of background to the war from AV1 that led to the creation of the Rusalka. The details are convoluted but it feels rewarding to piece everything together and if the third game ties everything up nicely then I won't mind that the gameplay in this game was mediocre. Although the ending felt rushed and unsatisfying, the final bits you get to connect the two games were pretty cool. There's just not enough there for it to be really good.  Sadly everything gameplay felt like a step down. The focus on melee weapons is uninteresting. Most of the combat is very easy and I never really cared what weapon I equipped. For how distinct all the guns were in 1 I thought this game would take it further - instead you choose from maybe 1/5 as many options all of which you can just spam at enemies mindlessly to proceed.   Combat is more of an annoyance than anything else. You can choose to use your mana to hack but it's usually unnecessary when you can just run by or use the explode move for instant kills.  Bosses are the biggest disappointment of all. Gone are the awesome moments of huge boss battles that stay with you long after. In fact I killed 3 bosses before I even knew they were bosses they were so insignificant to me, along with offering no challenge. Even the final boss is supremely unsatisfying but at least a few of them have some nice pixel art.  The pixel art in general is fantastic. The references to old Sumerian statues and architecture mixed with robots is truly unique and a highlight of the game. However it just doesn't quite match up to the HR Giger look of 1. You never get that awesome feeling of apprehension that came with discovering Rusalka, not knowing if they were dangerous or truly your allies. The one large creature in the game does basically nothing outside of lore.  The music is very beautiful, the singer has done something amazing here... But it doesn't really fit with a metroidvania. Loud middle eastern horns and wild singing with big crescendos are just not "exploration" music and I got tired of hearing these tracks on repeat except for a couple cool ones like Stranded and Monsoon that felt like they set the mood properly. I'm not a fan of bit tunes type music but the breach music at least felt more appropriate in most cases. I'm still a big fan of amazing tracks from the first one like Vital Tide and Otherworld and I hope the next game goes back in that direction.   I will recommend this game solely for the story which I think it's even cooler if you read Happ's manifesto on simulation theory, but everything else left me a bit disappointed knowing it could be better. I really hope Happ takes these criticisms and improves on them for the next game.

Has some stuff in common with Axiom Verge - There's lore referencing Trace, the Breach is here. The soundtrack is *great*. This is a new setting, so far I've seen nothing of the characters and world from the first game. There are thematic similarities, it seems the player character has become embroiled in an pre-existing conflict in this world.  There's only one problem, unfortunately it's a really really big problem. You're familiar with the phrase about bringing a knife to a gun fight? The player character is a knife fighter, and many enemies not only have powerful long-range attacks, they also actively kite to keep you past arm's reach.  I'm six hours in, and debating whether I want to continue playing this game or not. Getting killed by enemies out of your reach is getting really, really old, really, **really** fast.

I enjoyed the first one despite some grievances, like the combat and the excessively reticent storytelling. Many of those grievances persist here but now accompanied by themes\atmosphere I find far less appealing. I don't think I'll ever boot it up again, leave alone finish it.
最终主角到底算是谁呢？

Contrary to some of the reviews here and elsewhere, I enjoyed this one way more than the first.  I'm starting to think that the guy makes these games just as a vehicle to sell his music, it's really good.  I couldn't tell what the story is about though, I don't think I got most (or any) of it, but you're in another dimension (I think?), there are some aliens, also some robots, and you slowly make your way through the map and find upgrades and power-ups, there's also something called "the breach" which is like a parallel dimension or something like in Soul Reaver, and you fight some bosses that look really good but are surprisingly easy and that's pretty much it.  The map is quite big, and there's 2 versions of it: the regular one and "the breach" (again, I think). Anyways, I enjoyed exploring it and finding everything I could find.  The bosses are ridiculously easy, so don't play this if you're looking for a challenge. Also, thanks to the developer for the fast travel power-up, it allowed me to go back and forth around the map super fast to find new things all the time, way less tedious than in the first game.
it's fun

And keeping with the thematic tone of the first game, you're whisked away to another planet/world/dimension, and as you naturally progress through the platform jumping Metroidvania gameplay mechanics, the lore unravels into a delectable plate of sci-fi spaghetti that's equal parts confusion and intrigue. And once it's done, it's done.

The first Axiom Verge was a hidden gem, a modern Metroid (NES) with a great soundtrack and sound design. This one feels hollow, polished but missing whatever spark the first game had. Maybe it's the change of setting?

Gonna have to be real, while there are marked improvements on combat, the direction the game takes is not a great one.  I played the original which while it had problems was a breath of fresh air and unique. Great story. the story here feels like it ends in the middle and not a final grand conclusion leaving me unsatisfied. The naming was quirky in the first game but made the game hard to follow. This time i was way ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ confused on who people were or why i should care. characters don't seem to do things in a way that makes sense. Why didn't Hammond just ask for help directly. The main characters daughter is involved which is what brought her out here in first place. she would have helped how she could. why have the subterfuge.   when it comes to the world I greatly dislike the fact that the boss battles for the most part feel entirely optional.  I'm not a fan of most open worlds and metroidvanias don't really have open worlds when it comes to fighting bosses especially. Do they feel totally linear? No. but boss battles shouldn't feel optional.  The whole game felt like they worked forever on the mechanics and not a lot on the world.  I really wanted to like this one but i cannot recommend it due to seemingly misplaced.  Play the first one its great.

I played the first Axiom Verge. I thought it was mediocre at best. It was like Metroid was skinned, and that skin was filled with subversion. "Ah ha. You [i] think [/i] you'll get a morph ball, but instead you'll get [spoiler] spoiler [/spoiler]. So clever!" It was slightly cute, at first, but then just felt overplayed. It had a plethora of useless weapons. Wimpy bosses. Horrendously slow map travel... I did like the big face visuals, though (probably 50% why I bought the game in the first place).  Anyway, I'm not reviewing the first game. I'm reviewing the sequel -- the sequel that got hammered for no particularly good reason. The art? Better. It felt less derivative. The sound and music? Pretty similar to the last game. The writing... a little better than the last game. The weapon selection? Still pretty bad. The enemies and bosses. Still easy. Yeah, not really looking up, so far. But the map travel... The map travel is some of the best that I've ever experienced. After a few item acquisitions, it really speeds up. I also really liked how the "breach" and the regular world were woven into each other (unlike the previous game, which felt like an actual glitch).   Now, is the map really enough to recommend the game? Over the first one, yes. In general? In general, I think this game got too much undeserved flak, and I'm trying to shift it in a more positive direction. I enjoyed my 15-ish hours that I sank into it, and I also preferred the ending to this one. A good ending makes the journey feel worth it.

Let me start this review by saying that I think Axiom Verge 1 is a great game and I would recommend it to anyone who's into Metroid.  Now that that's out of the way, let me say that this game is awful and I wouldnt recommend it to anyone. For a sequel this game somehow made so many steps back in enjoyable gameplay that I'm honestly baffled by how did this even happen.  I'll talk a lot about this game, but I want to mention the things that this game did worse than the 1st game, as I think those will be important if you've enjoyed that game and think about playing this one:  1. Physics - In this game for some god forsaken reason, the devs decided to make the air physics awful. Where they keep some kind of momentum, which leads to you sliding in air like you're on ice, instead of being able to take instant sharp turns like in the 1st game.  2. This game is SLOW - There are animations everywhere that slow things down compared to the 1st game. One of the first power-ups is literally: Stop time, deal damage around player. Another thing slowed down is picking power-ups to use. Where in the 1st game the best comparison would be the Weapon wheel, which was great. You could assign weapon to left stick, right stick and still have a 3rd weapon by default, while the wheel was an actual wheel so on a controller you could quickly choose what you want. In this game? Yeah, just a horizontal wheel. It would be fine if it was used, like rarely. But nah, you use the freaking wheel every time you want to do anything. Which just feels so painful as they already did a system that would've worked better than this. Hacking also got affected by this, where in the 1st game it was just a gun, which honestly was a unique idea that made enemies act differently, which was interesting. In this game the hacking is an expanding circle, which leads to a menu allowing you to choose which hack to use on an enemy. Interesting idea, but ♥♥♥♥ me, it is, again, SO SLOW. The first game thrived on being fast, and the only thing there that was slow was teleporting back to egg by leaving back to menu. Here? Everything is crawling slow.  3. Power-ups - You get a power-up every 5 minutes, honestly. Which sounds fine, but it just means that you dont care about anything in this game. Why would I try to remember anything on the map and play this like a Metroidvania, if I get power-ups so often, that I have no clue where could they even be useful? It just felt better to try and get all of them before bothering with the map, which I also did in the 1st game...ON MY 2ND PLAYTHROUGH, when I knew how powerful the last Lab Coat was. Also the power-ups in this game are so damn weak. Where by the end of the game you have 2 mobility skills, and only 1 is unlocked for the main form of the character...and that one is unlocked literally one step before the last bossfight.  4. Bossfights - They're so boring in this one. Literally 90% of them can be 1-shot by a power-up you get 30 minutes into the game. Meanwhile in the 1st game you had to actually think about "How do I deal with this one?". Not all the bosses in the 1st game were great, but there were couple that could stop your progression if you didnt prepare at least a little. Here? Only 1 boss that is even threatening, and that's only on a Deathless Run.  5. Health - You start the game with like x4 times health of the 1st game, sounds good? Sure, it could be. But that makes health upgreades feel really small, meanwhile all the enemies deal x3-4 times the damage of the 1st game, as they tried to balance them to kill you in the same amount of hits. This is so bad, that the game gives you multiple 15% damage reductions, which dont really do anything. Only the last one made me notice that I dont die in 3 hits.  6. Weapons - They are so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ boring. They show more stats, which is GREAT, I really wished that the 1st game did that. But they also are practically all the same. Just a melee stab, some faster, some longer, some can even swing overhead and completely miss the enemy in front of you. Compared to the 1st game, I just rushed a weapon in the first 30 minutes of the game, and never switched it, as that one was just the best. And unlike in the 1st game, you cannot get damage upgreades or any type of upgreades for these weapons, unless you really go out of your way to do so, but that leads to not being able to upgreade hacking, which will just lead to a highier amounts of locked doors in your way.  7. Less fun modes - The 1st game had multiple options for randomizer, and speedrun mode. This game Has speedrun mode...that's it. Why add less content? It would take a bunch of testing, but I honestly think that randomizer would work great in this game. I wouldnt play it, as I dont enjoy playing the game, but it would have potential to be fun for someone.  8. Background vs. Foreground - In many locations its practially impossible to tell the difference, as its literally Gray vs. Gray, but +1 shade lighter. There is an accessibility option for making platforms more visible, but if the devs noticed that it could be a problem, why not make stuff more understandable and visible in the first place?  9. Drone Hitbox - The drone hitbox is so clunky in this game. In the 1st game you could stand next to a wall and shoot up to make a drone easily get somewhere that is high on said wall. Meanwhile here, if you try and do that, the drone just instantly hits the wall and falls to the ground, as its hitbox is wider for some reason, so you have to stand back from the wall to do something that should be simple and not clunky.  Those were only things that were made worse than in the 1st game, but there are still things that I need to complain about, as they made the gameplay expierience miserable:  10. This game is Melee - I dont mind this in concept, I absolutely love playing many difficult Metroidvanias Melee only, without spells. Not in this game, tho.  As I stated before(1), the physics in air feel awful in this game, so you are waaay less precise in your movement, meanwhile the average enemy has gotten way stronger than in the 1st game? Almost all enemies are faster, are ranged, and want to run away from the player. There are even flying enemies that do that, AND YOU CANT HIT THEM! Brother, why tf do you make this game melee, and every enemy so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fast and annoying, while also(5) way more deadly. I've never skipped every enemy in my path in another game on my 1st playthrough. It feels so ungodly punishing to fight anything, and you dont feel any stronger by the end of the adventure.  11. Boomerang - There is actually a ranged weapon in the game, but it sucks absolute balls. You dont upgreade it until the end-game location, meanwhile the base boomerang already feels weak when you first pick it up. So you CAN deal with flying enemies, but it will take you 10 hits, when they are spamming you from all sides, at the speed of light, dodging anything that you want to hit them with.  There's more(like up to point 17, honestly), but I'm limited by letter count.  That was a lot of complaining, but let me say some positive/neutral things about this game that I either enjoyed, or didnt mind.  The music in this game slaps - Same thing as the 1st game, its just great.  Story is OK - While I didnt enjoy the notes, and found the story to be less interesting than the Rusalkas Vs. Athetos. I found the concept of this game, that is basically "A world where people can be turned into power-ups for other people", to be interesting, and pretty fun.  This game also is a better Metroidvania than the 1st game, with the player having way more freedom in upgreades they decide to get. Also the game is way more enjoyable to 100% the map, but that is a low bar, as the 1st game sucked at this.  Overall, there are some positives for the game, but its base gameplay is so strictly unenjoyable, that its impossible for me to be even close to recommending it.

I'd have been delighted with AV2 simply being more of the resplendent AV1 but lone developer Tom Happ decided to change things up a bit and, while not [i]quite[/i] as cohesive as the first game, this exotic and bold sequel quickly became my favourite of the two simply due to the incredible atmosphere it brings, while expanding upon the lore of the first game. It feels more...mature, more confident, more the product of an Auteur spreading his ultra-talented wings than merely riffing on Super Metroid's genius design.  One of the best changes made is making boss fights OPTIONAL, instead of funnelling you into the fights, This might not please some of the skill-challenge junkies out there but as someone who hates Boss Battles I ADORE this change. Thank you for remembering not all of us gamers want or need extremely repetitive skill-cliff choke-points killing our exploration progress!  Another genius addition for the series are the ethereal, otherworldy vocals of Maysaa Karaa. Seriously, this lady can SING.  Axiom Verge was one of the best Metroidvanias I've ever played. With the release of Axiom Verge 2 it is now one of the best metroidvania [i]franchises[/i] I've ever played. As in the best novel series each book should have it's own flavour and texture, and AV2 accomplishes this almost effortlessly. Don't even think, just Buy.

I loved the original Axiom Verge. But this game has very little in common with it. Sure, it's a Metroidvania and it has similar looking graphics, assets, and the sort of music you'd expect out of Axiom Verge... but this game kinda sucks by comparison.  So, the first game really took STRONG inspiration from the first Metroid game, introduced the main character, introduced an alternate dimension, gave you a gun, had great bosses, it had a huge variety of enemies as well,    But... this game introduces a whole new protagonist I don't think I'll grow to enjoy. I'm 9 hours into the game and I'm just using stupid melee weapons. You go to a different dimension, but it doesn't look like or feel like anything from the first game. The BOSSES look like really big bad guys that do next to nothing. They're easy to kill, and you get their loot. And the variety of enemies? Well... there's some... but not as big of a variety from what I can see.   I don't think that this is a natural evolution of the game. Because this feels like the replacement of the game I love... Like an appropriation.  It's like the sequel trillogy for Star Wars. Stupid characters and story, but it really sounded like and looked like Star Wars.

По началу, первые часов 5-6 при нормальном темпе прохождения, игра будет играться именно так как выглядит на фото в магазине: незнакомые герои, незнакомое место, всё совсем никак в первой части и зачем я вообще это прохожу. Нормального оружия нет, саундтрек проигрывает первой части, родного черно-неонового визуала нет, вместо монстров какие-то банки консервные скучные, охота дропнуть...  Но я вас умоляю,.. нет... ЗАКЛИНАЮ, перетерпите, передушните это интро, потому что дальше на вас вывалят ТАКОЕ количество лора, отсылок на первую часть и механик, способных сломать любой мир, что вам просто крышу снесёт. Ради механик второй половины игры, красивых и атмосферных локаций и главное лора в это стоит поиграть.  Конечно есть минусы, которые нельзя оправдать: 1) музыка (мне больше первая часть зашла) 2) отсутствие портретов героев в диалогах 3) отсутствие нормального оружия (да, это так и не вылечится после "интро") 4) фем.повесточка (протагонист сильная и независимая женщина, её главный конкурент-корпорат тоже женщина, главный злодей тоже женщина, предводитель Феноменонцев... естественно женщина, и Ламассу, который выглядит как бородатый старец, тоже иногда срывается на женский род в диалогах).

**Axiom Verge 2: Не сиквел, а смелое переосмысление**  **Рекомендую**  Если вы ждете прямого продолжения истории Трейса с его кибер-пистолетами и глайч-мира, приготовьтесь к сюрпризу. Axiom Verge 2 — это не сиквел в привычном понимании. Это скорее духовный наследник, параллельная история, которая смело отказывается от многих формул оригинала, предлагая взамен уникальный, амбициозный и глубоко атмосферный опыт, который заслуживает внимания всех фанатов метроидвании.  **Что изменилось? Новый взгляд на знакомый жанр**  С самого начала игра знакомит с новой героиней — Индирой Чоран, владелицей мегакорпорации, которая отправляется на поиски пропавшей экспедиции в антарктической пустыне. Ее путешествие быстро переносит нас в два взаимосвязанных мира: суровый, первобытный Земной шар (Earthly Sphere) и стерильный, техно-органический Утробный шар (Breastborne Sphere).  Главное отличие от первой части — геймплей. Здесь акцент смещен с дальнего боя на ближний. Ваш главный инструмент — боевой топор, а позже — бритвенно-острый бозе-лезвие. Стрельба отходит на второй план, и это заставляет по-новому подходить к боям и исследованию. Вместо того чтобы отстреливать врагов с дистанции, вы учитесь уворачиваться, парировать и наносить быстрые, точные удары.  Но настоящая звезда шоу — это **исследование**. Axiom Verge 2 предлагает одну из самых нелинейных карт в жанре. Уже через пару часов у вас появляется доступ к огромному количеству инструментов, которые ломают привычные границы. Ключевой механикой становится хакерский дрон, а позже — возможность вселяться в маленьких роботов и даже врагов. Это открывает по-настоящему головокружительные возможности для секвестров (обходов препятствий без получения нужного улучшения). Чувство удовлетворения, когда вы находите способ попасть в "закрытую" зону с помощью смекалки и доступных инструментов, — несравненное.  **Атмосфера и сюжет: Тайна превыше ясности**  Том Хэппи (создатель игры) снова демонстрирует свой талант в создании гнетущей и загадочной атмосферы. Саундтрек, как и в первой части, блестящий, сочетающий эмбиентные синтезаторные звуки с резкими, индастриал-мотивами. Миры пустынны, опасны и полны скрытых секретов.  Сюжет подается через нахождение дневников и аудиозаписей, оставляя пространство для собственных интерпретаций. Он тесно переплетается с событиями первой игры, но не требует их обязательного знания. Это самостоятельная история о технологиях, человечности и силах, лежащих за гранью понимания.  **Не без шероховатостей**  Игра не идеальна. Некоторые могут найти боевую систему излишне упрощенной, особенно по сравнению с богатым арсеналом Трейса. Босс-файты, являющиеся кульминацией многих метроидваний, здесь часто представляют собой скорее головоломки или уникальные столкновения, а не испытания чисто боевого мастерства. Это может разочаровать любителей классических схваток.  Кроме того, несмотря на гениальную нелинейность, карта временами может быть немного запутанной, а быстрая travel-система открывается достаточно поздно.  **Вердикт**  Axiom Verge 2 — это смелый, экспериментальный и безумно амбициозный проект. Это не та игра, которая пытается угодить всем. Она берет жанр метроидвания и смело тянет его в новом направлении, делая ставку на исследование, головоломки и атмосферу, а не на экшен.  **Плюсы:** *   Гениальная, нелинейная система исследования *   Уникальная механика вселения в врагов и дронов *   Потрясающая, давящая атмосфера и саундтрек *   Интригующий, загадочный сюжет *   Смелый уход от формул первой части  **Минусы:** *   Упрощенная боевая система может разочаровать *   Нетрадиционные и порой простые босс-файты *   Карта может показаться излишне запутанной  **Кому подойдет эта игра?** Если вы цените в метроидваниях в первую очередь **чувство открытия**, тайну и желаете исследовать мир, ломая его правила, — Axiom Verge 2 станет для вас жемчужиной. Если же вы ищете напряженный экшен и сложные бои с боссами, как в Hollow Knight или Ori, — подходите к игре с осторожностью и пониманием ее философии.  В итоге, Axiom Verge 2 — это триумф авторского замысла, достойное продолжение, которое не боится быть другим. И за это Тому Хэппи можно аплодировать стоя.

Yes, it’s different to the first one. I put it down quickly at launch, thinking the developer had really screwed up. Fortunately I was convinced to give it another go a few years later, and I am glad I did. Yes, it’s still different, but it’s as good as the first, for sure.

overall recommend, but it's not as solid as the first one.  pros: -an A+ accessibility menu. as someone who loves exploring in games and doesn't like the stress of a healthbar, the option to just turn off enemy damage is perfecto. every single game with a healthbar should have the option to turn off death, don't @ me.  -it's not just the first game again, which is worth credit. in concept it's a pro. in execution... we'll get there -by the end your mobility options are wide open, and getting around to all the last couple of spots i hadn't poked around yet felt really slick -fast travel between savepoints. mwah mwah chef's kiss. huge qol improvement on the first game  cons: -i never like a sequel to be a carbon copy of the first thing, but this felt like it went a bit too far in the other direction for me? there are recognisable things from the first game, but it doesn't hugely feel like it's set in the same universe (whatever 'universe' means for this series) or much continuity or anything. i didn't 100% so maybe there were notes i missed -there are points where you need to fall through thin platforms, and i don't think the game ever communicates what the controls are for that? (down + jump) spent half an hour stuck in a box thinking i'd have softlocked myself by getting stuck there, if not for the option to save+quit from the pause menu and respawn at the last savepoint with all your progress (returning qol from the first game, incrediblé) -pacing of both story and exploration is off sometimes. too many gates with a switch on the other side just arbitrarily blocking you at any given moment, and in the mid-game it feels like it's limiting exploration. storywise, at one point your character gets severely inconvenienced and limited, and i got the impression from dialogue that it was an end-of-act-one-lead-into-act-two sequence, and i wasn't really feeling the idea of dealing with that limitation so i thought i'd rush story to get past it. and, uh, turns out the 'sequence' ran muuuuuuuch longer than i thought. (gameplay limitation does get fixed relatively quickly to be fair, but plot point wasn't resolved for a while after) -writing isn't fantastic and ending is a bit of a wet fart, but i don't really play these kinds of games for the story anyway so i wasn't too put off personally -little bit short?  i know the pros list is shorter than the cons, but i enjoyed it more than i didn't. this feels like someone's first 'okay' game before making a banger, but they've happened the other way around this time? bit weird, but i had fun. maybe wait for a sale

For an hour or two i thought that i'll ditch it, but no. After "the event", game expands quite nicely and becomes far more fun.   Where Axiom Verge 2 shines is in how its world is interconnected and how it all works together. Although it's not even close to being as alien as AV1's world, and that can be a bit of a meh for some, but it is alien enough to stay interesting. Movement skills are fun, environmental puzzles are easy but not in a obvious way, so if you like exploration it can click with you. Lore is interesting too, deepens AV1 lore nicely.  Combat is crap tho, no other way to say it, it's crap. Clunky to the point of sapping all fun from the gameplay. After some time i just ignored most enemies. You get nothing important for defeating them and all they do is drain your resources. Combat hacking enemies is mostly useless. Bosses are mostly optional and easy.  Finished the game with 79.90% items and 96.35% map. I don't feel any interest to 100% it or search for secrets but it was far more fun than it was frustrating.

Смешанные эмоции от этой игры. Геймплейно она очень приятная, и я получил определённое удовольствие, бегая по карте в поисках улучшений. Визуал тоже неплохой, но какая же Axiom Verge 2 простая и… пустая. Если бы не видосы на фоне, я бы сошёл с ума — девять часов бегать по огромным локациям с редкими врагами под заунывное пение.  Разработчики ещё и хотели, чтобы я останавливался почитать записки. Ну вы знаете — эти записки в метроидваниях. Не понял этой идеи ещё в первой части, но, видимо, без них не обойтись, если и во второй игре они есть.  И какая радость наконец-то встретить босса! Но как жаль, что любого босса можно закликать одной кнопкой.  Неплохая идея с переключением на дрона, отдельной картой и улучшениями для него, но глобально это мало что меняет. За дрона бегать чуть веселее (тем более я сам Дрон), немного разнообразия это добавляет, но теперь у нас в два раза больше скучных локаций с бессмысленными улучшениями.  Прокачка персонажа — это вообще что? Никакой мотивации собирать ресурсы для неё нет. Пару раз улучшить здоровье и атаку — и уже достаточно для прохождения. Всё остальное — либо сомнительные апгрейды, которые нужны только для доступа в комнаты с ещё улучшениями, либо абсолютно бесполезный мусор. В любом метроиде древних времён получение апгрейда имело бОльший смысл. Даже добавка в пять ракет к уже имеющимся пятидесяти там воспринимается приятнее. Как это вообще возможно?  Разочарование сплошное, а не игра. Я даже начал сомневаться, насколько первая часть была хорошей — может, в тот момент я просто мало играл в других представителей жанра. Наверное, лучше и не проверять, чтобы не разочароваться ещё раз.  Сейчас дописываю текст и понимаю, что эмоции вовсе не такие уж смешанные — разве что эта смесь на 90% негативная.

I don't really want to recommend *or* not recommend this game because it isn't really bad enough that I find anything objectionable, but it isn't good enough that I think anyone in particular should play it. It kind of just exists. if you really like the story of Axiom Verge 1 and if you find the act of exploration interesting enough in itself to be a game of its own this is alright. The environments are tidy, the traversal is clean, and the world itself is pretty big.  One of the bigger issues with the game is just that combat is very underwhelming. Your tools for fighting are kinda crap, the enemies themselves tend to lean more towards annoying than challenging, you can usually damage boost your way through everything, and it just feels like there was a general lack of design focus on combat. Almost every boss in the game can be killed just by standing in front of it and spamming your main attack until it dies. Even if one of them manages to kill you before you kill it, almost in every instance you'll spawn in nearby, the boss's hp won't be reset, and you can just continue to wail on it. I barely remember any details about any of them except for this one snake thing that you have to bomb nearby rocks to damage. It wasn't particularly interesting, it was just something that required a different method to defeat. Then there is the final boss which at least has some of the trappings of being an actual boss, but once you realize there is a revival pot in the middle of the room the tension is right out the window and it just becomes an annoying puzzle segment. This really prevents the game from feeling like it ever reaches a crescendo of any kind. Most Metroidvania games use bosses as a capstone to an area. Axiom Verge 2 barely treats them as an event and for the most part they are just something you bonk your head against for a few seconds to get a very minor upgrade.  The story has pretty substantial ties to Axiom Verge 1, though you don't really interact much with the core cast of that game. The main draw that connects Indra (your main character) to Trace (the first game's main character) is a mutual acquaintance in Elizabeth Hammond. Hammond isn't a super built up character in the first game... she sort of gets mentioned. It's easy to have forgotten that Hammond ever existed just from having played Axiom Verge 1, so maybe be ready to pick up a wiki to look up characters. There is at least one mildly interesting twist that happens in the main narrative, but for the most part the meat of the story seems to be in inferences that you have to make based on the content of notes and a handful of things that might seem like eastereggs or creepy pasta nonsense at first glance. And while the implications (should you manage to think about them at all without consulting fan theory) are interesting, I just don't really think they amount to much unless there is going to be a third game at some point that explicitly sets out to tie everything up in a neat package rather than set up another dozen minor mysteries. The story focus does build a bit towards the second half, but I don't really consider the conclusion to be all that satisfying with or without the 3 second post credits scene at the end for high completion percentages. I don't know if any of it says anything.  Still, competent game, it's not too long, and if you don't mind being a little frustrated and aimless (and if you are a fan of the genre you should be ok with that), it's interesting enough to warrant a play through.

Axiom Verge 2 builds on the foundation laid by its predecessor, expanding the Metroidvania formula with fresh ideas, deeper narrative layers, and refined gameplay mechanics, all while maintaining the distinctive retro-inspired aesthetic that fans have come to love. Developed again by Thomas Happ, this sequel takes players on another immersive journey through an alien world, but with a notable evolution in scope and complexity. It manages to balance nostalgia with innovation, delivering a sequel that feels both familiar and excitingly new, offering an experience that will appeal to both longtime fans and newcomers to the series.  Visually, Axiom Verge 2 continues the tradition of pixel art mastery but introduces more detailed environments and a richer color palette. The world feels alive and varied, moving beyond the darker, more claustrophobic corridors of the original to include expansive outdoor areas and diverse biomes. These vibrant settings are complemented by an atmospheric soundtrack that enhances the mood without overpowering the gameplay. The audio design, including sound effects and music, reflects a polished and thoughtful approach, adding layers of immersion and emotion to the exploration and combat encounters.  Gameplay in Axiom Verge 2 retains the core Metroidvania structure of exploration, puzzle-solving, and combat, but it introduces new mechanics that add depth and complexity. The protagonist, Indra, possesses a unique ability to create living weapons from her own body, which can be upgraded and customized, offering a level of strategic choice and personalization that is more pronounced than in the first game. The controls are smooth and responsive, and the combat feels more dynamic, with enemies exhibiting a greater variety of behaviors that require adaptive tactics. The sequel also introduces a more interconnected world design, encouraging backtracking and exploration, but with a better-guided progression that reduces potential frustration.  Narratively, Axiom Verge 2 takes a more ambitious and philosophical approach, exploring themes of identity, consciousness, and the nature of existence. The story unfolds gradually, using environmental clues, cryptic dialogue, and intriguing lore fragments to create a haunting and thought-provoking atmosphere. Unlike the more straightforward mystery of the original, this sequel invites players to engage more deeply with its narrative, posing questions about reality and self-awareness that linger long after the game ends. This intellectual layer enriches the gameplay experience, making it not just a mechanical challenge but a narrative journey as well.  The game also addresses some of the pacing and accessibility issues from its predecessor by refining the map system and incorporating clearer visual cues, making navigation more intuitive without sacrificing the joy of discovery. The puzzles are cleverly integrated, often requiring creative use of Indra’s unique abilities and careful observation of the environment. This blend of action and puzzle-solving keeps the gameplay fresh and engaging throughout, preventing stagnation and encouraging players to experiment with different strategies.  While Axiom Verge 2 is a significant step forward, it does retain some elements that may not appeal to all players. The slower, more deliberate pace might feel cumbersome to those expecting constant action, and the sometimes abstract storytelling demands patience and attention to detail. However, these aspects contribute to the game’s unique charm and depth, rewarding players who invest themselves fully in its world.  In conclusion, Axiom Verge 2 is a worthy successor that respects the legacy of the original while boldly expanding its horizons. It offers a richer, more polished Metroidvania experience, with refined gameplay, a hauntingly beautiful world, and a narrative that challenges players on both an emotional and intellectual level. For fans of the genre and those who appreciate games that blend exploration, combat, and storytelling into a cohesive whole, Axiom Verge 2 delivers a compelling adventure that is as thought-provoking as it is entertaining. It stands as a testament to Thomas Happ’s vision and craftsmanship, proving that a solo developer can continue to push the boundaries of what indie games can achieve.  Rating: 7/10

Ho atteso molto prima di volermi approcciare al secondo capitolo di Axion Verge, perché rispetto al 1 era totalmente diverso seppur la componente videoludica è la medesima. Ma sapendo a cosa sarei andato incontro non me la sentivo di comprarlo subito, anche se ho amato alla follia il primo Axion Verge, ma l'idea di dover principalmente giocare solo per esplorare con pochissime se non nulle meccaniche di combattimento/sfida ho reputato eccessivo il prezzo di listino. Ma senz'altro era nella mia lista dei preferiti ed un domani lo avrei giocato perché sapevo che comunque sarebbe stata un esperienza piacevole e senza pentimenti. E al netto di alcune critiche doverose da fare, così è stato. Tom Happ è una certezza, assurdo come da solo sia riuscito a tirar fuori un capolavoro come 1 capitolo e comunque un ottimo secondo gioco seppur totalmente distinto dal primo (non capendone il motivo, rischiosa come scelta visto che il primo ha avuto un enorme successo ma vabbè son scelte ha voluto dosare probabilmente). Parto subito col dire che Axion Verge 2 è un titolo validissimo ma giocato secondo me a prezzo più basso. Appunto perché manca quasi totalmente la componente d'azione. Sì, ci sono i nemici che ti colpiscono e puoi acquisire le abilità di combattimento... ma il feedback è totalmente fuori fuoco, puoi comodamente skipparli i nemici raggirandoli, hackerarli per non combatterli mai sul serio ed il sistema di combattimento lascia a desiderare. Anche i boss, qualcosa di scandaloso. La maggior parte tutti buggati, li permaskilli e rimangono fermi e mai seriamente impegnativo. Ma ripeto, Axion Verge 2 non punta sul combattimento perciò non l'ho comprato subito, sapevo che avrei comunque giocato ad un titolo non al pieno del suo potenziale come al predecessore. Ma debbo dire che, a livello narrativo e di esplorazione si è davvero superato! Ha qualcosa in più del primo, ancor più difficile ed impegnativo da completare al 100% e soprattutto un buon feeling di storia (che si spera il finale porti ad un futuro sequel). Le musiche le ho sentite meno focussate del 1 capitolo ma comunque gradevoli e ben impregnate. La cosa che mi ha stupito di più è stato il feedback di progressione man mano che si acquisiscono abilità, più ricco e gradevole e soprattutto ho adorato tanto giocare il drone. Tant'è che il PG personale l'ho trascurato di più e potenziato meno. Mi spiace solo che la difficoltà nel completare la mappa e gli oggetti vari sia stata fatta con tanta confusione, perché al di là della difficoltà c'è troppa incoerenza in dove e come ci si sposta da un mondo all'altro. Non è chiaro né tantomeno esplicativo. Ti buttano i ping sulla mappa e buona fortuna, molto dispersivo. Chi non è pratico dei Metroidvania si ritroverà abbastanza in difficoltà, io bene o male a parte 2 occasioni in cui ho perso parecchio tempo son riuscito a chiuderlo. Ma essendomi appesantito un sacco nel fare tutto ciò che mi è stato possibile SENZA AIUTI non me la sento di mettermi a vedere le varie guide dove poter trovare le restanti cose. Contate che il gioco lo si può terminare tra le 8 e le 10h se tutto va bene, io né ho superate poco più 15h e preferisco terminare al 98.8% di mappa esplorata ed 87.7% oggetti ottenuti. So che potrei arrivare al 100%, ma sapendo che mi richiederebbero altre ore nel vedere i vari tutorial passo passo non me la sento proprio. Sono comunque soddisfatto che per conto mio, abbia fatto tale score. Io si solito eccello nelle skill e meno nei collezionabili, seppur me la cavo piuttosto bene. Per tutti quelli che hanno adorato e finito il 1 AV e vorrebbero prendere il 2, siate pronti e preparati ad un esperienza diversa. Perché qui è incentrato tutto sul collezionare/esplorare ad una difficoltà superiore dove però è ben fatto e godibile, con una narrazione ben fatta. Ma vi consiglio lo sconto come ho fatto io. D'altro canto se vuoi fare un gioco incentrato su questi aspetti, non puoi farmi un combattimento quasi del tutto assente o realizzato male, con IA poco attenta e dei boss per lo più buggati.

I had a blast playing this game. I was a bit apprehensive at first because I'm more into Metroid than Castlevania and I read that this game leans further to the latter than the first but I still really enjoyed it. The combat really is quite fun and responsive with very fair difficulty on normal settings but with tons of tweaking available in the accessibility options. The art style is a huge tonal shift away from body horror and dread and more into a beautiful loneliness but is more cohesive than the first game. The music is absolutely superb and I've been listening to it for weeks on Spotify. The story ads a ton to where we left off from the first game while still leaving lots of room for more storytelling. I really hope Mr Happ is able/willing to return to this project and bring us back to this multiverse.

Axiom Verge 2 is a solid Metroid clone if you want little to no combat, just a well-put-together map and some fun powerups. Its pacing (of how often you find items/powerups) is its best trait, though I think the first game was a bit better about that. The world opens up steadily. Some powerups felt quite clever, and even familiar types of upgrade (wall climb, grappling hook) felt fresh because they didn't work the same as others. The fast travel you get eventually may be convenient, but it also led me to disengage with the world and rush the lategame objectives, exaggerating how abrupt the ending felt. It also felt like I only needed to use some later powerups 2-4 times, so in a way the experience lost steam in the end.  The combat in AV2 is almost completely optional, which really shows how mediocre it is. Combat falls into the two typical boring playstyles of face-tanking -- not bothering to dodge and mashing attacks until you win -- and turreting -- finding cover and jumping and shooting from that spot until you win. This goes for basic enemies and bosses. So, it doesn't feel skillful, and after fighting a couple bosses, I realized I wouldn't lose much by skipping them. It's impressive that I generally enjoyed my time with the game anyway; at the least, it reflects well on the exploration.  AV2, like AV1, has poorly paced lore, but here the issue is info dumps. I played every other day or so for a week, but struggled to retain information, and although I liked bits of the story, it didn't feel like it had much of interest to say. Also like AV1, AV2's soundtrack is too energizing for its darkish story and ambient exploration. In Super Metroid, I occasionally felt genuinely unnerved as I explored; I never did in AV1 or 2.  Overall, I'm mixed on AV2. I went into it thinking I'd prefer it to AV1, but it feels about the same, and I care less about it than the first. If you like one you'll like the other, but I've played several metroidvanias I've preferred, including most of the actual 2D Metroid series, and Zexion, which blows AV1 and 2 out of the water IMO.

incredible game, one of the best metroidvanias i'v ever played, the exploration is 99% non-linear and always rewarding, all the unlocks are unique and fun to use, and the skill ceiling rises with every new item you find.  he enemies are deadly and require strategy to beat because they are extremely intelligent and reactive (for a 2d game). they chase, jump over obstacles dodge your attacks and always try to position themselves to be hard to hit and hard to avoid  you don't need to play the first Axiom Verge to enjoy this game, it's almost a completely different story.

All this being said, it's a recommend from me *only* if you really liked the first one.

これ聞いていた事前情報を思い返すと「なるほどなー」と思った。 大体1とは違うという話だったが。 大筋としては  前作がメトロイドフォロアーだったことを思えば、今作はメトロイドヴァニアになっている。 メトロイド風味はかなり減少した。 飛び道具があまり使えない。接近戦メイン。 撃破必須の通せんぼボスはほぼいない。ただでかくて強いスルー可能な雑魚がいるだけ。 探索カラーはさらに上がった。というか[h1]良くも悪くも探索地獄[/h1]  というところ、本当にその通りだった。 なので  [h1]アクシオムヴァージ1のイメージで考えると全然別物にしか見えなくなってくる可能性も結構ある。[/h1] 批判もそこまで的外れでもない。 勿論やっぱり同じ系譜のゲームだなと思うこともあるが、人によっては求めていたものじゃない可能性もかなりある。 その点は注意。 あとこれ片方を買った後、面白かったからもう一方もと思っても、後からバンドル買えないタイプらしい。 なので買うなら同時セットか、単品狙いする覚悟を。 知ってりゃ最初からセット狙ったんだけどなあ……1もっかいやりたくてもバンドル値引き使えねえぇ。   前作は別ハードでプレイ。 そのままメトロイドっぽいゲームだと思った。 当然違いはあるのでクローンなどではなく、独特のプレイ感があるいいゲームだと思ったが。 自力でなんとかエンディング一つは見たのだが、攻略情報も見つからず他にエンディングがあるのか？どう決着しているのかもよくわからないままになってしまった。 で、Steam来て早々に2が安かったので購入して放置。やっと崩せた。   最初気になったのは「え？この女社長が主人公操作キャラなの？」ということと、アイテム取った時の効果音がアクシオムヴァージだ、そして今回は殴る武器と飛び道具で明確な違いがあるらしいというところ。 序盤はピッケル女社長奮戦記。若くも見えないけどどんだけアクティブなのか……。  前作は武器異様な数出てくるわりに、射程と攻撃力の差がさっぱりわからなかった。 今回はその辺わりとすっきりわかりやすくなった。  同じマップを能力追加の度に行ったり来たりすることになるので、正直かなり迷いやすい。 正解ルートがどこなのか……目の前のあれこれにこだわっているとハメられる。 敵の当たりも異様に強く、セーブ回復ポイントも近いようで遠く、すぐ死にかねない。 目の前に見えていても延々遠回りしてやっと手に入ったり、疲れること……。 どうやら元々前作もメトロイド風味ながらより探索重視のゲームだったらしいが、今回さらに探索重視になっている。 やーっと見つけた次の導線、おー範囲が広がったという作業に没頭できないと辛いかも。 そこにハマるとやめられなくなるが。  強烈なアスレチックやキチガイレベルのボスはいないが、だからといってお手柔らかにはいかない。 驚いたのはえ、これボスだったの？という雑魚敵。 確かにデカいしめちゃくちゃ体力あるしなんだこれと思ってはいたけど、倒して実績解除されて初めて気づいた。 雑魚の当たりは本当にきついので、気を抜くとあっという間に死ぬ。  で、これ [h1]オプション、アクセシビリティに敵と味方のダメージ倍率を弄る項目がある。 辛い人はこれすぐ弄りましょうということらしい。[/h1] これで探索メインどころか完全探索オンリー敵の存在を蹴散らしながら進められるようにもなる。 実際初期値だとダメージが異様に高く、一々きちんと対応せずに雑に扱うと雑魚相手ですらすぐ死んでセーブポイントからやり直しになるため、相当うざったい。 前作比で考えると本当にきつめ。回復も少ない。 硬い敵に大ダメージ食らいながら探索という苦行プレイをする意味は多分ない。 自分はそれでも文句言いながらやっていたが、途中の長丁場のハンデキャップ戦（大体察して、あそこです）の時に音を上げて思い切り弄らせてもらった。 おかげで当たりは随分楽になって探索に集中できるくらい楽に。 結局初回クリア後の死亡回数は13回と表示されたが、多分倍率弄ってからは1度くらい（わざとで）しか死んでいない。  実績に絡むことも一切ない。 つまりこの方面のバランス取りは完全放棄で好きにやれということらしい。 わかっていないとほんとにダメージバランスきついし、ただでさえ探索も本腰入れないと駄目で同じ場所延々行き来することになるので、両方このきつさでは続かない可能性もある。 というか自分は続かなかった。 この辺は放り出さずに前作くらいのバランスちゃんと取って欲しかった気もするが。 全体的に回復の出が悪いんだよなあ今回の雑魚。回復量も少ない その辺の調整だけでいけそうな気もするんだが。   エンディングまで到達してみればあの独特のおどろおどろしさ、不気味な世界は色々変化はしていてもやっぱりこのゲームだなと納得したし、翻訳もまあ気にならないし（早速「再開」と普通の誤植をしてはいたが）、探索は絶望するほど堪能させてもらった。 特に途中から手に入る要素で「え、これもしかして凄まじく探索範囲広がらない！？」と思った時はゾッとした。 しかし案外うまくいくもので、確かに取りこぼしは大量にありはしたもののちゃんとクリアできた。 いや脱落ポイントも無数にあった気はしたが。  ちらっと読んだ攻略では、序盤に手に入りそうな強化をあっさり見逃したままさっさと次々進んでいたりもした。 クリア後はちゃんと攻略見ながらやってみた。 さすがに自力でやっていたらこれ以上はいつまで経っても終わらないだろうし……。 いやこれ情報見てさえとてもじゃないけど埋まる気しないぞ。 それどころか[h1]どこ取ってないかわからん[/h1] アシスト要素が想像以上に頼りにならない。 背景も青地に白が多いのに、アシストも白く光るため、画面ではなく隅のレーダーをしっかり凝視していないと仮にアイテムあるよと光っても気づきにくい。 これだけが頼りなのにちょっと気を抜くと見逃してしまう。 範囲広げるか赤くフラッシュして隅注視しなくても気づけるくらいの派手さにして欲しかった。  多分あと1個だけなんだけどなあ。 延々画面中探し回るより、諦めて最初から攻略通りにやった方がマシかも……いやそれも辛いな。  結局虱潰しでなんとか全実績解除。 これが一番時間かかって辛かった……。 ハッキングもできるかこんなもんレベルだった。  [h1]探索地獄を楽しめる人だけようこそ。 敵ダメージ倍率は0.1、味方ダメージ倍率は4倍でどうぞ。[/h1]  これはこれで強化の意味合いがほぼなくなってしまって極端すぎるけど。 一度クリア後はメッセージ類を全カットできる「スピードラン」でプレイするのがオススメかな。 こちらでのクリアも実績のうちだし、やってみれば既読スキップ以上の効果でプレイに集中できる。 それでもアイテム取得時のジングルとかワープ時の再構成とか、ちょっと時間かかってしまうのだけど。  自分はやっぱこのシリーズ好きだなあ。肌が合うというか。 次も期待したい。

Axiom Verge 2 is not just a sequel, but a reflection. Quiet, strange, intriguing. It gives you not action, but the opportunity to get lost in another world. And that's what I respect it for.  🎮 What's here:  A world that grabs you not with its design, but with its atmosphere. Dark Antarctica, hidden worlds, ancient technologies.  Gameplay without guns - yes, you heard right. Tactics, hacking and a smart approach to enemies are more important here.  Minimalism with depth. You are given a minimum of explanations, but a maximum of freedom. Exploring here is a real pleasure.  🌍 What surprised:  Lore. The game unexpectedly touches on real cultures, Sumerian mythology and sci-fi ideas that you rarely find in metroidvanias.  The soundtrack is hypnotic. Just like an underground signal from another dimension.

Huh, this one was much more simpler than the previous title. Less game breaking hack stuff, overly simplified combat, less brain busting puzzles and also less bosses and I think the world seemed somewhat smaller...  The previous titles had a Metroid-style game play which I liked a lot. The one plays similarly to those "Metroidvania" titles with melee combat and more free exploration with wider areas instead of corridors.  As with previous title, going for 100% is just ridiculous. You have to scan every room for secret paths, portals and do puzzles. I solved...most of them, but still there was just too many frustrating secrets that I just didn't find, even if I scoped around quite a lot and scanned the rooms for secrets as I went along.   I'll give this a mixed review just because it felt so short and there just wasn't that much to do in this one. I got the better ending with +90% items and 99% (yeah) map completition. But the plot didn't seem that good this time around.  But the gameplay is solid enough that I can't give this a negative review, but there are a lot better Metroidvania titles around. I liked Axiom Verge 1 a lot more. Play that one instead.

[h2]One of the strangest sequels the indie world has seen...[/h2]  ...but still a thumbs up from me, somehow.  Axiom Verge 2 follows up its acclaimed predecessor by reworking most of its mechanics, abandoning the dark atmosphere and 8-bit-inspired graphics, reducing combat to a triviality, and switching your laser guns with a pickaxe and a boomerang. Huh?  Its title should've been anything other than "Axiom Verge" plus the number two. [b]There's no building on the foundation of the previous entry here[/b], it's a sharp turn of the wheel from the developer to catch a completely different highway to a completely different headspace. Honestly, a shot in the foot when it comes to setting the right expectations.  (Warning: several negative paragraphs coming up, but bear with me.)  [h3]The bad is bad and the weird is weird[/h3]  [list] [*]You can tell something's off from the get-go. The menu greets you with cheery music with an essence of adventure. Then you hit NEW GAME and you get a wall of text with still images introducing the story. What happened to the cool cinematics from the first game? The presentation is so much worse. In fact, the story as a whole felt undercooked, the main character is uninteresting and the secondary ones are as deep as a footnote, everything is overexplained, there's barely any sense of mystery unlike AV1... Even the dialogue boxes are noticeably worse, they lack character portraits and that satisfying clickety sound as the text fills in. [*]There's a handful of useful abilities to find but this time around, you have a SKILL TREE™. (Yes, a SKILL TREE™. Why, you ask? What a funny coincidence, I ask the same!) In this SKILL TREE™ you'll upgrade your health, attack and some other simple values. This means most of the items you find in the world will be SKILL POINTS™ to spend on your SKILL TREE™; other than that, you can find notes that provide lore and a few health upgrades that feel inconsequential since the biggest health upgrades will come from your SKILL TREE™. So this SKILL TREE™ manages to both feel uninspired in itself and make the exploration less exciting. [*]What's more, the drone ability makes a comeback and, in an ironic twist, this drone ends up being more useful to traverse the world than your actual character, while standing its ground against the enemies without much problem. There's not much reason to play as human for a big portion of the game, rendering the human-related upgrades in your SKILL TREE™ effectively useless. [*]Over 5 paragraphs into this review and I still haven't talked about the combat. That speaks for itself. The enemies are fewer in number but more lethal (at least in the first half of the game), they now have vision range and you can sneak past them. You'll have to rely on melee weapons, except for a boomerang and a hack tool. The latter lets you apply some interesting effects on enemies, but I just miss the lovely glitch-fest that was the hack tool from AV1, imo its most memorable mechanic. [*]And the bosses... boy, what happened here? They're big enemies you may randomly find while exploring, there's no incentive or obligation to kill them and they don't complement the story in any way. The reward for defeating them is (you guessed it) SKILL POINTS™. Oh, and they are as easy as jumping to their heads and spamming attack. I couldn't even tell you what the later bosses' attack patterns were from how fast they went down. The idea of making all bosses optional is promising, but by also making them trivial, unremarkable in their design, and irrelevant to the lore surrounding them, I'm just left scratching my head. [/list]  Just so many weird decisions were taken in the making of AV2. But hey...  [h3]The good is good![/h3]  Ignoring my endless rant just now, believe it or not the game can still be a good time. I can't bring myself to slap the thumbs down because [b]I actually had a decent time playing this[/b], despite my confusion and disagreements with its design. [list] [*]Exploration is the big focus and by far the best aspect of the game. In classic metroidvania style you'll make your way through the different areas, clashing your head against a dozen "how do I even get there?" paths until you find the actual reachable one, and soon enough the whole map opens up and everything interconnects smoothly.  [*]The game features special areas with a distinguishable graphic style, separated from the main world. Let's just say that once I understood the actual concept behind them, I was blown away. Dare I say the map comes together in a much more impressive way than AV1? [*]There's actual fast travel, a big improvement over the first entry. I never again wanna sit through the long and tedious spawning animation that plays everytime you fast travel, but there's fast travel. [*]The minimap is more detailed, it's not just squares anymore. Much easier to remember those places with unreachable paths—speaking of which, you can place as many markers on the map as you want! You're not limited to 2 anymore. [*]The accessibility options are always welcome. You can tune the difficulty however you like, or just remove the challenge altogether. [/list]  [h3]Conclusion[/h3]  If you come in blind after the first game left you thirsty for more, Axiom Verge 2 will feel like a cold splash to the face that'll leave you'll be confused and disappointed—and fairly so, because this is by all means a bad follow-up to AV1. It's should've been named something else, plain and simple. But the main thing that it sets out to do (exploration), it does REALLY well. So if you know what you're getting yourself into and can be open to a unique entry in the metroidvania genre, yes, this is worth your time.

I really enjoyed this game. It built on the foundation of its predecessor and branched out a lot. The map and progression make sense, the story was intriguing, the graphics are amazing, the map is excellent, and the movement feels natural and satisfying. It is packed full of hidden items and lore just like the first game. I found it to be easier in some ways and harder in others.  Spoilery notes: [spoiler]I think this game really benefited from not having 15 guns to choose from. I did miss the kilver, though. I hated the drone's grapple/slingshot mechanism at first, but I persevered, and it soon became second nature. This is my bias against boss fights talking, but I didn't miss having so many unskippable boss fights. I almost feel like the game ended too soon, but maybe that was the developer intentionally leaving room for another sequel. I really wanted to 100% the map without using a guide, but there's one square that seems to make that impossible, which is vexing.[/spoiler] By the end, [spoiler]I no longer needed the human form. I was a crawling drone down to my very core.[/spoiler]

Do yourself a favor, use your map reminders. It helps a lot. Otherwise, take it slow, really enjoy the music immerse yourself in the story especially if you played AV1 this has great theming and evolution of its graphics/music. Don’t expect grand boss fights, there’s a few that are cool but nothing technically challenging, dodge, keep distance attack when safe, you’ll be fine. This game is more fun when treated for its exploration and fantastic looking world,   *spoiler*  The drone is really fun. The beginning of the game can feel sluggish just take your time try to learn enemy movement, you’ll don’t have to fight anything unless you have to but it’s good to know how to quickly and safely dispatch your foes. Go for upgrades, do lots of exploring, use those map markers, and you’ll have one hell of an experience!   Overall, this is probably a 9.5/10 for me. At first I was harsh on its gameplay but then I got more used to it and shifted my perspective, clearly Mr. Happ was trying new things and innovating, and when you learn to play along with it you get a wonderful experience. That goes for both games really, they can be challenging but once learned really fun to play when paired with the graphics, story, and music. I cannot wait to hear more on the AV series, I hope everything is well Mr. Happ!!

Eu infelizmente vou ter que não recomendar esse jogo para pessoas que não jogaram o primeiro. Somente quem jogou o Axiom Verge 1 vai ter o interesse.  Axiom Verge 2 no começo parece um jogo que não tem muito a ver com o 1. Não tem como não comparar pois com o desenrolar da história existe muita coisa que se sobrepõe com os eventos e universo do primeiro jogo.   Se você compara esse jogo com o primeiro, ele está muito abaixo do esperado, ele não conseguiu fazer o que o primeiro consegue: entregar um mapa incrível e misterioso, com um arsenal enorme e sempre novo, entregando mecânicas que são excepcionais e variam muito o gameplay.  Se você tenta ver ele como um jogo separado, por si só, sem comparações, ainda é muito superficial e desinteressante, principalmente no começo. É provável que se você não jogou o primeiro jogo, você pare de jogar nas primeiras duas horas. O mapa é em sua maior parte desinteressante, o level design não tem muito de especial, tirando algumas pouquíssimas áreas. As habilidades são extremamente simples no começo e até no final do jogo, você passa horas sem alguma novidade relevante. O mapa parece vazio, mas não é um vazio intrigante, só parece meio mal acabado, faltando algo. O final consegue ser abaixo das minhas expectativas que já estavam baixas, mas um acontecimento de última hora até que foi interessante. Mas se você não jogou o primeiro jogo vai ficar boiando infelizmente.  Eu esperava mais desse jogo, de verdade. O primeiro é espetacular, consegue imergir o jogador em um universo de perguntas fantásticas com uma arte diferente e jogabilidade divertida e interessante. Axiom Verge 2 é um jogo meio sem sal que merecia um pouco mais de tempo de desenvolvimento...

This is a great game. There are some downsides, though, that make this not essential, and less good compared to the original Axiom Verge. The game still has really good upgrades, and it's still fun to explore the world. The story is as confusing as ever, so don't worry about it if you don't understand the game world. However, the boss fights in this game are nonexistent; there are two "bosses" that you have to fight to beat the game, but you can't really lose to them. There are also stronger enemies that count as the game's bosses, but if you have enough HP, the fastest way to beat them is to just run up to it and start hitting it without worrying about getting hit yourself. Secondly, the music is less good compared to the original game (which, admittedly, has some weird music). Lastly, the combat itself feels less polished, because a lot of enemies are just like the bosses: attack them, hope you don't take too much damage. This is still a good game, but focus on better metroidvanias first. 9/10.

Если хотите познакомиться с Axiom Verge начать лучше с этой части.Так как это приквел к первой части,судя по графике и вооружению.Иначе как объяснить разницу в картинке между первой и второй частями. Здесь интересная механика с перемещением между внешним миром и разломом,куда может входить только дрон. Механика со взломом врагов,дверей. Дрон теперь полноценный второстепенный персонаж ,а не утилита как в первой части. Ну карта вроде получше стала по сравнению в первой частью,но все так же хренова,среди пикселей сложно что-либо  разобрать,метки можно ставить но они ставятся на всю комнату а не на конкретный уголок,приходится постоянно проверять что же там пометил,дверь,склянку или способность очередную. Телепорт в игре есть,открывается в конце снежной локации.Которая находится в левом нижнем углу карты. Без телепорта игра пиз... в общем очень душной кажется. Стандартно  путаешься куда идти,сложности добавляет то что пока по сюжету ты управляешь дроном можешь забыть способности главного персонажа,которые так же нужны для продвижения по сюжету.Некоторые места открываются если дрона использовать определенным образом,например войти в разлом,переключить карту и посмотреть куда надо попасть,идешь туда потом возвращаешься во внешний мир,и можешь пройти дальше или открыть секрет.Из-за того что подобное или не видел вообще или видел один-два раза в других играх  это сложно в голове держать как возможный путь дальше. В общем и целом исследовать мир 2 части мало смысла,основные улучшения найдешь по сюжету,а без остальных можно обойтись,в секретах максимум что находил или банку с 1 очком умений,или + к хп,или портал для дрона,или тот же + к хп только при наличии 5 таких кусочков.Прокачка навыков также не сильно важна,из того что пригодилось это +хп,сложность взлома,скорость атаки дрона,урон ледоруба у персонажа. Эта часть больше сосредоточена на исследовании мира и чтении записок открывающих лор игры.Так что и динамика игры соответствующая. Тут настолько упор сделан на исследование мира что можно послать нафиг всех боссов кроме финального, и просто бегать искать записки и способности для продвижения,хотя наверное можно и записки не искать если пофигу на историю. Немного разочарован после первой части,ибо ожидал примерно такого геймплея с полутора десятками пушек,а получил ледоруб,бумеранг в качестве дальнобойного оружия, и второго персонажа в виде дрона и ухудшение картинки. Оценка нейтральная. Палец вверх только из-за первой части.

[h3]前作がメトロイド味１００％なら、本作はメトロイド味とヴァニア味が５０％ずつ入っているゲーム[/h3]  ・機械的なものが大幅に減り、自然物がかなり増えた。 ・銃の類が無くなり、近接と中距離武器の二択に変更 ・バグ利用みたいな能力が無くなり、移動手段が大分大人しくなった  １と同じようなメトロイド味強めを期待して買うと後悔する可能性大なので注意 普通のメトロイドヴァニアとして見れば面白いので買っても後悔はしない。

ChatGpt lied to me about this game. It said Axiom Verge 2 has zen-like meditative vibe because combat is optional and you can pause to think of a solution to your problem. It couldn't be more wrong. Robots would rush at you and there is no way to stealth around them and combat option is limited and pathetic. You simply cannot pause and think of a solution to the puzzle because robots are everywhere and it is a painful process trying to remove them with limited resources. Do I have to kill the giant helicopter robot? Do I have to kill the giant sea snake? I don't know. But I am sure my character is not capable of doing so, yet there is a possibility that they have progression related drop. I am stuck and I don't want to waste more time with this game.

Much like the first game, 90 minutes in and I'm completely stuck. Got this little robot thing and have no idea what I'm supposed to be doing now. Explored quite a bit, but if it dies I just get brought back to the main body, which is trapped.  Other than that, this game is much different to the first. If there are any bosses they are few and far in between, and I never saw one. Axiom Verge 2 is a 2021 sequel to the critically acclaimed metroidvania Axiom Verge from 2015, both made by the talented Thomas Happ. And it's the most frustrating kind of a sequel, the kind which if released under a different name, would be received far more positively. AV2 is so different in gameplay, presentation, and tone from the first game, that you could be forgiven thinking this was originaly developed as a different game, but then changed titles for brand recognition. But let's talk the game itself.  First, the Good: Exploration  Exploration is by far the strongest point of AV2. The areas are far less weird this time, but still visually distinctive, and the game feels far more open than AV1 in terms of routes you can take at any given moment. Abilities are once again different from your standard metroidvania fair of double jumps and dashes (although I do think the game could use some fast movement options), and this time your drone gets its own set of abilities, separate from your human form. Oh, and there's a fast travel!  By far the most interesting aspect of exploration is the existence of two overlaying maps, which you can jump between at specific points to bypass obstacles and access seemingly inaccessible areas.  Now, the Bad: Combat  Unlike the first game, which was 100% ranged combat, AV2 gives you a melee weapon and a boomerang as a ranged backup, which you can upgrade by distributing points gained by finding certain goodies throughout the world. The idea to change the nature of combat in itself wasn't bad, but its impementation IMO is. Why? Because the vast majority of enemies you encounter are ranged, with fast firing weapons, and take many hits to bring down. Some of them are flying, and move in weird, erratic patterns, which makes hitting them with your boomerang almost impossible, while they spray you with laser bullets from a screen away.   At some point yo uwill get frustrated and realize it's better to just not engage with this aspect of the gameplay. Okay, it's not the first metroidvania where combat is not a major element, except AV2 is built like it is - you keep finding different, cool weapons, and you can spend your hard-earned points to upgrade them. Yet at no point combat becomes anything more than a chore. Unless we're talking killing bosses, which this time are competely optional, and even more of a pushover than in the first game.   Lastly, the Meh: Overall Vibes  This will be hella subjective, so bear with me. I loved the first Axiom Verge in large part because of its weird, alien setting of Giger-esque biomachinery and extra-dimensional fauna and flora, complimented by electronic tracks driven by pulsating bass, harsh synths, and melancholic melodies. The sequel simply does not do it for me: the main world - Kiengir - is woodlands, mountains, lakes, cliffs, etc. with an occasional ruined settlement and monuments inspired by ancient Babylon. The second world - the Breach - is closer to the vibe of the first game, but it's marred by this weird 8bit stylization making everything look blocky. Not a fan. The idea for the world of Kiengir is neat enough (it's a world inside of a torus, which you can observe when in outside areas), but visually it's just too familiar to be truly alien.  And the music? Meeeeeeeh! There are some tracks that bring back the feel of the OG soundrack (An Otherworld is a great example, probably my fav track from the game, so is Primordial Axiom and Essence) but most of the OST is made up of wailings of a female singer, accompanied by the strumming of an acoustic guitar, trying to bring about the feel of ancient middle east. Sure, it works with the aforementioned setting, but man, I'm not feeling it in the slightest.  Just one final note about the story: it's interesting enough that it made me read the notes scattered around the world (same as the first AV). It's loosely connected to the story of the first game, you may recognize some names, and even some places. If you paid attention to the story and the setting of both games, there will be a couple of 'A-HA! I get it!' moments, which is always a plus in my opinion.  So with all that being said - do I recommend the game? I think yes. It's still a decent metroidvania, even with all it's flaws. And I acnowledge that a good chunk of my comlaints about the game are just a case of the game's artistic choices not aligning perfectly with my tastes. From what I've seen online, people seem to overall llike the acoustic soundrack, so I am in minority in this. On the other hand - I haven't seen anyone praise the combat, so I guess there's at least one consensus.

Well, this was a frustrating disappointment. I played the first one to 100% in the boxed version when it released and lost my save, so you'll have to take my word on that.  Before I start digging into this game, I'll list some positives: + The enemy AI is actually quite good + The map design is convoluted while still being decently fun + Decent pixel art with cool glitch graphic effects + Very distinctive sound effects  The game now has primarily melee combat, which is one of its big issues. Normal enemies are often fast, sometimes with ranged attacks, with a strangely precise and varied AI. Your character is slow in comparison with an even slower melee attack. You do get a boomerang, but it is very slow and doesn't do much damage. So even when you get used to it you often feel like a chicken with your head cut off trying to lure, corner, and chase down enemies.   You do eventually get faster weapons, but these are lower damage and there isn't much hitstun (sometimes none) so enemies will walk all over you if you don't kill them in a reasonable time, especially later on when enemies start doing a LOT of damage.  On top of trying to deal with ranged attack enemies with slow melee attacks you have several types of "bomb" enemies that explode when they get near, even if you kill them. They often move fast so if they spot you unless you commit to running away you will generally have to eat or juke a hit. Clearly the best way is to try to pick them off with the boomerang before they notice you, but it takes 3-5 SLOW shots as it hits the enemy and slowly returns (you can press the key again to speed up the return but it also returns it mid flight so you have to be careful there). The mini robot form's attack functions the same way, as you'll often mash it only for the saw to return before it hits an enemy.  Bosses you generally just sit on spamming attack and they generally die quickly.  Sometimes it is hard to tell the difference between normal monsters and bosses and normal monsters often have inflated health. Also, every time you die you have to wait about 7 seconds for a dust animation to happen, which is ok at first but you'll get tired of that quick.  The story is... well... there. The character work is pretty bad though, it seems to be done to check boxes and the designs are not great.  The music is popified "price of persia" music as I like to call it. It isn't bad, it has just been done better before, many times. Which... is really this game's other issue - there are a lot of good metroidvanias out there, especially since the first axiom released. Any of Team Ladybug's releases is better than this in almost every category (even the slightly disappointing latest release).

The combat was pretty underwhelming. I wish there were fewer weapons/items and more thought put into the engagements.
