I really haven't played much yet, but everything vibes poorly so far. It started with the starting cutscene not being pauseable, and instead trying to pause it skips it. I then notice the controls haven't adapted to my keyboard layout (something that is becoming more common thanks to proton), so I go in to change my keybindings.  They're... a bit of a mess. The items are in a really weird order for some reason, space is accept and enter is cancel, hitting the wrong button will leave the menu (resetting your changes), the keybinds aren't in a list (going down from item 2 doesn't loop to item 3), and so on. Home and End are bound by default?? Maybe it's designed for laptops?  After rebinding, the controls feel alright, but melee and ranged aren't bound next to each-other (They're item 1 and 3...) so combat was a bit strange at first. You unlock wall-climbing very early, and it feels like old 90's games where you have to fall down such that your hands interact with the ledge before you can climb up it, making poorly timed jumps feel terrible. I think this might be a nostalgia thing, perhaps, so maybe It's fine. I found it jarring. They then spend the next hour or two showering you with upgrades, just barely introducing an item before giving you the next one. It's well possible this isn't really indicative of anything, but not having played more it's... at least unexpected from a metroidvania.  Overall, I found many segments tedious, and the ones that didn't feel tedious were either mundane or over too fast. The respawn animation is insanely long, and some segments where you become a tiny little robot had a very long (30s and up) reset if you ever missed a jump or went through the wrong door at the wrong time.  The speedrun leaderboards make no sense, the any% world record is 605h, the second place is 10 minutes. The second place any% is at 2.5%, the record low% is at 16%. The record 100% is 13 minutes, the second place is 3.5 hours. How have the devs not given a ♥♥♥♥ about this?  The art is alright, the music varies a lot. Some tunes were cool, some really weren't.  I loved the first game, I'm currently feeling like this one might not really be worth the money or my time.

Несколько раз пытался пройти, принял волевое решение забить. По сравнению с первой частью вторая больше в лор и меньше в драйв. Сууупер скучный геймплэй как-будто растянутый. То есть нужно по несколько раз бегать по одной и той же локации пытаясь понять на инстинктах чё делать. К тому же очень отвратительная интерактивная карта, ориентироваться по ней не удобно. Как бы объяснить претензии к карте, в первой части это была конкретно метроидвания с раздельными комнатами-кирпичиками, тут же карта в стиле цельной схемы, а вот переход между экранами остался прежним. По этой причине приходится постоянно открывать карту на каждом шагу чтоб не заблудиться.  Ни в коем случае не призываю игнорировать эту игру, но будьте готовы к скучному и растянутому сюжету в стиле записок на дороге как это происходит в Cave Story. Если вас это не пугает и есть желание пройти обе части, то лучше начните с этой, а уже потом первую, потому что если наоборот у вас может произойти такой же нюанс как и у меня.

No hay ningún tipo de unión entre las historias, no os preocupéis por eso, aparte del nombre de "la brecha" que para más inri aquí se refiere a otra cosa.

前作をプレイ済みで、セールで半額になっていた際に購入。  前作に続き、探索要素コンプリートにやり甲斐があるメトロイドヴァニア。 ただ他の方々が仰っているように戦闘バランスが序盤は厳しめな事、アイテムや特殊能力が揃ってない事が相まって、終盤になるまでは探索が大変でした。それに中盤ではジャンプからのアクションをギリギリのタイミングで要求される事もあり、ある意味そこが一番大変でした。  武器に関しては前作の方が良かったですね。 武器の種類や射程もなんですが、近接武器が明らかに格差あるので。1発あたりの威力と連射力の違いで4種類あっても、チャージ攻撃（あるアクセサリーが必要）の性能に格差がありすぎて１択になってしまうという。 中ボス戦も前作だと緊張感ある戦闘だったのですが、今作ではザコ戦のストレス解消になってます。こちらの攻撃を当てにくいザコと違って、中ボスは攻撃をガンガン当てられて楽しいのですが、コレはどうかなーと思ったり。  BGMは前作と違って遺跡や自然の中を探索するので民族音楽風がメインになっていて良い感じです。 また、前作をプレイしていると「これはAxiom Vergeの続編だ」と感じる要素が中盤以降に出てくるのもGOOD。 それに戦闘難易度もプレイ中に変更できるので、キツいと感じたら易しくする事も可能です。  オススメの部類には入りますが、アクションが苦手な人には向かないですね。探索の面白さに触れる前にやめてしまいそう。 前作の方が面白いと思います。

Buen metroidvania para pasar el rato. No lo considero una segunda parte de Axiom Verge ya que argumentalmente va a otra cosa, y hay una sola nota que haga referencia a Trace (excelente hilo conductor y protagonista del primer juego). Las zonas son un poco inconexas, ya que al no haber final bosses (hay enemigos grandes que solo hay que aporrearlos a base de bien pare vencerlos, nada de estrategias, y no dan un premio gordo que te haga mejorar como pasa en otros metroidvania) ni elementos que conecten las zonas de forma que quede todo integrado. Da la sensación de ir perdido de un lado al otro, a ver que nueva habilidad encontrar, y a seguir buscando. Las zonas B donde tienes que ir con la "araña" están muy bien. Si lo encuentras de oferta, hazte con él, te dará unas horas divertidas. Pero no marcará un hito como si hizo su primera parte, que a nivel argumental, gameplay y protagonista sigue siendo uno de los top 5 en cuanto a metroidvanias. Esta "segunda parte" tiene muchas y muy buenas cosas, pero le falta algo que cree una unidad, y destaque el juego.

It's a quality metroidvania. Not outstanding, but worth your time if you are a fan of the genre.  What you need to know: 1. Barely any connection to the 1st game, for good and ill. 2. Use your map, make reminders. 3. Not a lot of secret, breakable walls for a metroidvania. Instead, you tend to access hard to reach areas by entering "the breach" a lower res world that overlaps with the main world. You can jump back and forth (with some limits. These form the more satisfying parts of exploration for the game.  Some notes. 1. Fast Travel. Dear devs, thank you for having it. However, the slow animation that plays every time you reform from your nano-cloud when you fast travel is not welcome. For emphasis, please watch the animation when you reach the end of a sentence. I expect you will be insane or patch the issue before Ishmael gets on the Pequod. 2. Above, plus a slightly faster movement speed would go a long way making getting around more fun. 3. Some abilities feel half-baked. In that they get used a bit then are forgotten. This can be late game abilities that don't really play into backtracking, or early stuff like the blast that makes some blocks break or fall. That get's used for a while then completely forgotten about. Even the final pass through gates ability felt lack-luster. There really weren't that many gates or that much reason to use it to fly.  And at 13 hours it's pretty short. 100% would take longer but I'm not motivated to do so.

This game is pretty conflicting because there are many good aspects to it and quite a few disappointing aspects depending on what your personal preferences are. I have to give credit to is the developer deciding to go a different direction then the first game and instead have more of an emphasis on exploration which is definitely risky considering that many fans of the first game may feel somewhat alienated playing this one. Overall though I think that this game is very different and special in a lot of ways and is a very artistic interpretation of a exploration Metroid esque type of game with more in common with Zelda than Metroid for sure and as an experience is completely worth the asking price.  Coming from somebody who's played the vast majority of Metroidvanias that exist I can safely recommend this game even at full price for people who are looking for a somewhat more casual relaxed exploration experience with passable combat mainly melee focused, at least optimally.   I think the third game is going to be very critical when determining how people look back on the second game. I really hope that the creator can find a way to tie together both styles of gameplay to where it makes more cohesive sense from a trilogy perspective. I have great faith in Thomas Happ and look forward to seeing how he continues the story.

Awesome game! If you loved the original, well, you may or may not like this because it's completely different. The movement feels comparable and there are callbacks, but the similarities end there. What is similar, though, is the in-depth lore and worldbuilding which I can't wait to see expanded upon even further in future sequels, if/when those come. (Thomas Happ said he has ideas for 8 games total in the series, so I'm hoping!)  This game's best strengths are interesting exploration, abilities that feel fresh without being annoying to use, and great atmosphere and music. The exploration does some things that really set it apart which I won't spoil here. I enjoy the moment to moment combat a lot, too, though it's a bit of a rough start due to the enemies dealing a lot of damage, having tricky patterns, and your attack range being very small. This quickly becomes a very fair and enjoyable difficulty curve, however.  I feel that one of this game's biggest weaknesses are the way it handles boss fights. The boss fights are mostly easy and optional, and the few times a boss fight is required, you can't fail because it gives you a save point right in the middle of the fight, which I honestly think detracts from the satisfaction you get from beating them. It's ironic because I usually don't care about boss fights, but here I actually think this style hurt the pacing. In the end, though, that was a minor issue for me and I enjoyed the rest of the experience through and through.  I strongly recommend this if you're fine with optional boss fights, and you don't go into it expecting something like the original in any way.

I'm an old school gamer who remembers playing the original Metroid and Castlevania on my NES. I still love Metroidvanias, and I highly recommend Axiom Verge 1 & 2. I was blown away by their creativity, lore, and gameplay. While Axiom Verge 1 was a really interesting game, it had a few jagged edges in terms of gameplay that made it sometimes frustrating. AV2 smoothes out those jaggies. It was more fun for me, right from the beginning, and whenever I got stuck, I was able to figure out the next path on my own. The art style is a little different, and there is no gun arsenal like in the first one, but movement and exploration is much more refined. I highly recommend it!

It's okay but not as good as the first installment. The good graphics and music, and reasonably good gameplay are still there, and the story is somewhat more understandable. It's easier to list the bad items:  - Even though, numerically, the runtime is about the same as in Axiom Verge 1, the game feels overly short, like it ends just when you were really getting started. Something about the progression is not tuned right. - The weapons are very underwhelming, particularly the ranged ones. All throughout the game one feels like one has to contend with frustratingly limited "starter" weapons for a while, but in reality those "starters" are all you're ever going to get. - All of the bosses are a joke, impossible to take seriously.

[b]在两个世界反复横跳！[/b]  还是熟悉的画风，还是熟悉的味道，但是2代从封面就可以看出来，大部分场景都不像1代那样压抑猎奇，更多的户外自然风景，建筑雕像融入了苏美尔神话的元素，甚至2代还有活的npc！ 剧情上和1代几乎没有联系，只有某些地方存在着和1代相同的物体和贴图，让人不禁推测两部之间的潜在联系。 前期流程较为枯燥，获得的能力都是很常见的跑墙、扒墙等。没有了枪械，唯一的远程武器是回旋镖，剩下全是各种近战武器（登山镐、双面斧、镰刀、匕首，非常朴实无华）；和1代一样也可以远程控制小机器人行动，并且进入“隙界”，但和1代不一样的是，1代其只是隐藏的挑战房，2代则变成必经之地。 直到流程中期主角肉体被夺走后，一切才变得有意思起来。而细心的玩家此时会发现，这个所谓的“隙界”，并不是独立的空间，而是能和室外地图完全重合的“里世界”，这也是2代之前重点宣传的新玩法，两个世界切换。但并不是说随时随地都能切换，需要找到传送门（里世界获得某种新能力后就可以直接回表世界，表世界则没有这种能力），又因为2代没有1代逆天的穿墙能力，所以很多谜题就依靠表里世界反复横跳。 机器人形态还有机动性更高的能力，滑翔（一开始可以向上飞一点点高度）和钩爪（直接钩平台边缘而不1代那种折磨的荡绳子） 2代虽然也有骇入的能力，但并非1代那种谁都能改造，只能入侵机械（不过恰好表世界的敌人全都是机器人）或者开门之类的。 2代还加入了成长系统，主要收集物（除了文档和能力）就两种，技能点和加血量上限的，像攻击伤害、攻击速度等被动全都变成自己选择加点。 2代最舒服的一点就是后期会解锁传送能力，打开地图就能回保存点。 另外2代除了最终boss，剩下的全都可以不打直接绕开（打了给技能点）。 虽然2代给我的震撼远没有1代那么大，但是在表里世界穿梭的新玩法以及相关谜题也非常精彩。

+++ Wonderful sense of exploration and discovery. +++ Novel combination of abilities. +++ Lushly detailed artwork. +++ Compelling story and lore.  --- Disappointing boss fights.  This title can offer 15 hours of pure goodness where exploration trumps above all, including combat. Every little nook and cranny appears to have been given more than its fair share of effort, and this attention to detail contributes to the making of an experience that feels like reading a page-turner novel. It will not make waves among enthusiasts of challenging battles — however, it is an excellent choice for those looking to get pleasantly lost into another world.

Axiom Verge 2 does not play at all like the original.  Mechanically, it controls similarly, but the actual game play goals and loop are very different.  Where Axiom Verge felt more like a Metroid game with dark corridors, minimal graphics and a very dark combat oriented tone, Axiom Verge 2 instead is a bright, colourful game where exploration is much more important than combat.  The pixel art remains gorgeous and the music stellar, but the sometime punishing combat of the original is instead replaced with what is frankly very easy fights.  If you don't come in with the expectation of playing more of the original Axiom Verge, you may enjoy the game.

Part of what makes metroidvania fun is revisiting old places, more powerfully, to unlock greater things. It's not fun in this game, imo. World traversal is pretty meh, and you don't get fast travel until halfway through - and that doesn't make revisiting places more fun. You get an indication of "you need to be here" next - but every avenue you want to try to get there is met with frustration and failure. It's literally whack-a-mole trying to find the one specific spot that you can use to break through to make further progress on an old area. On top of that, the controls are pretty meh - you have to "tap" to use the [spoiler]grapple[/spoiler], not hold, and if you hold it doesn't work. You have to double tap to use [spoiler]hover[/spoiler] - it's like the dev thought "how does every other game's controls use this mechanic? Lemme do the opposite of that". Frustrating in so many ways.

Y ahora toca la review del segundo juego. He publicado hace nada la review del primer juego, el cual recomiendo completamente. Este también lo voy a recomendar aunque se queda un poco atrás respecto al primer título.  Básicamente es otro Axiom Verge, pero le han dado un ambiente a mundo Inca o cultura sudamericana antigua, que le queda estupendo al juego. No quiero hacer spoilers pero diré que, técnicamente no seguimos la historia del primer título y se podría jugar de manera independiente al primer juego. De hecho tengo la intención de irme a Youtube a mirar vídeos sobre teorías sobre estos dos juego y como se entrelazan. Pero volviendo a la reseña, decir que es un muy buen metroidvania; toca conseguir nuevos objetos para explorar nuevas zonas. Han tenido el detalle de señalarte en el mapa la localización de los próximos lugares a explorar, para sí seguir bien la historia, pero no os irá mal explorar el mundo cuando descubráis nuevas habilidades. Hablando de explorar, este juego tiene un problema con la parte visual de zonas que puedes pisar o simplemente lanzar tus objetos para golpear interruptores. Aquí ya no se tienen armas de fuego, sino que se tiene una arma blanca y un boomerang, sin más. Pero volviendo al tema de la exploración, a veces, es demasiado enrevesado encontrar el camino para avanzar. Casi parece que el escenario se haya convertido en un puzle y se me ha hecho muy complicado al puntio de necesitar mirar guias en Youtube. (también puede que yo sea malo explorando, pero llevo mucho tiempo jugando metroidvanias y nunca había tenido tantos problemas explorando, ni con metroid, ni con castlevania). Tengo una segunda queja: los jefes son todavía más fáciles que el primero juego y encima no parecen ser obligatorios de derrotar en la inmensa mayoría de los casos. Solo el jefe final hay que derrotarlo sí o sí... 'Más o menos' (no diré más). De nuevo, he muerto un puñado pequeño de veces, y de nuevo he muerto más por explorando que en los jefes. Eran escandalosamente fáciles.  Con todo, sigue siendo un juego muy recomendado, aunque la parte de exploración lo ha hecho más pesado de jugar a pesar de ser en comparación un juego más corto que el primero. He tardado casi lo mismo que el primer juego por que me he perdido muchísimo más por el escenario que en el primer juego. En cualquier caso, un juego muy recomendado si te gusta la ambientación que tiene.  A este juego le voy a dar un 6 sobre 10 de nota.

There are some really cool ideas and mechanics in this sequel, and it's great that the series gets a chance to grow beyond the Axiom Verge 1, which seemed to build a lot of its identity around Super Metroid in terms of gameplay. If Axiom Verge is to be its own franchise, I think this is important, but I have really mixed feelings about the execution. Calling this Axiom Verge 2 seems like a mistake. It should have been marketed as a spin-off or reimagining to separate itself from the original while still connecting lore as a spiritual prequel.    Here, you get something very different, more akin to a fusion of the first Axiom Verge game, Breath of the Wild (stealth elements), and to a weird degree classic NES sequels (a complaint I have seen elsewhere about this game in terms of how different it is from the first game). Progression is still metroidvania, but it feels really weird, in the sense that some items are just in plain site (e.g., the water mobility item is just found immediately with no effort at all traversing in the water beforehand). Nothing wrong with doing metroidvania differently, but its a number of moments like this that make the game feel lazily strung together in some ways. To enjoy the mechanics, you really need to experiment with items, and recognize that combat is often not the answer. Often, hacking (a cool mechanic) is the best way to move forward (which is probably one reason why others expecting more Axiom Verge find the combat janky), so there is some interesting choice-making when navigating through areas with enemies.  That being said, the combat clearly has flaws, feeling very hit or miss because of inconsistencies in difficulty progression. Random difficulty spikes occur, most frustatingly with the enemies that break out from floor tiles prior to the drone-only section of the game. The drone-only part will feel frustrating at first, but it becomes a test of your understanding of the mechanics you learned up until that point. That said, it still seems that the large reduction in health from specific enemies should have been taken into account better when designing the difficulty progression, it just seems all over the place. Late game becomes a pushover, which makes the experience feel pointless.   Items are pretty cool, and after familiarizing with the inventory and skills system, I don't really have complaints about them, besides the fact that some items don't contribute much to the progression. Some of them end up being very forgettable. This actually got me stuck looking around the map for a few hours picking up extra items before the next morning when I remembered the homing boomerang and a suspicious upward tunnel I could not climb. That speaks to the beauty of the genre more than anything.  What makes things particularly iffy is the "bosses". This game has a series of large enemies that serve as optional bosses, as in the case of breath of the wild, but they play out like bosses from Castlevania 2, in that they kind of just stand there and are either too OP or too easy depending on where you are in progression. There are only 2 main boss encounters otherwise, and the way they are done requires no skill whatsoever. Having infinite respawn in the fights makes them completely pointless, regardless of whether that is an element of the plot or not. The stakes feel minimal (despite the story actually having high stakes). I am not sure why this was chosen as the way to design these sections, but I have a feeling it has to do with the massive amount of health that is lost when taking damage from enemies, which would make boss fights miserable in the game's current state.  I like the main map a lot, and I think the breach as a "B side" is cool, especially since they make an effort to distinguish the environments from how they are the main map (unlike Castlevania SOTN and Harmony of Dissonance alternative castles, and other instances of the alternative dimension trope in many metroidvanias). It doesn't end up being too confusing here either. That said, the execution still feels iffy. It just doesn't feel like a cohesive universe, but rather disjointed. It doesn't help that music genres are all over the place in terms of volume and style, and rather tone-deaf to the heavy subject matter of the story when you enter many areas of the breach. Some of the vocals also seem way too loud compared to the rest of the music, but maybe that is sound system dependent. Even though that took me out of the experience, there are a few tracks I loved from the OST, particularly the base camp area below the glacier, the first climb on the mountain (breach version is a little obnoxious though), and underwater breach version.   What makes this sad is that the core story idea was really interesting, but the endings come across as rushed and uninteresting. I remember seeing this game release to immediate backlash, followed by a more recent surge of people defending the game without much constructive criticism. I think the backlash is fair as a response to what seemed to be promised as more Axiom Verge. That's what happens when you put a 2 in your title. That said, I think a real identity for this franchise is starting to shape here beyond Metroid-inspired gameplay, and I really hope the ideas excel in future Axiom Verge titles!

Axiom Verge 2 does not get as much love as Axiom Verge, for a variety of reasons, but the game does not deserve the hate it gets from some of the other reviewers. It is quite good and worth playing.  First off, as everyone points out: this game is very different from its predecessor. If you only want to play a continuation of the first game, you will be disappointed. But by not following in the direct footsteps of its parent, the developer has made a much tighter game with better pacing and plot, while at the same time making the world more open. The world is easier to navigate and feels more lived-in due to the more detailed backgrounds and the interactions with other characters. Additionally, you can play AV2 without having played the first game (all the allusions to the first game are basically Easter eggs).  The game is very exploration-heavy, moreso than AV1. The world is both easier and harder to navigate: it's easier to get around, but there're more nooks and crannies to search. Combat has been replaced from run-and-gun to a more leisurely hack-and-slash style. *I* think this is mostly an improvement, because the multitude of weapons in AV1 was ridiculous, but YMMV based on preference. The boss battles are also both better and worse: there are more minibosses, but fewer big baddies. This is a consequence of this game being much more open-ended than the first. If Axiom Verge plays primarily like classic NES Metroid, then Axiom Verge 2 plays primarily like an 8-bit Hollow Knight.  Axiom Verge is well worth playing for Metroidvania fans, even if you haven't played the first game. If you like your metroidvanias to mostly be an adventure game with occasional boss battles, you will enjoy this. But, if you are a die-hard fan of the first game, you may be disappointed.

Many consider Axiom Verge 2 inferior to the original. After playing both games back to back, I strongly disagree. AV2 is a big improvement over the original in many areas: - Very satisfying exploration. Original AV had many obscure hidden areas which you'd never find without a walkthrough. AV2's exploration feels much more logical, relying on observation and reasoning. Fast travel system makes the experience far less tiring compared to AV1. - Art style. Original AV had a distinct 8-bit style which was minimalist to the point of being jarring. AV2 upgrades it to 16-bit. The sprites and environments are more detailed. Color palettes masterfully use subtle colors. The cyber-Sumerian theme is awesome. - Music. Just like the art style, the music is much more lush and organic. The inclusion of traditional instrumentation fits the Sumerian theme well. 8-bit style music featured in breach is less memorable though. - Combat is a downgrade from AV1, true. Gone are the varied ranged weapons, you're basically stuck with a melee attack and the boomerang for the entire game. However, I feel that criticizing combat too heavily is unfair since it is not the game's main focus. - Boss fights. Another often criticized area. Bosses are optional and are sometimes too easy. But I must remind you that AV1's late-game bosses were a mindless button-mashing experience without any strategy involved. And if you played on hard and did not find certain weapons, you could even softlock yourself. With that in mind, skippable or easy bosses are actually a huge improvement.  Bottom line: must-play if you enjoy exploration and willing to overlook simplistic combat.

Coming staright from Axiom Verge 1, at first this game felt a lot different, but the core gameplay is still the same.  A lot of things improved overall. There is less weapon variety, but that makes getting each of them more important, and they feel more unique as well. Most of the abilities felt amazing to use. Secrets were a bit easier to find in my opinion, but that might be just experience from the first game.  The map improvemet and the ability to fast travel made the experience way less daunting and much more enjoyable. The lore and story are very well written out, and there were lots of references to the previous game.  What I missed were the bossfights. You can skip most of the bosses, and a lot of them feel more like minibosses compared to the ones in Axiom Verge 1. They were way too easy and not that imaginative.  Accessibility settings are a cool addition, but I missed a set difficulty option that you can choose from at the start.  Overall a really solid addition to the series, would highly recommend.
Axiom Verge 2 improve upon every aspect of the original. The traversal abilities are good fun and the two-map system is quite neat to explore. A must-play for Metroidvania fans.

Unwieldy, in short.  My previous review was a bit harsh, so let me try again.  The good: - Interesting visuals. - A lot of very cool ideas, like hacking and the breach. - Exploration and platforming is fun. - Odd weapons require a bit of getting used to, but once you get upgrades you steamroll everything, in typical metroidvania fashion.  The bad: - The odd choice of weapons create unfair situations often. - No real boss fights required to progress, all of them are optional. Some scenes could be considered bossfights, but they're really just glorified cutscenes and you can't die. - The breach gets quite annoying after entering it for the 100th time, my ears are bleeding. - Biomes aren't nearly as interesting as they were in axiom verge 1. - The story is confusing, but the first half was great. - The ending is disappointing.  I'd say a 7/10, purely because platforming and exploration is the entire game, and it's the most fun part. Also I might have missed some story related cues, or I just need to replay Axiom Verge 1 to understand it better.

i loved it. a lot of people say the first game is better. and, while i do agree, i feel like this game might be overlooked because of that. it shouldn't be. this game is VERY MUCH worth playing. it's story is a lot less confusing as well.  another difference is the first game focused heavily on ranged weapons. this game has one or two ranged weapons, but it's focus is mostly on melee weapons. which means you're going to have to get a lot closer to your enemies. so, it's a good thing that the game's movement is very fluid. it feels so buttery smooth.  another big difference is this game's greater reliance on the controllable drone. you'll face some bosses with it this time. and surprisingly, it feels pretty good. you only get one main attack with the drone, but it's pretty versatile in how you can use it. especially with some upgrades.  there's even puzzles to solve here. not that the first game didn't have them, but here, they're a part of progression. not just stuff you do for hidden items. though there's plenty of that too.  there's a lot to love about this game, and i'm glad i gave it a play instead of continuing to ignore it due to hearing people complain about how it's not as good as the first game. i do still prefer the first game. but this one is a worthy sequel, and i still love it. honestly, the main reason is the atmosphere. the first game is a lot creepier and twisted, the music fits that tone too. the music in this game fits as well. but i'm not as big a fan of it. i love that dark creepy tone. it's what hooked me on the original metroid, and it's what i wanted from the first game because it sought to recreate that feeling. and it did. the atmosphere of this game reminds me more of Zelda II the adventure of link. there's even a hidden powerup for one of the weapons that makes you fire beams from the dagger weapon, just like link in zelda 2.  the TL;DR though, if you liked the first game, you'll probably like this one too. maybe you'll like it even more. maybe not. but it's worth playing. it's just, good.

I was unsure what to expect from the sequel before its release, but I ended up enjoying it immensely. It made for an especially fun game on the Steam Deck.  The first game was essentially a 2D Metroid game with all the H.R. Giger-inspired flair cranked up to eleven, yet it still had enough cool, unorthodox ideas of its own to make it feel original.  The sequel retains some of that inspiration, but adopts a much brighter art style, focuses on melee combat instead of shooting, and features more ancient ruins and Earth-like outdoor environments.  The world map feels wonderfully varied, with each area boasting its own distinct mood, music, and atmosphere. There are usually multiple ways to get from point A to point B. All the areas are connected in a very organic way, and you unlock some pretty fun and creative traversal mechanics.  Optional bosses are a new addition that I think could have been executed better. While their visual designs are good, they sort of just feel like regular enemies with extra health and larger hitboxes. Additionally, the lack of unique boss music fails to give them the fanfare they deserve.  It is somewhat hard to pinpoint how it compares to its predecessor. While I prefer the shooting mechanics, plot, and darker tone of the first game, I believe the sequel made significant improvements in terms of exploration and how you move around the world.  Positives: + Varied and organic world map, with each area having its own distinct mood + Fun, creative power-ups and traversal mechanics  Negatives: - The optional bosses felt somewhat underwhelming

A superb sequel, everything about this game improves and builds on the original. The world is much larger, the movement and combat are refined and the "boss" fights while optional are better designed. However, it is different, while the first game was purely Metroid this game adds a lot of Castlevania influence to the mix, there is a focus on melee combat and the world is a lot more open, this allows it to experiment with new mechanics and expand the gameplay.

Axiom Verge 2 is NOT Axiom Verge 1.  It's in many ways a very different way that takes some bold swings.  Some hit, others miss.  For one, I didn't like combat very much in this game, and I thought the bosses in this game being optional was kinda an odd choice.  I spared them all [spoiler] thinking there'd be a secret ending or something.  There was not.  There was an achievement, but that's not as cool. [/spoiler]. I do think the exploration in this game is actually really cool.  I won't give anything away, but there's actually a lot to explore here and the mechanics are all in place to make traversal pretty fun.  On another note, this game took a while to really sink its hooks into me.  I don't know why, but this game didn't really grab me until 3 hours in.  However, once it did, it didn't let go.  Overall, Axiom Verge 2 is a fun game although it does make for a game I enjoyed less than the first.  I am glad to see that Axiom Verge as a franchise continues to evolve.  I think that'll make for a more diverse series.  8/10

The game isn't horrible, but it is a disappointing sequel to Axiom Verge.  One can't help but wonder if some DEI was involved in the making, considering the white young scientist was replaced with a black female scientist. Later the first npc you run into, is a woman who makes sure to not fail the bechdel test. The scientific alien technologies have also been replaced with folklore of indigenous people ground to powder and hidden in bottles.  DEI aside, those changes do not really impact the game. What does impact the game is the changes from using space weapons to using pickaxes. So instead of being able to play safely shooting enemies at a distance, you have run through shooting enemies to whack them in melee range, making the game much harder. Alternatively using the new mechanic of hacking robots, which also requires running near melee range and capturing them as friends or trying to disable them.  The minimap has also been updated to show exactly how each room looks like, which adds jitter and makes it harder to tell what is ground, what is background, what is platforms, and so on. On the bright side it does give you a hint of where the grounds and walls are, that may help you, even if it is a bit unclear. A middle version with just walls would have been superior, instead of the far too detailed background inclusion.  For some unknown reason, the resolution has also been dropped from the previous game. With the breach sections seemingly dropping the resolution even more, making it feel almost like you are playing a game made to fit on a calculator screen. Potentially they want to make it look like you are following the drone in the breach on a screen, rather than seeing it in reality. But that just feels like an unnecessary effect, when the game could just remain at the same graphic resolution the whole time.  The bosses guarding important items seem to also have been removed, and replaced with some kind of room roaming large monsters. Which are now optional to fight, but you get skill points if you defeat them.  Speaking of skill points, this mechanic is also not a good choice compared to the Axiom Verge design of giving you health when the game wants to give you health and giving you power when the game wants to give you power. Now that choice is instead in your hands, meaning you have to guess what the developers think would be best. Are the enemies going to do more damage in the next area so you need more health? Are they going to be higher level so you need to upgrade your hacks? Are they going to have more health so you need more damage or attackspeed? Game doesn't help you at all, and you get to make that choice yourself. Probably nice for speed running and your second playthrough or so, but for a first playthrough it is a poor design.  Overall the game is not bad. But it feels so far from Axiom Verge, that it is hard to see why they bothered to use the sequel name. Maybe they thought nobody would be picking up the game unless they tried to ride on the former fame. But far from a metroid game, this feels a lot more like an open world castlevania game. It is more unforgiving, more frustrating, more confusing, and I'm not sure if that is really what people wanted in a sequel.

A divisive follow up to the beloved Axiom Verge that I enjoyed. It's been a long time since I played the original, but I remember liking the world quite a lot. In the sequel, there's more story and... that's good and bad. The babylonian future tech stuff is excellent - nanobots, clones, AI powered by ancient ghosts. The actual plot and how it connects to the original, I couldn't save.  Gameplay-wise, this is all about exploration. There's a lot of abilites that revolve around the Breach, an alternative map that mirrors the main map. Cool stuff.  Bosses were weak. All of them you can just walk past. I don't get this. Either make they required or don't have them in at all. They were so uninteresting that they were basically just giant enemies.  Overall, a fun game that diverges from its predecessor in some interesting ways. A fantastic metroidvania if you like pure exploration.

If you enjoyed the first one, I don't know if I'd consider this better, but with a sale on the bundle it's more love letters to Castlevania to play through, so go ahead if you want.   Axiom Verge 1 was very much on the 'Metroid' angle of the term 'metroidvania'. Axiom Verge 2 is certainly on the '-vania' part. A lot of the gameplay is based on exploration rather than combat, with boss encounters amounting to there being an enemy in a room you can either avoid or kill outright.  Comparing the two, Axiom Verge 2 feels slower. The teleport animation is about 10 seconds long, so if you're hopping around frequently get ready to watch the same swirling graphic over and over. The unique exploration gimmick is very interesting, and leads to a satisfying amount of puzzles you can solve with some thinking.  I don't know how they did it, but 2's story is infinitely more confusing than 1's. It's supposed to be in the same universe, but the amount of terminology and concepts thrown at you are really strange. Couple that with the flat ending and I don't really know if I could say this game is above average compared to the first, but I got it in a bundle with the first which is a really good game, so I'd say it's worth it.

This game is a fantastic metroidvania.  Although it maintains the side-scrolling format of its predecessor, it offers a distinctly different experience in terms of atmosphere, gameplay, and mechanics. The visuals are bright and the environments are richly detailed, moving away from the traditional Metroid mood.  The game emphasizes melee combat with heavier, more substantial jumps compared to the first installment. The drone plays a significant role, and there's a skill system allowing you to level up various aspects of your character. With a vast map full of items and secrets to uncover, exploration is a major highlight.  It also finally has fast-travel :)  However, the game is quite easy; bosses, including the final boss, are straightforward to defeat. The most challenging part is how to get to where you need to go; I did get stuck near the end for a while trying to figure out where to go next.

I think Axiom Verge 2 is just as great as Axiom Verge 1. It is also a VERY different game from the first Axiom Verge both in themes and gameplay.   Axiom Verge 1 takes heavy inspiration from Metroid with feelings of loneliness and being in a very alien world with a biomechanical setting. AV1 also focuses more on ranged combat with different types of bullets.  AV2's aesthetic is much more bright, with most of the map taking place outdoors in more Earthlike settings (snowy mountains, desert plains, etc.) and a Mesopotamian theme. The theme is more about creation and life, compared to the dead world of AV1. I think the music is great but is way more energetic and airy with a middle eastern/indian flair. The gameplay itself focus way more on melee combat with your main ranged option being a slower boomerang compared to a gun.   The story itself to me was just a good as AV1's story, just as heavy and leaves you pondering and trying to connect the dots with the first game. I thought the map layout was also pretty good, with a fun swapping to different world maps mechanic to get around obstacles and find secrets.   I don't believe the game is very hard, but AV2 also has some accessibility options like lowering the damage you take or increasing the damage you do if you find yourself struggling.  If you go into Axiom Verge 2 hoping it will be exactly like the first you may be disappointed, but to me both games stand among the great metroidvanias and I look forward to the next one.

[h3] Overview [/h3]  Axiom Verge 2 (AV2) is a side-scrolling Metroidvania with a focus on exploration, platforming, and little puzzle solving. While AV2 is a stand alone sequel and it is not necessary to play or understand the story of the first game in order to enjoy AV2. There are some references to concepts and characters mentioned in the first game, but it is not necessary to understand AV2's story. If you enjoy Metroidvanias, AV2 is a solid game that is worth your time as the platforming abilities, map, and exploration are all quite good.   [h3] How Does Axiom Verge 2 Compare to the Original? [/h3]  The original AV was a straight Metroid-like with really none of the "-Vania" elements of a Metroidvania. AV2 incorporates more of those Vania elements with a greater focus on backtracking, exploration, and includes a skill point/skill tree system to level up your character. The focus on AV2 is more on puzzle solving to explore more of the map, where as AV1 was more focused on combat. Both AV games have a pretty light story that is mostly told through journals found throughout the world. I didn't find myself too invested in either game's story, but I will say that I do find the story of AV2 to be more enjoyable and a little easier to piece together.    [h3] The Map [/h3]  The world in AV2 feels much more sprawling that AV1, which I enjoyed. The level/world design is good, as well as the enemy placement being pretty solid as well. After you get the ability to call a drone, you get access to an additional world where you can only enter with your drone. This is often required in order to get access to other areas in the main world, but the "drone world" is a fully fleshed world as well, but with a more low-res visual style. As the world is more sprawled out, the game added the ability to fast travel at save points - which is something I feel AV1 could have benefited from having some limited form of fast travel. However, you can only fast travel only between points in the world you are currently in, unfortunately. This means if you are in the "main" overworld, you will have to enter the "drone world" first before you can fast travel to specific point in the drone world. I don't think there is anything that would have broken the game by making able to fast travel between points in the different worlds, so it became a little tedious to have to find an access point to swap between worlds before fast traveling.   [h3] Combat [/h3]  In the first game, there was about a dozen or so guns to choose from, where as in AV2 you have a melee attack and a ranged attack that you will upgrade by finding stronger versions of those weapons (there are a few different melee weapons you can choose from, but it was a hassle to swap between them so I just stuck with the one that had the longest reach).  In AV1, you would spend most of your time in your human form for combat and use your drone just for certain platforming/exploration sections (or occasionally scouting). I would say I spent 80-90% of my time in human form in AV1. In AV2, there is a much stronger emphasis on swapping between human and drone form - both for combat, exploration, and platforming as each form as an exclusive set of ways to attack and platform. This adds plenty of options in order to explore the world and tackle obstacles. Though towards the end of the game, I feel the drone's later platforming abilities were superior to the human form, and found myself being in drone form for almost all of the back half or so of the game.   [h3] Hacking [/h3]  One of my favorite mechanics in AV1 was the glitch/hack mechanic where you could glitch out enemies and certain terrain. In AV2, instead of having a gun that shoots out this effect, now you hold a button to expand a circular area in order to hack an enemy or object within range. Depending on what enemy or object your are hacking it can have a number of effects:  *Make an enemy become friendly. *Disable an enemy's weapon. *Slow down the enemy's speed. *Make the enemy emit healing drops. *Turn the enemy into a platform. *Overheat the  enemy to cause massive damage. *Certain doors will require hacking to open.  Some things can be hacked for free (such as opening barriers), but most "offensive" hacks against enemies use up you nanotech bar, which acts as "mana" to use these hacks. This bar can be expanded with skill points found in the world and dropped by bosses. Hacking is particularly useful against bosses as they will often have multiple components/weapons that can be hacked. This mechanic added an interesting wrinkle to combat.   [h3] Summary [/h3]  I beat both AV games without using a guide and found the game did a good job of guiding and teaching the player through the game's mechanics/combat/puzzles without ever feeling lost, stuck, or frustrated. The 2nd game has a greater openness with more branching paths/routes your can go down to explore the world in different ways, where the first game felt a little more straight forward. To help reduce some confusion with the openness, AV2 will put markers on the map to show the player where to go to in order to progress the story.  The first AV took me about 13.5 hours to beat with about 84% of the items found and 95% of the map explored. AV2 took me about 12.5 hours to beat with 94% of the items found and 99% of the map explored.  As I prefer the the "vania" elements of Metroidvanias instead of the pure Metroid-like gameplay of AV1, I found myself enjoying AV2 more than the original game. If you weren't a fan of the first game as it was a Metroid-like and enjoyed other games of the Metroidvania genre, then I would recommend AV2 even if you didn't like AV1 because it is quite a different game.

TL;DR: This is a really creative and interesting metroidvania. You're likely to get stuck, Get a guide and don't feel bad about it if you do. Otherwise this is worth it  Axiom Verge 1 had unique and interesting abilities, along side a distinct retro style, and the second game in the series keeps that innovative spirit up. It's 'navigation challenges are also unique, and I found to be very fun.  The routing in this game is very circuitous and challenging. But it's also a very linear metroidvania. To take circuitous routing in a linear metroidvania means the author had to put up tons of impassable barriers and blockers. The world feels bizarrely locked down. Get used to running into a ping barrier with a switch on the other side. You will be seeing that a lot.   In my playthrough I got stuck twice and needed to look up what to do to continue. In both cases, I failed to notice a hint the map was giving me. But when I did fail to notice it, I ended up concluding that "there must be another way around or another ability I need" and then explored every nook and cranny of the available map, looking for some clue that was staring me in the face right at the start.   I think the problem here is this: Very often in metroidvanias they will tease you with area's you can't currently get to due to a lack of an ability or some kind of key, and the solution is to find some other route around. Axiom Verge 2 compounds on this challenge in a a few ways:   1. The unique traversal mechanics and abilities mean you really can't predict what you need for a given barrier,  2. AV2 does not make it very obvious that an ability is needed for a given blocked. eg. a 2 x 3 square block in the terrain. 3. The complex navigational challenges in this game leave you wondering if there isn't some sneaky way to get the thing you're after with the abilities you currently have. 4. AV2 has lots of different types of keys and locks, and instead of using them it will use switches and pink barriers.   I suspect these all these facts will make getting stuck in this game pretty likely...  One last negative. The game's a bit buggy. I experienced two hard crashes, and two softlocks. I never lost more than a minute of progress to these, but it was annoying.  OK. despite all those negatives, I still recommend this game. Damn fine metroidvania. If you enjoy this genera you should check this one out, it's a creative entry to the category and worth having a look for yourself.

Where it's predecessor was on the Metroid end of the spectrum, [i]Axiom Verge 2[/i] is very much on the Castlevania side of things. If there was a "middle thumb" this would get it.  It's not 'bad' but it's also forgetable. If they meant for the game to feel like I should be running from just about everything, then that fits, but otherwise I just feel underpowered after several hours of game play. The pixel grfx are 'fine', their art is nice but to me it just kinda looks like noise after a bit. Finally I think the biggest downside is the map. While not a Hollow Knight-level Metroidvania game, Axiom Verge 2 certainly is a noteworthy one. The story is one I wouldn't mind seeing adapted to the silver screen. The controls are easy to learn, given the rebinding feature within the game's options menu, granting you access to your favorite keyboard setup. The items given to you are an upgrade from the first Axiom Verge, which is not a low bar by any means, though I do wish there was a long-range weapon for some of the enemies. My biggest complaint would be the vastness of the map. It feels rather grandiose, and there were times where I found myself lost, merely to stumble upon the correct way, which I could have done all along if I had known the path was there. However, once I did find it, I found myself enjoying the new areas.  All in all, I would give this game a solid 7/10 stars. A good game, but not quite great. I would recommend this game to anyone who enjoys the Metroidvania genre, lore-heavy story, and/or a good, solid 8-bit adventure.  Looking forward to Axiom Verge 3, should there be one.

I loved the game. Yes, it's different than the first one, but no less of an evolution of the games it was based on. Like others said, the first was more Metroid (combat and backtracking) while this one was more Zelda 2 and Castlevania (more exploring and less combat). Most modern "metroidvanias" lean heavily into the Metroid style, so I can understand why some people would be disappointed if they didn't play a lot of the older games.  I think the negative reviews have some valid points, but I think many of them are overly dramatic and critical and might turn people away who would otherwise enjoy the game (like me). Here are my comments:  • This game is not very hard; it's just very open and non-linear, allowing you to encounter areas you're not ready for yet. It's more Zelda 2 than Metroid. This is one of the things I actually like about the game, unlike most modern metroidvanias that lock off the entire world until you get the one magic upgrade that allows you to progress to the next closed off area. In Axiom Verge 2, you can explore most of the world, while the ugprades generally allow you to progress into areas of the game that move the story forward. I think this is a really underappreciated aspect of the game's design, and I wish more games did it. • If it is too hard in places, there are Accessibility settings that you can turn on and off at any time, to do things like crank your damage way up, or the damage the enemies do way down, or slow the game down. I wish more games did this (Rogue Legends 2, Hades, and this game are great for it.) My seven year old managed to beat most of this game because I set it 70% enemy damage. If he can do it, you can do it. • The game is also very generous with upgrade points, there's practically one or two in every room, so you can always explore a little to upgrade health or damage if you're having trouble. I had a lot of points left over at the end of the game, so it's not like you have to grind for them. • Despite its openness, I rarely was unsure what to do next (I had to look up how to get the last story upgrade though). I'm the kind of softcore gamer that likes quest icons and pathfinding, and I think this game had plenty. Any time you progress the story, it adds a new icon to the map so you know where to go next if you want to move the story forward. Almost every room has secrets or things in it that you can find through the game, and the map design is constantly steering you back towards revisiting places you've been before by unlocking shortcuts, but it never felt like "backtracking". There's also plenty of fast travel points, so you can move quickly around the world. • Melee is fine, and early in the game, you get upgrades for strong ranged charge attacks. Like the negative reviews say, there are a few enemies that are simply impossible to avoid taking damage from, but they're only in 3-5 rooms out of the entire huge game. It's 30 seconds of frustration out of an entire 10 hours of fun. If it bothers you that much, you can temporarily turn enemy damage down. The entire rest of the game melee is adequate; combat is not the focus. • I played on Steam Deck, which was great. No other technical issues, except for one crash. There's plenty of save points, so it was not a big deal. • My one minor complaint with this game (even more so than the first one) is that the creator came up with this absolutely brilliant and captivating scifi world and philosophical story about the nature of reality, time, and consciousness, but then makes it so subtle and hard to piece together that I expect most people will not get it. It also ties in very heavily to the first game's story, which I hadn't played in years, so that made it harder to track. I only understand the plot because I dug through reddit for timelines and supplementary material that others pieced together on Reddit. I'm not a big story person in games generally, but I love the concepts so much and would even read novels set in this world (Peter Watts could do it justice). I just wish the game itself did a better job of exploring it because it's so unique and fascinating.  Overall, the price is worth it for 10 hours of compelling gameplay, and I loved it enough to 100% the items and map. If you're looking for another Metroid game, you might be mixed on it. If you were fond of old school Zelda 2, Castlevania 2+, or other games like that, I highly recommend it.

After playing the original Axiom Verge, I was hesitant to tackle it's divisive sequel. Rather than continuing the story of Dr. Trace in his search of answers concerning the alien world of Sudra, Axiom Verge 2 barely has anything to do with the storyline of the first game, except for a few references here and there to characters and concepts.   This time you take on the role of a millionaire technology mogul named Indra who is investigating the mass disappearance  of a team of scientists in Antarctica. Her involvement in the mystery is personal because she received an almost threatening message informing her that her estranged daughter was at the facility when the crew went missing. Shortly after arriving, she's pulled through an interdimisional portal and thrust into an alternate world that resembles ancient Mesopotamia with robots. The original inhabitants are long dead after a war, but their kill-bots are still active. To survive, Indra merges with Arms, the civlization's sentient super-technology, which also doubles as the power-ups and traversal abilities.   Unfortunately, this also drags Indra into the conflict between the Lamassu, the god-computer controlling the kill-bots, and the Arms. As remnant of the last war, Lamassu believes they should be destroyed and the Arms, being self-aware entities that were once people before super-science made them something else, want to continue living. The running battle between factions will threaten the multiverse.   It's not just the plot that's different. The look, feel, and combat goes in a completely separate direction. The first Axiom Verge felt like the original metroid game. It was set in a truly alien environment where nothing looked familiar. Axiom Verge 2 feels sort of like being an American in a foreign country. There's grass and trees and mountains but the architecture looks kind of funny.   This is also reflected in the musical score. Those upbeat chiptunes from the first game only make an appearance when you are piloting a drone to explore the Breach, an alternate reality that functions as a second map and gets Indra to places she can't reach under normal circumstances. When she explores the 'normal' world, the music for each zone is very similar to that one track from the first game for the Kur zone. A vaguely middle eastern rhythm accompanied by a synthesized voice doing this ethereal chanting. I was unsure how I'd react to the music in this game since that was my least favorite track from the original. I found the computer voice grating. Here the voice is provided by an actual person and her vocals lend the soundtrack a mysterious, almost mystical quality.   The original game's combat was all about those super-science guns. By the time credits rolled, I had about nine guns and that wasn't even close to being everything I could have found. The sequel feels like combat is entirely optional. Indra starts with a pick-axe for a melee weapon and later gets a boomerang for a ranged option. After a hour or so, she'll get the ability to hack enemies and turn them into friendlies. Theoretically, you could avoid combat altogether and do a pacifist run. The game doesn't even have traditional bosses. It has boss-like enemies you may encounter, you don't even have to engage with them as they can be skirted around. The only thing you are required fight is the final boss. As someone who struggled with the late-game bosses in the first game, I appreciated this approach in practice far more than I thought I would.   So we've established these are very different games. When I saw the reviews for this game, they honestly sounded like every change was going to be for the worse. But after playing it, hands down, Axiom Verge 2 is the superior entry to the series, and if there is an Axiom Verge 3 I hope it follows the conventions of this game as opposed to its predecessor.   The first game was very retro in the sense that it could be frustratingly obtuse and would have you combing the map looking for what to do next. Axiom Verge 2 is more streamlined in that it gives you quest markers to keep you on task. Don't think that this feature dilutes the exploration element. If you make a beeline for the first objective, you will find yourself blocked and will have to make a detour into some underwater ruins for a new ability. You might have to go off the beaten-path to get to the objective, but you always know where you are ultimately headed. There was only one time in the game where I felt like I was truly stuck, and that was only because I overlooked a platform on the corner of the screen even though I should not have.   And this game has fast travel. Hallelujah. You can warp back to any save point at any time. All you have to do is open the map.

This is a review from someone who DIDN'T like AV1, since this is a rather rare occurence, thinking someone might need said input.   A lot of people in these reviews who liked the first don't like this, and I understand why - it's a completely different experience. I think making it its own title would have been better. It plays completely different. As someone who didn't like the first I consider it a good thing. This is an exploration-first title, in fact there aren't really any boss fights, it's all really exploration. I mean there's combat, but this isn't a combat-MV like HK, Ender Lillies or Blasphemous. And I approve of focusing on what the game is about, which is exploration. And it does so very well. This is honestly one of my favourite MVs.

This one is good but not as good as the 1st. My main complaint is that the melee focused combat can feel annoying due to the types of enemies you'll fight but I loved being able to hack the enemies, story, and figuring out puzzles by going back and forth through the breach.

I really hate the combat.  The lack of ranged options isn't the problem.  The problem is the enemy mechanics are NOT balanced around this fact.  Generally, enemies move quickly and don't have any kind of openings after they attack so you either have to attack from a direction they can't hit back or just tank hits and button mash to kill them as fast as possible.  The majority of enemies have frequent ranged attacks as well so you can't really approach safely for melee, which means you're either running away from such enemies or doing the brute force method I mentioned above.  Then there's enemies hiding in blocks, blocks which are IDENTICAL to normal blocks!  Like silverfish, except they immediately hit you because they only pop out when you're right next to the little bastards.  Cheap damage!  There are other enemies which will swoop in from offscreen and hit you unless you know it's going to happen and are able to hit them as they rush in.  I've fought a few minibosses and one of them just starts spamming missiles once it's at low health and there's no way to get close without taking damage.  Just awful, complete unfun garbage.  Thankfully you do get enough health upgrades after the first hour or so that you won't die too much despite that. So I don't think the dev really put much effort into making the combat fun.  Maybe even the opposite, some enemies seem designed to be as frustrating as possible.  Combat complaints over.  Unfortunately I must also complain about the music.  MOST of the tracks are fine, good even.  Others are ill-fitting; like, why does it sound like mysterious hindu music in areas that are just normal wilderness environments?  And then one of the music tracks feels like the singer is howling into my ear.  No thanks!  Oh, and the plot sucks.  At first it's fine, you show up and things immediately get weird, but the plot hasn't really done anything within the past 4 hours I've played.  I keep going to new locations and being told to go to a different one with no reason given.  It's like the developer had ideas for the start of the plot and the end but nothing for the middle!  I do like the map.  Being able to see the shape of the environment clearly instead of a grid of rooms is very nice.  Then again I don't like that there's only one type of map marker you can place, and backtracking to use new powerups is annoying because there's so much ground to cover and the compass you get points at things you can't reach all the time so it's near-useless for the intended progression path.  The graphics are fine, but you can see that from the trailer, no new information here.

Hará unos años desde que me terminé Axiom Verge que me gustó la jugabilidad y la historia. Pero años atrás supe sobre la secuela de Axiom verge 2 pero esta vez en otro mundo (todavía tengo el deja vu de la brecha del primer Axiom Verge) y esperaba que fuera disponible por steam. Ahora justo me compré Metroidvania de Humble Bundle por Axiom Verge 2 que esperaba terminarmelo y justo lo terminé. Mi opinión sobre el juego, ya que sabia que sería diferente la jugabilidad a diferencia del axiom verge que usaba mas las armas de scifi, pero axiom verge 2 es más bien como RPG con armas cortas, igual me acostumbraba asi, se sentía como Zelda 2 de NES, además siendo metroidvania, la busqueda de otras habilidades y mejoras es muy bien hecho. Despite all the warnings and cautioning, I absolutely LOVED this game. Beat it a few minutes ago with 98% map and all weapons and all arms. In some ways I love it even more than the first one.  The world is a lot more open ended where the first felt a bit constrained. I LOVE how the huge bosses are entirely optional which to me gives a lot more of a realistic vibe to it. Also, there was that huge worm thing in the water that I really felt bad putting down, it's just so cute.  I didn't really like the protagonist though and the whole narrative seemed a bit jumbled, though it was interesting and seemed like [spoiler] a prequel to Axiom Verge. Proto-Xedur variants and all that, even... [/spoiler] but no, no spoilers. The world building was impeccable and the overlay of Kiengir, the Breach and the Overworld and how they interact and how you explore all three was again genius.  The visuals I really like, especially how varied the Breach is and how mind-bending the Overworld is.  I also loved how combat was de-emphasized and how I managed to rely on the hacking mechanism a whole lot more. This made exploration so much more enjoyable to me with creative items that make the traversal really interesting rather than just another big gun whoopdy-f-doo. I didn't even mind that the little combat that remained was primarily made melee. I didn't use it all that much anyway.  Finally, the music. The music is extremely amazing this time around as well and even better than the first game. Just take a listen to the track titled Monsoon with a good headset or sound system.  All in all this is a very solid 9/10 for me and I highly recommend it to whoever like the first one.

One of the best metroidvanias out there, focusing primarily on stellar and unique upgrades instead of combat perks. It is slightly behind AV1 imo, but its still amazing regardless, and easily the second best indie metroidvania I played.

Axiom Verge 2 in many ways is a direct continuation from the original, and should be played right after for the best experience. Despite this, it seems like a conscience effort has been made to innovate and change up the core mechanics - so different move systems, weapon systems and upgrade system. The ability to fast travel makes going back for upgrades considerably more enjoyable that the first one. What I would say is that while there are improvements such as this, the combat feels a little less tight than the original - and the ending surprised me with it's abruptness. Still well worth playing, but treat is as different than the original, rather than an outright improvement. But with the original being so good, that's not faint praise.

I really enjoyed Axiom Verge and was excited to find out there is a new one to play. Surprisingly, the game is very different from AV1 apparently due to being based off of Castlevania instead of Metroid. TBH I hated it at first, but decided to push on because I was curious if it was going to get better.  Well it did. I'm not sure exactly what made it really endearing to me but around 3 hours in it just sort of clicked, and I really started to love it even more than the first one. There is a scene where you stand at the edge of the world near an ocean, and you can really see how bizarrely the world is structured. There is a song that plays in this area called "White Sand City." Hearing and seeing that at the same time hit home to me that this isn't just a sequel to AV1, its an evolution of that game's soul. Moments like that are special in gaming. If you know, you know.  The music and art design work together fantastically to communicate an odd feeling that you can't really put into words but permeates both Axiom Verge games. The story is somehow even more strange than the first game and even more bold in the big-brain sci-fi ideas department. The beginning is difficult, but just like in AV1 you end up horrendously overpowered very quickly and the abilities are far more interesting and useful than in AV1. That is NOT a diss on AV1.  Go into this one with an open mind and some patience and you will be rewarded.

Heck, most people don't even realize what happened to the main character's own daughter. Not to mention endless fractal vassal universes, programmable afterlives, or the wide-ranging implications for what actually happens in the first game. Very postmodern in the sense that what is real is non-existent, or so far removed from what we see that it doesn't matter. The vibe this game creates is substantially different from AV1, in a way that is less to my taste personally. The game is fine on its own but I would place it a couple tiers down from the original. To me it didn't feel like the rewards for exploring were as meaningful as the first, Lack of weapon variety was definitely noticed. I also found myself playing most of the game as the drone, due to mobility reasons primarily, which is fine, but it means I basically didn't interact with any of the human form character systems after the first couple hours of the game, which doesn't feel like the intended experience.   I also found the ending to be somewhat anticlimactic, I was a little surprised when it ended and was expecting another couple hours of story to happen (you can put together the pieces with the lore pages, but I was still expecting to do more before the credits), the default ending cutscene feels like its setting up the final act of the game rather than putting a bow on things, but I suppose the ending of AX1 was a little similar in that regard, just in a way that felt different to me.  TLDR, buy the game if you need a metroidvania fix, but don't go in expecting AV1.

Usually I tend to appreciate the sequels that introduce entirely different atmosphere and themes from their predecessors. Sure, it takes a moment or two to get used to the changes but in the end they offer a familiar experience dressed up in an entirely new set of clothes. In the case of Axiom 2 the changes are glaring. The music that welcomes the player even before they can see the game's menu is milder than it used to be in the original, the game discards it's browns and bloody reds in favor of shades of blue and steers away from the glitched bio-punk towards the more metaphysical concepts.    [h3](X2b.mX.33xXf) Cognition is an algorithm processing the information in an organized and chronological fashion regardless of the actual structure of the information given. [/h3]     Having said that, I don't believe that in the case of Axiom Verge such a transformation is a success. Too much of the original's appeal relied on its nature including its grungy, melty looks, it's raw, synthetic* music and the insane amount of effort put into making the game resemble Super Metroid without actually shamelessly copying it. The sequel discards almost all of that in favor of somewhat shallow metaphysical musings that additionally never get sufficiently developed.   Being an owner of a global supercorporation, the game's protagonist, Indra, initially appeared to me a fascinating possibility. She seemed far more interesting than the first game's Trace who was basically a generic scientist. And while Axiom1 had managed to build Trace up and make his story sufficiently intricate, the sequel does exactly the opposite. The motivations driving the characters' actions, the history of the game's alternate dimensions, basically every element of the narrative  felt to me as if introduced not to create a congruent world, but a facade of gambits necessary to push the events forward. And it didn't help that almost every single of those plot tokens was instantly dropped and forgotten the moment they served their purpose.  The characters Indra meets are shallow and uninteresting. Most of the encounters are only a few lines of dialogue long. And while there is one event that affects them later in the game, I've found it disappointing how the characters reacted in exactly the same fashion when Indra approached them as a drone as they did when she was a human. There is some worldbuilding done in the logs and tablets found throughout the game but, as mentioned before, the plot seemed to me rather forced and not particularly interesting.      [h3](X2b.mX.00a5Xa) Algorithm perceived as a reality remains valid and undeniable to any internal observers regardless of the subroutines executed within said algorithm. [/h3]       The art is a mixed bag. Some of the bosses look monumental and are animated with quality I would never expect from a pixel art game. Backgrounds are usually somewhere between adequate and very good, and there’s a lot of effort put in creating a distinct art style for the alternate dimension of Breach that the protagonist visits. The human characters, on the other hand, look disappointing.   I've found the music difficult to withstand. While the opening theme is pleasant to listen to and actually unexpectedly upbeat, somewhere around the half of the story the player is subjected to ceaseless chanting which I've found obnoxious.  Mechanics are a mess. The basic platforming remain similar to the original game – basic but solid. I've also appreciated how the game switched the combat balance towards the melee. Still, almost none of the choices make sense when put together. For example, there is an enormous amount of collectible upgrades hidden throughout the world but there is no point in looking for them since any boss can be skipped with the exception of the final one, which turns out to be a puzzle fight. The enemies are often irritating until the player realizes that in most of the scenarios the drone can be used to scout and clear the areas without any punishment. Admittedly, some of the abilities are unique and interesting like the already mentioned drone transformation, climbing vertical surfaces which offers a welcome alternative to the usual wall jump mechanics or visiting the Breach. Hacking the enemies during the combat is also an extremely welcome option, although it seems – like the entirety of this game – more tamed than glitching them in and out of the reality in the first game.     [h3](2b.mX.7a$$X) Spirituality is a subalgorithm developed overtime by the cognition as a translator for the mother algorithm said cognition perceives as reality. [/h3] **  Axiom2 does a lot of things right, but in the long run I could not escape the feeling that it crumbles underneath the weight of its own ambition. The world(s) are expansive, but also unnecessarily complicated which makes exploring them tedious and irritating, the game lacks some balancing and polish and certain enemies are nothing more than a nuisance. The mechanics did not evolve much since the first game and at some points they even seem like a downgrade.   Axiom2 made me realize the staggering difference that exists between a 6 out of 10 game and a 7. The first Axiom was a 7. 7+ even. It grew on me in time. It's world and ideas managed to convince me after a while. Axiom2 lacks in most of these areas. Perhaps, it is me who fails to appreciate or even understand the complexities of the world given***. Nonetheless, it is best to assume that Axiom Verge 2 will be mostly enjoyable to the fans of the original game while the new players should first try the prequel.     * I am perfectly aware that if you take a closer look at it, the words 'raw' and 'synthetic' are antonyms, and I believe this is exactly what made the soundtrack of the first game such an unique experience.  ** I’ve grown to absolutely love the original game’s Axiom1. Therefore I’ve tried to keep the titles of my paragraphs here in the same fashion and also resembling my attempts at understanding the game’s storyline.   *** There is also a very distinct possibility that Axiom Verge 2 is setting the stage for yet another sequel. Not only, as mentioned above, some branches of the story get cut short before they are able to sufficiently grow. There are some events that notably change the lore or even contradict some of the story known from the previous game. These discrepancies are, in my opinion, too substantial to be a coincidence and I can think about at least several interesting possibilities in which they could affect the lore of Axiom Verge. Long story short, yes, I’m waiting for the third installment.    [h3]As always, do with this whatever you wish and, as always, try to have a wonderful life.  [/h3]

Overall, I really liked this game. It is a well-polished Metroidvania with an interesting world, interesting and mostly unique abilities (no double-jump or dash to be found) and mostly fun gameplay. I had a good time playing it. It took me about 14 hours to 100% it, and I recommend it.  However, there are a few nits that I feel I must pick.  1. By the time you enter the second major area in the game, there is a sudden difficulty spike, and the game becomes outright frustrating for a while. You are ill-equipped to fight the enemies who all take way too many hits. They either have super fast melee attacks or move fast and do contact damage, or they completely outrange you with projectiles that are difficult to dodge (or just have lasers that auto-hit you unless you are in cover). Your only projectile weapon is a boomerang that is rather slow and has a significant downtime between attacks due to the time it takes to return. As soon as you get a few key abilities and items and invest in a few skills, this gets better, and near the end of the game you become very powerful, but the phase during which your combat abilities just suck is just a tad too long. I can imagine some people just give up during that phase and miss all the good gameplay that happens later.  2. The fast travel system just wastes too much of your time: a) You will dematerialize and then rematerialize at the destination in a very unnecessarily long animation (and, if you are not [spoiler]in drone form[/spoiler] during the travel, you will then have to watch your character get up from a prone position which tacks on another second). b) You cannot fast travel between Kiengar and [spoiler]the Breach[/spoiler], so every trip across tacks on another minute that will just be spent fast-traveling to a save point near a portal, entering it and then fast traveling again on the other side. It takes no additional skill, only time, and makes the late-game item hunt much more of a chore than it needs to be.  3. Most of the hidden items are easy to mildly challenging to find, but there are a few stinkers that are completely invisible/unknowable with no indication whatsoever. The most ridiculous ones are: [spoiler]the one nano dispenser on which you need to perform a specific hack twice to get a health upgrade (all other dispensers just cycle between free nano and free health every time you perform this hack)[/spoiler] and [spoiler]the one rock formation in the middle of nowhere that has invisibly breakable blocks that require the Gud-An Na (an AoE shockwave) to break. All other blocks in the game that require this move have a somewhat visible indicator (cracks or a certain shape). All other invisibly breakable blocks in the game can be destroyed using normal attacks. So this is just inconsistent with everything the game has trained the player to do in not one, but two ways. Yay.[/spoiler] Fortunately, there are only a few hidden items like this, but it is still annoying.  If you can look past these small flaws, the game is quite enjoyable for any 2D Metroidvania enthusiast.
