Bom, vamos lá, na minha concepção, qualquer continuação de algum jogo, acaba sendo comparada com o antecessor e a expectativa é que seja um jogo no geral, melhor em todos os aspectos, eu vejo como algo natural, e na minha visão é onde esse jogo falhou.  Axiom Verge 2 só tem esse nome pelo universo dele estar conectado com o jogo anterior, já que a maioria das coisas foram trocadas aqui. Invés de uma arma que tem diversos modos de disparo, aqui você deve se contentar com um sistema de arma branca e bumerangue, sendo 5 armas brancas diferentes e dois bumerangues, um verdadeiro retrocesso, o titulo anterior possui mais de 20 opções em seu arsenal. Agora você tem a opção de conseguir pontos de habilidade e usar como quiser no seu menu, isso te dá mais escolha do que só achar os upgrades, enquanto tira um dos prazeres de explorar por esses upgrades. Essa troca não deixa de me passar a impressão de "menos conteúdo"  Ah sim, o sistema de hackear, uma função que permanece no segundo jogo, apesar de esse sistema ter até uma interface, ele não parece mais o mesmo, a ambientação não é mais a mesma, você sai de um jogo com um ambiente alienígena, e efeitos de glitch que fazem você se questionar se aquilo é mesmo realidade, para um mundo mais "humano", isso não é necessariamente algo ruim, mas todas essas mudanças fazem esse jogo parecer uma cópia, um titulo inspirado no anterior, um spin-off se quiser.  O motivo de tanta comparação com o Axiom Verge 1? Ele é melhor, simples. Uma continuação não precisa seguir a risca e ter tudo que tinha na obra anterior mas, quando se muda tanta coisa, ou muda muito o sistema e jogabilidade principal, a base que vai do começo até o fim do jogo, aí se assume um risco.   Eu não considero esse jogo ruim, o sistema de mudar entre planos foi muito bem pensado, porém é uma dor de cabeça para o jogador, queria eu que fosse só algo que eu tive dificuldade, mas quando se vê diversos jogadores com dúvidas em fóruns, por não conseguirem seguir a história por conta de um sistema que pode ser tão confuso, se percebe que não é algo pessoal.   Se isso fosse de fato um Spin-off  eu não teria achado tão ruim, isso não é digno do nome que carrega, eu tinha minhas expectativas por gostar muito do primeiro, e ainda mais pelo final do jogo anterior sugerir uma continuação, mas esse jogo é outra história, e para piorar, termina com um final aberto para continuação também, não faço ideia do que esperar de um Axiom Verge 3.

The early game is a bit linear for my tastes, but the game isn't telling you where to go its just funneling you down a certain path and there is little that can be explored outside of the main objectives.   It opens up a decent amount afterwards, but by the time things feel like they're just getting going you're 2-3 hours from beating the game. If you go for 100% then it'll take longer; however I didn't feel all that inclined to do so. Most of the collectibles are skill points which you can use to upgrade your character. Nothing on the skill tree is all that rewarding so I didn't want to max it out.   There are two weapons that you get to use, an ax and a boomerang. I couldn't figure out a way to really use these as you have to be pretty close to the enemy to use either and its almost impossible to dodge some of the attacks from certain enemies. By the time that they show up you have plenty of health but its still annoying.   There are some interesting abilities, but the menu to switch between them sucks and you have to click down on the right stick to activate some of them which is a bit clunky (you can remap your controls, but I shouldn't have to, especially when the left trigger does not get used for anything until the last hour of the game). There also aren't that many abilities. By the end of the game you don't feel that powerful like you do in other metroidvanias. I felt like I was in the late midgame when I beat the final boss.  Speaking of bosses they are pretty much optional (except for the final and one or two others). This is not a bad thing, but they're either completely broken and annoying to fight or waaaaay too easy. That's also not the worst thing in the world because you don't play this sort of game for boss fights; however, I didn't feel particularly challenged by any portion of the game and I feel like a lack of competent boss fights was partially to blame.  One thing that was not lacking was the story. The lore is great and reading the logs lying around was enjoyable. It was interesting to learn more about the world and whatnot. I did feel that the ending was a bit lacking and it didn't really answer all of the questions that I had.  I do recommend the game, but don't go into it expecting a metroid like experience or even an experience like the first game. It's a decent game and I enjoyed playing it a lot. It was the only game I played for the past week, just don't expect too much. Maybe wait for a sale rather than paying full price.

This is a great metroidvania.  Stellar exploration.  Two overlaid fully fleshed out maps to jump back and forth between. Much, much, much better than the first game's obtuse level design. Sublime. Frankly, I wish more modern  metroidvanias toned down the combat in favor of puzzles/exploration. Story/Lore: its a hot mess.  Main protagonist feels like she's a protagonist from an Ayn Rand novel,  some of the writing in the English version clearly wasn't edited by a native English speaker.

Axiom Verge 2 throws you off initially if you played the first game. Then you notice that the design decisions are too deliberate to be a fumble by the developer Tom Happ.   The protagonist in AV2 is Indra, a ruthless, middle aged tech company CEO searching for her daughter. Instead of a multitool-weapon you're handed an ice axe and a boomerang that are borderline ineffective against the enemies, which are fast and have access to guns, lasers and other ranged weapons. Combat is designed more about avoiding, manipulating or zipping past enemies, although you can take every fight with ease, face tank and trade hits after the first third of the game.   The game contextualises more of the Axiom Verge Multiverse and starts off quite sluggish. In true metroidvania fashion you will unlock abilities that help you traverse the environment while you get stronger and find out more about the mysterious world Kiengir that you landed in. You acquire different movement abilities for your human and drone form and are encouraged to combine them to get around. Later, you also unlock an alternate world that is layered over the normal dimension and constitutes an alternate way of traversing.  Overall, I think Axiom Verge 2 is a good game, but it is simply less fun than its predecessor. The combat element of the game is very simplified, all bosses but one are optional. The art style better rendered, but overall less intriguing and beautiful than the monumental Rusalki and the strange, alien world of Sudra in AV1. I think you should play this if you want to know more about the story of Axiom Verge, but if you are in for the metroidvania-gameplay there are many better titles for you. The game is also overall much shorter than AV1, I have reached the ending of the game with 98% map completion in only 10 hours, really taking my time to explore.   It's not a fumble, it's deliberate. But it might not be your thing. If it was not named Axiom Verge 2, but something like, "An Axiom Verge Story", it would maybe set the expectations better. Proper titling is important.

don't evaluate this product by the means of pure gamers. This is a Master in game design and world building. This is Art at its finest. T. Happ is not simply a game developer , he's an artist. Don't play this game , live it. 10/10

You get a mysterious world full of ancient technology and mythological beings. I really love the mesopotamia/sci-fi theme. The pixel art is lovely and the soundtrack (again) absolutely stunning. I hear the tracks in my head, even when I'm not playing. The hacking tools and the two overlapped world maps add some interesting new gameplay elements. To summarize - in my opinion Thomas Happ delivered a worthy successor and a must-play for all metroidvania fans.

SINTESI DEL GAMEPLAY  Come il capitolo precedente AV 2 è un metroidvania sostanzialmente classico se non per alcune trovate assolutamente geniali. A differenza del primo però qui il gioco investe molto di più in quella che è l'essenza dei metroidvania cioè l'esplorazione. L'esperienza di gioco è in pratica un immenso puzzle esplorativo, labirintico, dove dovremo attentamente studiare la mappa e ingegnarci con i nostri perk per poter accedere alle nuove sezioni. I combattimenti e i boss non mancheranno ma hanno un ruolo più marginale, sono più facili rispetto al passato. Per il resto è sostanzialmente molto simile al primo se non per il fatto che il combat è più melee rispetto al passato.   ESTRO ARTISTICO  All'altezza del meraviglioso game design la direzione artistica suggestiona con una pixelart estremamente ispirata, musiche "8bit" e una narrazione fantascientifica coi fiocchi. Si celebrano in tutti i modi i sentimenti nati nella goldenage dei metroidvania e lo si fa però con una marcia in più, offrendo un'esperienza che ricorda si tremendamente il passato ma ne è anche conseguenza, evoluzione.  PRO  Essenza esplorativa dei metroidvania all'ennesima potenza e grandissima direzione artistica.  CONTRO  Chi cerca grandi confronti nelle boss fight rimarrà deluso come chi non avrà pazienza nel districarsi in un'esplorazione molto ardua e complessa.

A sub-par metroidvania. Axiom Verge 2 disregards many strong points from Axiom Verge 1 and instead builds on those aspects of Axiom Verge 1 that weren't great to begin with. Minor spoilers ahead.  - Combat is Zelda-esque melee with an additional (low-damage) bumerang now. This alone is not a problem, but many enemies have ranged weapons and an AI that deliberately exploits the limits of your attacks (e. g. by moving away when you jump into melee range). In the end, combat feels tedious and in many cases, it sadly is more fun to just run past (or through) the enemies than to fight them.  - Fluid controls weren't a strong suit of AV1 and controls are even worse here: Normal movement is slow and stiff. You get near to no skills for faster, more fluid progression (e. g. double jump), and those skills you do get just feel bad. Climbing, for example, unintentionally sticks you to walls and has a pointless, limited range you need to cheese around by drone form.  - There are many items and upgrades for your human form, but most of these see little to no use. You lose human form about half-way through the game and only regain it shortly before the end. And even then, you'll be keeping to drone form because it is so much easier to move around with.  - The path through the game world is a random mess, even for a metroidvania. You just explore open ends until you find another progression upgrade at some unpredictable hiding spot. Upgrades are disdainfully waiting for you on the ground. There is not a single leading boss fight and in most cases, there is not even any kind of celebratory architecture (say, a shrine) around the upgrade.  - Exploration is excessively tedious. This comes from the limited movement options (as above), but also from the irregularly distributed quicktravel spots and the annoyingly pointless choice of prohibiting quicktravel between both game worlds.  - Dialogue is just as obtuse as in Axiom Verge 1, but there's two things worse now: First, dialogue boxes don't show pop-up heads anymore, so it is hard to follow who is talking. Second, the dialogues between humans sound weird, like unrelated monologues rather than communication (as if two people both had a stroke while talking).  - I experienced several minor bugs and glitches: from 100% CPU load while the game is minimized to momentarily getting stuck in walls to items that said I could "take" them, but I couldn't (without any "nope" sound or anything).  - The pixel art has improved over AV1. However, since the environment with its La-Mulanese touch isn't as technical and abstract as in AV1 anymore, the minor flaws in the pixel art become much more apparent.   - I still like the lore and the setting, the new biomes are interesting and varied, and the soundtrack is awesome.

For as long as it lasted, I was completely absorbed in this game. One after another, perhaps in unintended order you unlock new systems and movement options. While not as great as the first game, in my opinion, Axiom Verge 2 is still a good Metroidvania. And a quite good sequel.  Whilst the first game was a very straight forward copy of Metroid, with dark and dank caves and alien life forms, the second game has a lot more variety in the environments, visuals and gameplay. This is a good thing, mostly, since it feels like something else. Something different. The areas are more varied, for sure, but also not quite as cohesive and feels a bit broken up. I still prefer the first game's vibe though. The map is better, and worse. Better as in that you can see more detail, and worse because you can see...more detail. It can become quite cluttered and annoying on the eyes at times to be honest. But it's somewhat easier to see certain paths and what not.  The story is as always equally strange and a bit hard to keep track on, which is exactly the point. You do feel lost in an alien world. Music and sound design is fantastic, as per usual, and the atmosphere is great.  The game focuses more on melee combat (and to some extent, more exploration), which differs greatly from the run-and-gun-approach of the first game. It mostly works fine, though I'm not completely sold on the action this time around. Mostly on account of there not being enough variety and ways to tackle it. It's not snappy enough to feel sublime as a melee platformer. Still, it gets the job done and is not an issue since most enemies and bosses are quite simple to overcome.  Overall, I quite enjoyed this sequel. It might not trump the first game for me personally, but I do applaud and salute the change in style. It's still very solid as a Metroidvania, and well worth playing. Recommended for people interested in the subgenre.   The fact that it's still made by pretty much one guy is as amazing as before.

Great for 100% - Save system; you don't lose anything but traversal time when you die - Story beats & lore   Dislikes: - YMMV; uncovering where to go next in the early part of the game can be a bit rough (traversal time, enemy strength)  Ambivalent: - platform art/contrast; at times noticing them wasn't "automatic" due to them not sticking out enough from the environment

Finally reached the end of Axiom Verge 2. I can see why people didn't like it as much as the first game:  There's no wide array of weaponry to find and upgrade (there's like 6 weapons and 4 of them can be upgraded outside of the Skill Tree system). Edit: I guess there's more weapons than I ended up finding but still, it's way less diverse than the first game.  Environments are more organic and less gamified, making things feel a bit blander. For example, takes place in a world that is very similar to ours rather than an entirely alien world which resulted in blander environments to explore.  Exploration felt a lot less rewarding as a lot of your upgrades (not abilities, those you have to find) are unlocked via a Skill Menu. There's considerably fewer Health and Weapon Damage upgrades to find throughout the environments because there are Skill trees that buff this too. You do have to find Skill Points but finding those isn't quite the same feeling as finding a carefully placed health, damage, hacking etc. upgrade in the environment.  I don't think there's any movement enhancing abilities to unlock, which makes the gameplay feel a lot slower than the first game since you move the same speed throughout the entire game.  Enemies didn't have anywhere near as interesting Hacking effects as the first game and I think enemy variety was lessened. The first enemy you encounter is frequently re-used for example.  I found the abilities to both move [spoiler]breach portals as well as the ability to exit the Breach at any location[/spoiler] made exploration a bit of a headache. My issue with exploration however may be because I kept overlooking what should've been an obvious route through an area of the game. The sky graphics along the path to the [spoiler]Weapons Factory[/spoiler] looked like a wall so I never realized I could [spoiler]toss my boomerang[/spoiler] a certain way to hit a switch until I started looking at a text walkthrough ([spoiler]I did throw my boomerang upwards and saw the note resting up there but[/spoiler] [spoiler]didn't know I could make it exit to the right[/spoiler]).  It's a competent experience... but not quite what you'd expect from a sequel. It's much more focused on telling a story than is focused on the gameplay and the world design kinda necessitated map markers telling you where the next big thing to find/do is located due to there being many big open areas to tread and re-tread as you get new abilities instead of tight corridors.  Overall, I'd say it's worthy of your time but don't go in to it expecting a deeper gameplay experience than the first game.

I both Can and Can't recommend Axiom Verge 2.  The game has a tremendous amount of upsides... and a tremendous amount of downsides.                                                                 Pros: +The Standalone Story is great!  +-The super varied combat system lets you tackle situations in a good variety of ways... sometimes. The problem is that most enemy placements end up being very repetitive, and when they aren't then the enemies end up getting in guaranteed hits anyways. The game has such a huge problem with trying to make interesting enemy placements that the Devs gave THREE Mega-heals that heal 50% of your insanely huge health pool... Speaking of which...  +The backgrounds and stage-art look significantly better than Axiom Verge 1.  +Is a great Stand-alone game.                                                                Cons: -You are way too tanky for no reason. The game's combat is so poorly designed that instead of trying to fine tune it, the Devs went "screw it, the player won't care about taking guaranteed hits if they have a metric F**k-ton of health." -The story and gameplay pacing is bad in quite a lot of areas with not enough time given to certain aspects of Indra's character and *too* much time spent with little to no characterization of her.  -Not a good sequel. The themes and world introduced in the 1st game are not expanded upon or hinted at. This game only shares a name and 6-ish journal entries about Trace and the themes present in Axiom Verge 1.  -Very often there will be an upgrade and as a player you want to use this upgrade to explore the world more but that upgrade can only be used in specific areas that scream "USE ME, USE THE SUPER EXPLODEY BOMB". This would be fine, but there's so MANY upgrades like this that you'll very often run into every possible side-path before finding the ONE area that actually continues the story,  -The enemy variety is weirdly lacking, even towards the end of the game. There are only 11 enemy types in the entire game:    1. drone that runs and zaps   2. Walker with big legs that shoots minigun... and sometimes zaps.    3. bird that shoots   4. Fake block Shuriken   5.Anenome Tendril Robot   6. Tentacle monster   7. Robo Spider   8. Spartan Drone spawner   9. Flying thing that shoots downward.   10. Things that stay on the walls and crawl around on them   11. Bosses And until you get halfway through the game, most of these enemy types are **not** present. so as a player you better get used to killing #1 and #2 in most rooms... if you even want to do combat at all. Where A.V.1 made combat a forced part of gameplay(where enemies would outright stop the player if they weren't dealt with), A.V.2. makes combat... not even worth it. There is only one required boss to beat the game, and that's the final boss.   -----------------  Overall I would recommend the game, but not to fans of the original...

amazing, creative game, one of the few metroidvanias which really leans into exploration over everything else, which makes playing this game a breath of fresh air when compared to most other games in the genre which tend to focus on combat, or at least that's what my experience has been. art style, world and music are fantastic, story is satisfactory, not too much, not too little. compared to axiom verge 1 it is quite different in some ways, particularly concerning the combat and weapons, 2 stand out elements in the original, which are both far less developed. The game, at least when in combat, is quite a bit easier as well. Many negative reviews of this game (from what I've seen) come from people who don't necessarily dislike the game, but would rather it be a more direct sequel to axiom verge, which is fair enough that game was great, but as someone who loves exploration I am very VERY happy with the direction this game has chosen to go in. 9.5/10 easily.
Not as great as the first game, but pretty good still.

Lamentablemente, creo que no está a la altura del 1.  Hubo algunos tramos en los que me sentí un poco aburrido por la movilidad tosca que tiene el personaje. El juego no es malo pero lamentablemente no podría recomendarselo a alguien ciegamente porque lo más probablemente es que no se termine el juego.  Espero que Tom Happ se ponga las pilas con el 3!!!

After the slap in the face that was Axiom Verge 1, I wasn't excpecting a second game. Heck, I've seen it like one year later. I am a huge fan of Axiom Verge 1, music was good, art was outstanding and deisng was just blowing my mind. Some areas feels pretty weird and sometimes you don't really know where too go or what to do but it is a metroidvania after all, you explore by yourself.  And then, I've played the second opus. And what a disappointment, The game is full of new features, a new story, a new mode, new bosses, new enemies and new areas and a whole new world. And it can be awesome but I think it's just not clicked for me. Every now and then I was feeling: "Wow, this is so fun, it reminds me of the 1 so much", and I was wondering, why not playing the 1 instead? This is not a bad game at all, it is, I think a hit or miss game where for some people it will be awesome and for some it will not click.  I do not understand the story at all, first English is not my primary language and it does not help, but I do not have the sense to achieve something in this game. Our character just walks from point A to point B without question, you follow your map and you kill a big dude, than you got to somewhere else and again. The final boss was meh, I mean, it is litteraly the first boss of Starbound but you loose nothing if you die. It feels pointless.  A big no for me is the comparison to the first game. In AV1 you are being told to do something but not how nor where. Here you are told where and how and when. It means you have always a marker on your map to the next point. This is not abhorrent until you try to reach it. In AV1 (and lot of metroid vania) the path to an objective is never the shorthest one. But in all these MetroidVania (Super Metroid, Hollow Knight, Blasphemous, AV1 and many more) the objective is not shown (or partially) so you wander and you find your path. But here because the objective is marked, you search everywhere near this objective, but sometimes, it is not even remotely close to this place because a new power up is needed.  I tend to never read a wiki before completing a game for metroidvania, but for this one it was mandatory, I was stuck 3 times on the course of 9 hours of play (and I was stuck for about 3 hours) this is not a good sign.  Again, if you enjoyed this game, good for you, I am not saying this is a bad game, I am just saying playing with the map on the second screen is not for me.

I've played a good portion of the first Axiom Verge game and I got to a point where I hit a wall. It wasn't too bad of a game, but it was not an easy one. Then comes Axiom Verge 2.  Have you ever played a game that you already know in the first area that it will be completely exasperating to the point where even getting to the next save is a chore you don't want to bother undertaking?  There..that questions sums up Axiom Verge 2 completely.  Based on this game...I pray that the series dies. I wouldn't want to see how 3 turns out. In fact this game is so bad that it caused this series to get a treatment no game in my collection has done before? (And to give you an idea of my collection size...I have not reviewed all my games yet, but this review marks #150.) After this review I will be uninstalling this catastrophe of a game, hiding it...and taking the first one with it. I don't even want to see either of them on my list ever again.  I stated in my Axiom Verge review that the first suffered from maximum effort/minimum gain. AV2 takes this to a new level: maximum effort/zero gain. Nothing I got was interesting. None of the weapons I used are a joy to wield. Its like going to work for 12 hours a day for 7 days a week, to get a christmas gift in the mail from your job...opening it...and see that they gave you a one year membership to the Jelly of the Month Club.  But I don't like doing a review without saying at least one good thing about a game. So here goes:  This game is uninstallable.  There I did it. Skip this whole game series. (yes, skip the first one as well now.) Don't play 3 if it ever shows up. You have better things you can do in life....like working those 12 hour days for 7 days a week.  Special note: Just to add insult to injury....I got the achievement for beating the first boss. Only 41.5% have this achievement. Let that sink in on how fast people quit this debacle.

TLDR I would say this game is really interesting mainly for platforming/metroidvania exploration. Just disappointing as a sequel. I personally can't recommend it because I think it misses a ton of potential for this reason. Have completed this game around two and a half times including other platforms but am distinctly mixed. Memorable more for its cool, unique art and music.   If you hoped for "just Axiom Verge but improved and expanded upon" this is not that. With hope that the game would still turn out good because its predecessor left an amazing impression, it's a bittersweet experience to play this. There is really awesome stuff here. Melee weapon+ boomerang gameplay as well as hacking alongside it/ offering many interactions with different enemies is really, really promising, but much like the storytelling & plot, throughout the game does not seem to flesh itself out to a satisfactory amount before the game abruptly ends.  Scenery & backgrounds are pretty & cool, but the colour palettes that take up most of the screentime can appear bland after a while, in contrast to AV1's vibrant alien setting. Personally I think a lot of the soundtrack is gorgeous, inspired, and even entrancing. Some really awesome work on the soundtrack in my opinion.   Overall though, I find myself a lot more frustrated with the pace of the exploration, dying repeatedly to certain combinations of enemies & platforming but still somewhat being able to brute force through obstacles or ignoring threats altogether, etc. Hacking is really interesting but combat isn't very satisfying because of this. This may also hurt the exploration with feelings of early-game sluggishness and requiring a few hours before things get only sort of satisfying. Skill ceiling/minimal deaths certainly requires a different set of skills than AV1, but I do not find myself nearly as drawn to this game enough to get similar satisfaction out of it.

The short of it is: I felt let down by Axiom Verge 2. It is not a bad game.  You can play Axiom Verge 2, without playing Axiom Verge 1. I do hope there is a third one.  AV2 has great style and music, but I couldn't enjoy it fully. Maybe I expected more from it?     I can't be more specific without spoilers, so this is your SPOILER WARNING.     Spoilery review (complaints?) below...      Keep going...      Just a bit more...      Ok. You were warned. I'll get to the drone part, but first things first.  ---I couldn't get into the story. And this has 3 levels for me.  - Indra's story (the protagonist's story). She's just being led on the whole game. Go to Antarctica. Go to this coordinate, now go to this other coordinate. Why? Because. Now go. Get your body back. Now go kill the big bad. She doesn't really achieve anything in the game. Couldn't get back, couldn't find her daughter, didn't kill the big bad at the end (well, one Indra did). She was just the errand girl for Hammond, the big bad and Lamassu. And about the 2 Indras at the end... That just like, happened. Didn't really had a payoff for me. I'd rather have the drone arm get Indra back on his own than, oh, it's just a copy of her mind or something. At least, it would be some sort of accomplishment by one of them. Indra accomplished nothing as far as I am concerned. - The big picture story. That was just too convoluted for me. Sure, I like reading about it in the wiki, seeing all the things I missed, and I appreciate the mystery surrounding it. But it just flew over my head during my playthrough. Also, lots and lots of loose ends. Where was Hammond? Did Hammond die? Then who was Indra talking to? How was she talking to her? Where was Indra's daughter? The other people she met just mutated and died? Why was there even a mention of the kazzacks (cosssacks?)?  jut died She goes to Antarctica to find Hammond and her daughter. She does not find her daughter. Did she find Hammond? I am not sure, She finds a body that's supposedly her, but then talks to her on the last ansible or something. Like I said, the story is not clear. So, while looking for Hammond she does errands for the bad Arm and then loses her body to it.  - The comparison with Axiom Verge 1. There is a point to be made that the same thing happened to Trace in AV1. Everyone lied to him and he was just let on. But he did succeed in his task. And the twist had a huge payoff for me. Maybe it didn't make an impression on me because of the similarities.  I could overlook the story, but other aspects of the game also felt like just things to do or get with no relevance. Sure, lots of things are needed to advance the story, but they didn't feel useful or fun to use.  --- Now the main game mechanics. Where to start?  - Weapons. Basically, and the for the most part, melee only. You get a boomerang early on, but I don't think anybody uses it. It's slow, does little damage. It sits mostly forgotten on the menu. I was willing to give it a chance when I found my second or third weapon and noticed different attack animations and thought they could have different uses or attack styles. But no, those weapons didn't matter because of:  - The over-reliance on the drone. I thought it was just a section of the game that used the drone, but it's more like .... 70% (?). At least, it felt like that to me. I didn't like the drone at first, but it grew on me. I preferred it late game because of its mobility options. But no mistake, it is overused. There's a sub-world, called the Breach, only accessible to the drone. Ok. Then, you have to use the drone for some puzzle/exploration elements. Ok. Then, you don't have a human body, so the drone is all you have. This is when I noticed this was the primary game element of AV2.  You do get a human(ish) body later, but by that time, the drone's mobility is better than the other body, so you don't want to switch back.   What are the consequences of the overuse of the drone?  - Useless skills, weapons, upgrades. Indra has a choice of several melee weapons. You don't need them. There is no reason to change the starting weapon. Indra gets 2, well 3, boomerangs. Couldn't find a use for the first 2. Maybe, occasionally to get the attention of an enemy or hit a switch. The 3rd. one does not count as a weapon. It's more of an item. It's a remote control boomerang that you need to use once (maybe twice) to hit an out of the way switch. Useless as a weapon. The skill points, skill tree and most of the exploration are rendered pointless for the following:      . There's zero need to upgrade the combat skill tree.      . Maybe upgrading health is useful, but you do find health upgrades on the map, so this is redundant.      . There's a ... let's call it mist cloud skill, and you can use points to move faster, but there is no need for it.      . Hacking skill upgrades... meh. Some are mandatory. Useless for the most part. It could have been fun, but it was not properly developed or used.      . Just do all the drone upgrades. Leave some points available for when hacking is necessary. Otherwise, go nuts with the drone.  Random grievances:  - The grab on the ledge skill. Really? - The see in the dark skill. You could've just put a switch to turn on the light in the area. - The flashlight as a separate item. Could've just left it as a default feature.
9/10 Started having way more fun somehow once the game made me play half of it as the drone
A supremely fun metroidvania game. I enjoyed the unique upgrades and beautiful pixel art. The map has some genuinely challenging puzzles to solve. Highly recommended!

Fantastic game! While Axiom Verge leaned heavily into the Metroid side of the Metroidvania genre, Axiom Verge 2 sits squarely on the Castlevania side. This is not a bad thing.  Intriguing story. Memorable characters. Beautiful landscapes. Good music. Great gameplay.  The lack of any sort of gun was unexpected, especially when many enemies can attack from a greater distance than you can. The weapons pretty much all feel the same; The only real difference is how quickly they can attack. This can feel clunky until you upgrade your attack speed. I found many enemies easier to fight as a drone, because of the hitboxes of the enemies and the player's attack.  AV2 improves on AV1 in many ways. The mapping system is much better, including pins to mark places of interest for later (just be sure to do so! A few more pins would be nice, because you run out quickly). The drone is much more capable. The Breach is an interesting change of pace from the main world. The world is much more connected and easier to traverse, and there's a very much-appreciated fast travel option. A pacifist run (and achievement) is possible. The lore is expanded greatly, yet leaves plenty to speculation. Hacking and "secret" areas are much less obscured, making it much easier to figure out where to go and what to do next.  I loved both games and am eagerly awaiting the announcement of an Axiom Verge 3, if one should come to be!

Got around to playing Axiom Verge 2 on my Steam Deck. This game was originally an Epic Games exclusive, but is now available here on Steam and on other platforms. Axiom Verge 2 is another Metroidvania which continues to deliver the same otherworldly allure that the original game did. I do have a slight preference for the first game, but you do not need to play the first game to enjoy this one, as the story is not directly connected. You'll have to pay attention to the lore to see how the two games are connected. The lore man.... it's captivating.... it demands deep dives and game theory-style videos. I love it.  Here you play as Indra who travels to the Antarctic to save her daughter, and gets entrapped into a huge mystery. Part of the mystery involves the dual world mechanic, as there is the main overworld, and the breach world, a world of NES-style graphics and banger 8-bit music. I liked the use of the two worlds with plenty of puzzles to solve that involve traveling between both worlds. I also enjoyed the expanded use of the drone, and found myself using that more than running around on foot.  Even though this game is solid, I think this game needed to be given a subtitle instead of being labeled Axiom Verge "2". That could have helped temper fan exceptions while keeping the Axiom Verge name in marketing. Also, the combat is really awkward. It's hard for me to put into words what is awkward about it.  Count me in the group of people ready to buy and play whatever Thomas Happ releases next.
A marvelous sequel to one of the best Metroidvanias next to Hollow Knight. Thomas Happ is a genius.
Loved the original. A true spiritual successor to Metroid. This is just some generic platformer I couldn't force myself to like.

Non 100% run clocked at almost 9 hours, gameplay start kinda restricted, but get very fluid after you get some movement upgrades, history is really nice, not a lot of exposition so you need to piece things together yourself, and even then a lot is left a mistery, which is not a bad thing in my opinion  if you played the first axion verge, the melee focused combat will feel kinda strange at the start, but it gets really fun around the mid point of the game, game has a lot of places to visit and there is enough choice for you to get side tracked.  i will give it a 8/10 - not amazing, but really, really good.

メトロイドヴァニア系ゲームの2作目。 前作同様バリバリのSFストーリーと生体機械のドット、そして素晴らしい音楽を合わせた作品。 一方でバッチバチに使われまくる固有名詞と耳慣れない名前が続き、 翻訳ミスなのかプログラムミスなのか示される座標が違ったり、 ピッケル取りに行ってる間に原因不明で消えた運転手がそのままノータッチだったり、雑な所も。  というか前作と比較すると結構パワーダウンしてない？ エンディングでも1枚絵はないし、そもそも不評だったのか顔グラフィックは削除。 前作では数体いた会話できる巨大な機械は1体に削減。 大型敵のデザインはどれもよく、ストーリー自体も興味深い（全部見れていないが）。 ただ、前作比較だったり細かいミスが気になって没入するには至らなかった。  ゲーム自体のアクション性は結構好みだったのでオススメは出来る。 セーブポイントも多く、操作猶予は割とある方。一部を除いて。 前作をやって楽しかった人ならそのまま買っていいと思う。 ストーリーは……まあ、期待しすぎない程度で……。
Great Metroidvania, love the differences yet similarities from part 1.  9 outta 10.

The first game was remarkable in a lot of ways, setting high expectations for this sequel; so, how did it go? "Badly", many will say. By this, I suspect they actually mean "differently", as though some kind of human being designed this game, and did so with the intention of creating more than just a glorified level pack for the previous instalment.  The map is supposedly more confusing now. I, upon playing, found it to be greatly improved. Instead of flat color, it now displays the actual features of the regions you've explored; if there was a big statue of a guy with the body of a lion, you'll be able to find it by looking. You also don't need to switch between regions before you can view them, they're all just one big, connected world. You can also place and remove map markers anywhere, not just at your current location... and as many as you like, not just two per area. How is this more confusing?  Well, alright, it's not exactly one big, connected world... there are two big connected worlds, and you do switch between them; the Overworld (more or less regular reality) and the Breach (where things get weird)!  Each has its own look, sound and feel: The Overworld being more somber, beautiful and relatively friendly to humanoid life, along with various hostiles... and the remnants of a culture resembling ancient Assyria or thereabouts, parallell development sort of thing. It's very sciencey and more importantly it has a cool, distinctive look, so why question it? Just let it wash over you.  By contrast, the Breach requires a drone to explore, looks and sounds a lot more oldschool - as well as more chaotic - and occasionally teases you with glimpses of the familiar from Axiom Verge 1. Hints, suggestions... The game's lore is much the same. Perspectives, suspicions, beliefs. Very little is known to be factual. Which is kind of nice, really. People like Dark Souls because it doesn't just plainly state this and that; understanding what is going on is a much more active process of piecing things together from what you know and suspect.  The combat is different now. I personally enjoyed it, though... some of the pacing could use work. Certain weapons are granted rather late in the game, with the unfortunate consequence that you barely get to enjoy them. This is not ideal. Other weapons, however, are in for a good chunk of the respectable run time (especially if you go secret-hunting a lot), and some of them are quite fun to use. Melee attacks are your mainstay now; at a distance, you get a boomerangy thing which isn't great, but has its uses.  You get skill points you can use to upgrade various abilities, including health and the strength of melee or ranged attacks. There are a good number of skill point pickups strewn along your path - or hidden about it - and by the end, I felt more than powerful enough to deal with what dangers emerged. Translation: Game too easy? Rush through faster! Game too hard? Go find more power-ups. Or play however you like, but the wiggle room is there for enjoyment to occur.  Some of the platforming (especially to reach tricky secrets) can be very nintendo-y, but that's understandable, given the series' deep roots in that sphere of game design from the era when people were convinced that "the harder a game is to get through, the better value for money it is". Thankfully, Axiom Verge 2 has checkpoints instead of the traditional blatant progress robbery of the original NES era. Sometimes you get bumped back a ways from where you were, but you can eventually unlock fast travel to any revival site - you may want to save most of your backtracking for after you get that.  This game is not perfect, but I respect the changes made and the chances taken compared to the original. Whether you agree things improved, or feel they declined, or see it all as a "sidegrade" which is neither better nor worse, merely different - games will not get better if all the developers do, if all they are [i]allowed[/i] to do, is copy-paste from the previous instalment(s).  This game has its own story to tell, its own journey and experience to savor. It is a work of art, lovingly rendered, and imperfect as all of ourselves. It is a challenge to overcome, and a set of riddles to make sense of. It is a pleasant pastime. It is many things.  In particular, it is a game I enjoyed and would recommend to platforming exploration fans and fans of the first game. If you go in expecting something different, you shouldn't suffer too much from the changes.

AV2 switches up a lot of things while Sticking to a lot of things that worked well in the first one. You still get plenty of fun items  that radically alter your ability to navigate the world and get's way more inventive than just unlocking a double jump. I enjoyed the hacking mechanic and in terms of colours, lighting and music it's a bit more HiFi than the first one while still being true to the character of a retro style Metroidvania. Grab it, especially if you're a fan of the genre!

This is not Axiom Verge 1, and that's a good thing; both are fantastic in their own ways, and you should almost treat them as wholly separate.  I found this one to be a much steeper wall to get over initially - especially given the map size, it was hard to not get thoroughly lost at first. Once you start getting your footing, though, it's incredibly satisfying.  As others have said, the world is MASSIVE in comparison, and there are so many quality-of-life improvements (thank you, teleport to save locations), and you essentially get a 2-in-1 game with the heavy drone play and two maps.  I genuinely think my only real critique is that I thought the music in AV1 was so good that this one just couldn't live up to the same legacy - no parts of the OST hit like they did playing through Kur for the first time.  Summary: I absolutely loved AV1, but AV2 hit me in a way I wasn't fully prepared for and I couldn't put it down. It was 100% worth every penny, and I would buy it again in a heartbeat.
Great successor of the first game. If you enjoy metroidvanias this is a must play!

This is by no means a continuation of AV1, but is still an absolutely outstanding game by itself. You really dont need to have played AV1 to play this game, as there isnt really any continuation in the gameplay, more so just in the story. This game also has much more emphasis on drone gameplay than the first game, which i quite like. The combat has also changed from ranged to melee (most of the time anyway), which means this game's combat is also significantly harder. There are also no mandatory bosses (excluding the final one) and a lot less of them, which in my opinion was a little bit of a downgrade since I kind of liked AV1's bosses. The graphics are prettier (imo) and except for an area sort of early game that I wont spoil, significantly less retro. It also felt somewhat shorter, which could have just been me not really taking my time, and with the shift to melee combat there are a lot less weapons to pick up, and you can only differ between like 4 in total, which I didn't really like that much.  TL;DR: Really good game, while not being a continuation of Trace's story, still an amazing game in it's own right. 9/10

This is a good game, but with a few problems. Level tree is a bit out of whack, since you spend a great deal of time as the little drone, which makes those upgrades more important. Combat feels very optional given how open the world is. The open world nature makes it sometimes unclear where to go next.  This game is very experimental though - really turns the idea of a metroidvania on it's head. Being able to explore a large portion of the map right from the start with very few powerup gates was really interesting. I also prefer the more natural aesthetics of this game to the more alien of the original. I do recommend giving it a shot.

Wow ok basically this is a really good game so far. It is NOT a copy of the first game, it is very different on its approach, but most of the feel of the first game is still in it. Most negative reviews are about the fact that it's very different and having difficulty increased. It is very different, combat is not that much difficult, as you have a new powerup that lets you 'hack' and work around said op enemies.  Briefly, it is very good. This is a 90% review game downgraded to 80% because it is very different in gameplay. But the actual gameplay and map workaround is VERY nice. It is a solid metroidvania. The music is also quite good.  I'm actually glad that the game went on its own way.
Huge fan of the first AV, but after giving this game 2 chances, it's just not fun.

It was an OK game I guess. I loved it more than Axiom Verge 1, but it could really benefit from about 20% extra content and polish to really shine. As of now it is short and the pacing falls a bit after first half.  It is less about boss fights and being super metroid clone and more about being it's own thing which definitely benefited it in my book. The combat, flow and traversal are super nice. One review mentioned that this game has no double jump and it sold it to me cause I was really curious how they make the overall traversal flow and it is super enjoyable.   The story is nonsense and forgettable, core gameplay is solid though, there is a huge emphasis on hacking and upgrades and there are a few core mechanics that are implemented really well. But overall, as I mentioned, the game is underdeveloped, a lot of things are underused, and some things like breach portals kinda do nothing interesting the moment they start finally clicking in your head.  The biggest gripe I have with AV2 is the same that I had with AV1. There is a point where you don't know where to go, and all interesting side areas are kind of dead ends with small meaningless upgrades. This game would really really need better pacing and some alternative routes and overall a bit of playtesting for the last stretch. It is not as egregious as, say, ESα, but I can see a consistent pattern here.

I went into this game thinking it wouldn't be as good as the first because of the lower score, but I was wrong. I agree that the combat is a little off but I think all that needs to be changed is adding a combat dash or dodge like many other metroidvanias with melee combat have (castlevania, hollow knight, blasphemous) Some combat encounters in this game aren't winnable through playing smart or agile and you end up just having to spend your health as a resource by using your damage i-frames to get in close and button mash, and the button mashing itself can use some qol tuning as well as it's easy to mistime it and have your inputs ignored from mashing too fast. However, everything else in this game is a 10/10. Amazing soundtrack, spritework, environments, and worldbuilding. I expected a direct sequel, but it's instead a prequel that provides more context to the overarching plot. The story isn't over yet so a third game is extremely likely.

[h1]探索の精髄[/h1] 前作を経験済みであれば「[b]前作と異なる形が徹底的に突き詰められていること[/b]」がよくわかって更に面白いので、順番に両方遊ぶのがお勧めです。独自性に満ちたアビリティを獲得することによって世界が再解釈されるダイナミズムは今回も健在です。プラットフォームの縁(へり)にさえ意味が与えられます!  [list] [*]サイドビューアクション [*]表裏二つの世界を切り替える [*]大小二つの自機を切り替える [*]近接攻撃が主体 [*]絵と音がごきげん [*]操作性が良好 [*]最高に楽しい [/list] ……と特徴だけを書き連ねてみると[url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/275390/Guacamelee_Super_Turbo_Championship_Edition/]Guacamelee![/url]を彷彿とさせますが、あちらは注目すべきシンボルと目的地が常に地図上に明示されるので探索感はありません。事あるごとに閉じ込められてBeat ’em upさせられるし、立ち塞がるボスを倒せないうちは先に進めないようになっており、ゲームそのものが「一本道を見た目だけ迷路状に折り曲げたベルトスクロールアクション」として設計されています。 一方で純粋探索型として先鋭化されたAxiom Verge 2は、概ねその逆と考えれば間違いありません。  [h1]敵が鬱陶しい序盤のうちに気付きたいポイント[/h1] [list] [*]機械系の敵は大半が接触ダメージを持たない [*]ドローンの近接武器は曲げられる [*]オプションのアクセシビリティから難易度やコントラストを変えられる [/list] ランキング上で難易度変更時の記録は区別されますが、ゲームスピード倍率や敵ダメージ倍率を最低値に設定した状態でも実績は全部アンロックできるそうです。たとえ無敵モードでも探索難易度は変わりませんし(最序盤で手に入れた謎のアイテムの使い方に私がついに気付いたとき、既にアイテム90%・マップ100%に達していました……)、グローバル実績が示す「プレイヤーの四割以上がエンディングを見られるがアイテム100%達成者は一割未満」という匙加減は、探索ものとしては絶妙じゃないでしょうか。  [h1]気になった点[/h1] [list] [*]複数種類のリマインダーを使い分けたかった。この物足りなさが良いのかもしれませんが。 [*]オープニングデモ「娘と再開」など、日本語テキストに誤字脱字が多い。翻訳そのものはちゃんとしていたし、会話のフォントサイズや書体の視認性は前作よりはっきり素晴らしい。 [*]背景がフラクタルアニメーションしているエリアでFPSが30台にまで低下した。低スペック環境だと危ないかもしれません。 [/list]

Real quick: this is not a continuation of Trace's story. Trace himself appears in logs, and the more attention you pay the more connections you'll see. But if anything, this game's a [i]prequel[/i] rather than a sequel - although that depends on where you're looking... it's complicated.   It also seems like the game's very hit-or-miss. "It's nothing like AV1" is a common complaint, but I'm not sure if I agree; it depends on what sort of comparison you're making. However, the achievement stats paint a completely different picture: 42% of players have beaten the final boss, which is a very high number - even higher than AV1's 30%.  In a sense, it's one of the best Metroidvanias I've ever played. It just grabs you and keeps you in. To me, the main sensation of a Metroidvania is discovery, and I think there's a lot to discover here. Just like in AV1, many hard to reach places contain lore pieces instead of powerups, and almost every single one adds something to the ever-growing picture.  The way your arsenal of traversal abilities grows is also fantastic. Can you believe that this game [i]doesn't have a double jump?[/i] If you're a Metroidvania veteran you're probably used to "predicting" what sort of powerups you'll be getting based on the level design; Well, here the game caught me completely off guard. Excellent game design.  Because of this, the game has some very good pacing. Admittedly, I got lost twice (as in, didn't know where to go), but the map is always there for you, it even has an outline of the level so you can see walls and such. And even if you -do- get lost, you'll surely end up finding a lot of powerups/lore pieces you couldn't reach before, so even that is satisfying.  An arguably controversial element of the game is combat. See, even though most enemies are robots with laser guns, you're mostly stuck with... a melee weapon. In other words, the world is more "metroid" but you are "vania". Peronally, I didn't have much of a problem with it, though - for one, the enemies go down in just a few hits, but also the game feels like you're [i]not supposed[/i] to engage in combat needlessly.  Almost every single enemy has a visible field of view, and they will only start chasing you once they take a good look at you. So it's entirely possible to just sneak/run past many of them, espcially considered that you actually can sneak by walking while crouching. The game's hacking system also allows you to simply take over one of the enemies and have others focus on this one while you make yourself scarce. I mean, you're not a warrior, you're a CEO! It just makes sense.  Oh, right. The protagonist. How many games have you played that star a 40+ woman of indian descent, who's also an evil CEO of a mega-corp? That's so incredibly refreshing, especially since, well, things [i]change[/i] over the course of the game. Indra doesn't manage to be as interesting as Trace (or rather... you know), but let's just say that she's not just some random woman. That's all I'm going to say.  Finally, I think the game's aesthetic is a huge improvement over the first one. The pixel art is incredibly varied and beautiful, the music is wonderful and hauting. Combined with the excellent writing and rich lore, this game's a feast in more ways than just something fun to play. But of course that's a subjective thing.  I've praised the game a lot, but I have to say: all those excellent elements kind of fail to come together. The game is very fun to play, but I don't think it will remain in my mind as a timeless masterpiece, and I can't say why. Still: I think it's very fun, to me, it's a great follow-up to the awesome weirdness that AV1 was.  (oh, and if, for some reason, you read the whole thing but haven't played Axiom Verge 1, get the bundle of both games together and play them back-to-back. It will make much more sense that way)  [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/42922988]Curator page[/url]

No es malo... pero tampoco es Axiom Verge 😩  Mirá, yo venía hypeado. Axiom Verge 1 fue una joyita, así que esperaba algo igual de potente. Pero Axiom Verge 2… se siente como un experimento raro que no terminó de cuajar.  Primero: el combate. En el uno te sentías poderoso, con armas locas y jefes que te hacían sudar. Acá todo se siente blandito. Las peleas casi no tienen peso, y los enemigos no dan ni miedo ni ganas de enfrentarlos. Además, la mayoría del tiempo evitás pelear porque no vale la pena. 😕  El mapa es enorme, sí, pero también es confuso y vacío. Pasás más tiempo caminando que haciendo algo interesante. Y aunque la ambientación “tecno-mística” suena copada en teoría, en la práctica se ve más gris y desganada.  La historia… bueno, es tan críptica que termina sin engancharte. Está bien querer ser misterioso, pero si no das razones para seguir leyendo los logs, uno simplemente deja de hacerlo.  No es que sea terrible, pero se siente como una secuela que perdió el alma del original. Es más una curiosidad que una experiencia divertida.  Puntuación final: 5/10 – “Más sombra que glitch.”

This game is so much better than the first game, it's not even funny. Axiom Verge 1 was a good Metroid clone with unique touches, reminded me of Blossom Tales, which is a competent Zelda clone. This game changes up stuff, though, and is more part of the "evolved" Metroidvania group, which Metroid certainly is NOT.   Areas are a lot more varied, traversal becomes much more satisfying, music is much better (believe it or not). There's less focus on combat, but the exploration is just so, SO much better. Many more shortcuts to unlock, so the map is more interconnected. Abilities are more fleshed out, the story is much more interesting. A lot more detail and context is added to the world, making things feel more real and "alive." Oh, and the game looks GORGEOUS. Oh also, the enemy placement is done better, less haphazardly.   Metroid fans, stick your heads out of your asses please, and stop souring the reputation of this awesome game. Props to Thomas Happ!

Recommended, with some caveats.  I do miss the visual style from the first game, but the music is an unbelievable improvement with better sound design and melodies.  Movement is also a lot more enjoyable and unique than it was in AV1, though having to manually switch between upgrades rather than have them mapped to separate buttons is frustrating, especially when half of the tools in that sub-menu are borderline useless and there's plenty of room on the keyboard.  Level design also feels a lot cleaner, with the moments I got lost definitely feeling more like my fault (I have a tendency to halt progression to explore an area more thoroughly then forget where I was supposed to be going).  A likely factor in this is the map system--not only is it much more useful, but it displays an impressive amount of artistic effort.  The Breach sections aren't super fun to traverse, but the subworld makes for some fun puzzles getting to secret areas.  Though some of my friends didn't enjoy the new combat, I actually rather liked it, though that may be because I prefer getting close and hitting things at a range I can't miss.  Likely as a result of that, I didn't get much use out of the boomerang.  The melee attack of the drone felt kind of unresponsive, as well.  Unfortunately, the second game shares a major problem with the first, in that the story is essentially over once the final upgrade is collected, with a short stint down a previously-inaccessible corridor leading to an impossible-to-lose boss fight.  I think some sort of "final challenge" area that tests the player's skills would have helped the ending to feel more satisfying.  I'm also disappointed we didn't get much info directly addressing the events of the previous game, though I'm willing to concede that I might just have failed to connect the dots.  Maybe Axiom Verge 3 will tie up the stories of Indra and Chaudhari in a neat little bow, but on the other hand I'd also respect the decision to make the AV series a set of disconnected narratives in a vast and confusing universe--one that has been expanded significantly by this game's plot.  Rambling aside, I recommend this game.  It doesn't get everything right, but it gets a lot more right than its predecessor and is genuinely enjoyable most of the time.

A continuação do Axiom Verge modifica bastante a dinâmica do primeiro jogo e pode demorar um tempo até engrenar. Os cenários alienígenas fechados e sombrios dão lugar a um planeta inóspito, quase incolor, e a ação frenética do AV1, à exploração. Considerando que o Thomas Happ não dá ponto sem nó e o terceiro jogo da série vem aí, fica a recomendação, pois Axiom Verge é uma das grandes obras feitas recentemente por aqui.  O começo não é dos mais encantadores, realmente, mas à medida em que a história vai se amarrando e a exploração dando resultado, a imersão é tão intensa quanto a do primeiro Axiom Verge. O que parece um passo pra trás na verdade é um bom complemento ao AV1, e desde que AV2 seja aproveitado assim, é um jogo inesquecível. Que venha o 3.

Axiom Verge 2 is an incredible follow up to the first title and faithful to the Metroidvania genre.  Just with the first game, it has good visuals, cool abilities, and I daresay; a better soundtrack than the original.  This game does not hold your hand, so it challenges you to find a way to progress.  My one complaint is that the bosses are entirely too easy: no challenge, no need for strategy, just spam your attack away to victory.  Otherwise, this is a fantastic game, and I hope to see more from this solo developer.  Tom Happ, don’t ever stop making games.

Axiom Verge titles are slowly becoming one of my favourite series to play, the world and it's soundtrack is just amazing, Axiom Verge really shows within it's story/gameplay and the world just how much time and effort Thomas Happ has put into Axiom Verge as a series and he really shows how much he loves creating MetroidVanias. I for one am a huge fan of these games and 2 whilst it might not be considered "Better" by a lot of players, it does however come with it's own set of perks that make this standout compared to it's predecessor. I really liked how Happ has made Axiom 2 feel like Metroid Prime 2: Echoes with the need of constantly having to enter the "breach" to continue further in the game and even for exploration purposes.  Without spoiling anything I love that this game starts and instantly feels like it has nothing to do with Trace and by reading notes and stuff through exploring it is nice to see how Happ is world building for Indra and how it all adds together in the end by showing you where this game lies within it's timeline. Axiom Verge 2 in my opinion is harder than Axiom 1 because you are forced into Melee combat over ranged and that's okay imo, every enemy can be avoided and can be walked by, bosses are not mandatory and I liked that as I was aloud to come and go when I pleased to defeat the enemies in my own time.  The one perk Axiom Verge 2 has is if you find yourself to be struggling at any point just access the Accessibility settings and adapt the gameplay to however you want, Do you want tough boss fights? Do you want them to be easy so you can just go to the next area? It's all there and I love that this is an option for anyone who isn't the best at either the combat or platforming, so that's a thumbs up from me. The music in this series of games are just inspiring to me, I really like how the style changes so heavily from the 1st game and it still feels like it fits the setting, some are annoying and I don't expect every piece of music to be amazing, but they all fit the environments they are in and I like that. I just love Techno and Happ really gets some good songs off throughout. Keep it up and i'll keep buying your games is all i'm saying.  Really hope that there will be a Third game down the line, These are the type of gems that gaming needs and I think everyone should try them.  9/10 Really really enjoyed Axiom Verge 2 and I will encourage anyone to try them and to enjoy them!
Very different from the original Axiom Verge, but still a great metroidvania on its own right!

От начала до конца почти одни те же. 5) Очередной перебор спрятанного никчёмного лора в ОГРОМНЕЙШЕЙ карте. Зачастую возвращает тебя на 3-5 сохранений до этого, если ты покидал игру. Это как? В общем и целом игра явно не стоит тех денег, которые за неё просят. Худшее продолжение!!!

Axiom Verge 2 has a great retro feel and offers alot of fun exploring the game world. It's a must have for metroidvania fans. The developer Tom Happ provides enjoyable pixel artwork, music and gameplay, woven into a solid level design. Some things feel a bit tricky, some parts are strange, but it's an overall great experience. The story itself is interesting and matches the sci-fi genre well. Collecting all the crumps of informations stored on collectibles is pretty time consuming, though. The replay value is mediocre for casual players because of its high emphasis of drone shape.  Compared to the previous game "Axiom Verge", it feels like quite some technical improvements and functions found their way into the game. However, the style of the successor game "Axiom Verge 2" has completely changed. It's far less about weapons and shooting enemies and much more avoiding fights, because that consumes alot of time and fighting doesn't feel rewarding, most of the time. The content of the game is fair, although it seems smaller than the first game.  Axiom Verge 2 has few downsides. None of these impose a reason for serious complaints, though. There are a few strange decisions regarding the gameplay. No matter what players fight, it takes plenty of hits. It doesn't make fights more interesting or difficult. It just takes time and most players will soon prefer to just jump over and past enemies.  After a while, the most interesting shape to use is the drone for movability, speed, combat and almost everything else. Players will also find themself teleporting all over the place and worlds so often and so fast. This can pull a player out of immersion. It can also drop tension and excitement level to zero, because character death has pretty much no impact. This exposes etremely at the last "boss fight". That fight wasn't exciting or interesting, but it was stylish.  Axiom Verge 2 has far less equipment and weapons than the previous game. In fact, it itsn't much but that's not a bad thing. The introduction of a skill system for improvements is simple and alright. It's nothing refined but it works just fine. A surprise is the limited utilization of equipment. At some point, players can optain a weapon (hammer) and an ability to slam down upon enemies or the ground. It could be great to break through walls but instead, the game is much more about teleporting around and switching between two world for limited puzzle solving. It's still great work but the game does not live up to its potential.  To sum it up: Axiom Verge 2 has a few quirks and oddities but it's fun and stylish. If we disregard "graphic blenders", the game could easily challenge many top titles of genres "platformer" and "metroidvania" and beat them. I can't say it beats the first Axiom Verge game but it does a great job.

so i understand where a lot of ppl are coming from when theyre disappointed by the lack of [insert any AV1 or typical metroidvania thing here], but i feel like its unfair to rate it based on what you [i]expected[/i] rather than what you actually [i]got[/i]. hell, i cant deny that its not what i expected, but the more i sit on this i feel like thats kind of the point, or perhaps the general design philosophy behind both this and AV1.  what this game does have is fun movement and satisfying exploration. if youve ever played zelda LTTP/LBW, this game has similar stuff going on, except its a metroidvania. weaving in and out of the breach is fun. collecting items and backtracking all over the place for them whenever you pick up a new ability is easy thanks to the checkpoints littered all over the place and fast travel to use them. the drone is back and better than ever and learning to use it to its fullest to traverse the world is where its at imo.   also wanna briefly mention, music/visuals/story are all great. the worldbuilding is cool and i hope to see more from thomas happ where he continues to develop it. i know its not everyones cup of tea but if you really dont like it its easy to ignore.  give this a shot if you like the exploration and platforming aspects of metroidvanias

I have thoroughly enjoyed the game and that's the issue. Its kinda short for my standards but considering what the dev has been through irl, he made a very good successor to the first AV expanding on the story and retaining the atmosphere while also not making it mandatory to play the first game for those who still haven't. You should btw, the first is equally good. Still hoping for a third one. Both games are more metroid and vania second and they deserve more praise for how underrated I feel they are.

This is a good game, but I wouldn't call it great.  I really enjoyed some of the original game play features that encouraged exploration, such as being able to switch to a drone to be able to access new areas and go scouting, and the addition of having a parallel world that linked with the main map and made you think how to use them together to access unexplored locations and progress the story.  The combat, however, was pretty basic, but I did enjoy the hacking feature.  Overall, this game had enough to keep me wanting to play until the end and I would like to see some of these game play features in upcoming titles, but implemented with a little more depth.  My suggestion is wait for a sale and pick up this game.  It's a fun one to play on Steam Deck!

The first one was fascinating because it left so many questions unanswered. It had an air of mystery, existential terror, and futility of one's struggle no matter how hard one tried. The sequel is boring AF because it wants too desperately to create a precise, geometric, cultural narrative that leaves absolutely no room for speculation, wonder, or fantasy. Mechanically, it is wonderful. That is all it has going for it.

Потрясающий сиквел. Раскрывающий многие вопросы первой части. В этот раз разработчик все сделал лучше, больше и доступнее для игрока. 90% загадок можно разгадать не прибегая к шаманству из интернета. Мне только два раза за игру пришлось открывать гайд, чтобы понять, как открыть тайный проход. И нет, самому догадаться точно нельзя. Эти два места расположены в конце карты, где идет спуск в океан, и в середине карты - большой водопад. Можете не бегать вокруг, там на геймпаде в определённом месте, нужно набрать определённую комбинацию. Все остальное открывается по ходу сюжета и ериеобритения нужных способностей. Чтобы получиться большее удовольствие и не растягивать хронометраж, дойдите по сюжету до получения превращения в облако наночастиц и тогда можете отправляться в путь - рассказывать все секреты. Это практически в конце игры, но без этого вы не сможете понять, какая именно способность вам нужна. Это последняя сюжетная способность, больше не будет и можно спокойно изучать мир.   А изучать там есть что, все секреты сделаны максимально логично, кроме двух описанных выше. Все секреты в таких местах, что к ним можно быстро добраться. В отличии от других игр, где надо пробежать в самый конец карты, чтобы получить очередной айтем.  Боевика отличная, правда я играл через взлом, и практически не использовал оружие, читал в обзорах, что многим не понравилась боевика, мол в первой части она была лучше. Полностью не согласен, это одна из немногих игр, где можно использовать что тебе нравится, здесь нет правильного варианта игры.  И последнее сюжет, все подается через диалоги и записки. Сюжет отличный, особенно для фанатов первой части. Мне как любителю фантастики и человеку, который не может найти хороший фильм и сериал с данном жанре, просто было как мед, что первая часть, что вторая. Лучшая на мой взгляд фантастическая дилогия на данный момент.  Ну и для тех, кто все таки ничего не понял по сюжету. Likoris, на Ютубе полностью разобрал эти две части, всех приглашаю к просмотру, откроется с новой стороны игра.
Шикарная игрушка, именно такой и должна быть метроидвания, с кучей бектрекинга и тд и тп.

I enjoyed Axiom Verge 1 (bought the bundle with both games), thought it was pretty-good-but-not-great, and got enough out of my playthrough that I would recommend it but I'm probably not interested in playing through again. Axiom verga 2, ahora con una mina como prota xD  Al igual que su antecesor, esta vez tenemos armas a melee y un arma arrojadiza (que casi ni lo vas usar) para atacar a los enemigos. A diferencia del primero, este lo note un poco mas pulido, pero a la vez un poco mas vació, pocos enemigos y pocos secretos para descubrir, salvo algunos que necesitas hacer algo especial. Puzzle casi inexistentes.   La historia, bueno, que se yo, es media complicada así que no le di bola... Solo entendí que sos una mina que va a la Antártida en busca de tu madre desparecida en un laboratorio que experimentaba con portales (tipo Black Mesa xD), y al llegar hablas con una PC del gobierno y te envía a otro mundo que párese ser la antigua Babilonia solo con robots y nanobots y cosas raras.   La jugabilidad al principio se siente algo lenta, pero mas adelante cuando vayas mejorando al personaje se vuelve mas rápido y fácil. Los jefes están por ahí dando vuelta, no tiene una arena especial así que capaz mates a uno y ni cuenta te darías hasta que te salga el logro. Ninguno fue difícil para mi.   Como todo metroidvania, a medidas que avances y desbloquees nuevas habilidades podrás ingresar a nuevas partes del mapa que antes eran inaccesibles, al igual que algunos secretos. Este juego se divine en 2 mapas, uno "real" y el otro como un "sueño" donde solo puedes usar el dron. Combinados tienes bastante para explorar.  Algunos cambios notable en este juego es que tienes teleport en muchas partes, así no tienes que ir de una punta a la otra en búsqueda de tesoros y esas cosas como en el primero. Tienes un item que lo consigues muy avanzado que te indica si te olvidaste algún item o algo en un mapa (solo te dice, pero no especifica donde, tendrás que ver una guía o algo), y el mapa tiene coordenadas, para que sea mas fácil usar las guías. Ah, y algunas opciones de "accesibilidad" que básicamente es hacer mas fácil o difícil el juego, como aumentar o disminuir el daño tuyo o de los enemigos.  El estilo artístico tipo Pixel combinado con la antigua Persia esta muy bien logrado, algunos enemigos tienen diseños buenos.  Si jugaste al primero deberías jugar a este, aunque la experiencia no sera la misma, al principio no me gustaba, pero al final me termino gustando. Dependiendo de que si decides explorar o no el mapa, puedes tardar varias horas, aunque hay speedruns de menos de 2 horas.  Nota:8/10  https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2940875793

Thoughts after finishing my first play through of Axiom Verge 2. As someone who enjoys slower paced exploration and world building heavy games, this one was thoroughly enjoyable and engaging. As an older gamer with limited time for hobbies, I have a very low tolerance for games that waste my time, and tend to bounce off them fairly quickly. Axiom Verge 2 kept my attention however, and I saw it through to the end fairly rapidly.  What It Did Well - The Mesopotamian influences made for a compelling backdrop, reflected in the writing, art and music. It felt like a cohesive and fascinating world to engage with. - Just the right amount of speculative sci-fi elements without leaning too much into exposition. - The body horror and existential dread themes were sold very well through a thoughtfully designed ludo-narrative dynamic. As the protagonist grew stronger and became more of a game character, they gradually lost their humanity in a sense. - Early on in the game, there was a real sense of tension and need to use stealth and hacking to manage enemy encounters.  - Nicely crafted spritework and animations. Each area was well distinguished with stark color pallets and varied level layouts. - Catchy background music with a few true earworms that never got too obnoxious. - Several upgrades with unexpected and clever ways to interact with the environments and overcome obstacles. I loved every "aha" moment when you realized how to reach a previously seemingly out of reach spot on the map! - Mastery of the mechanics sometimes allowed you to make your own shortcuts, which is always fun.  Missed Opportunities - Would have liked to see more aspects of the world anchored in lore. Who were the optional bosses, and their role in the ecosystem or history of their locations? How come the world was layered in the way it was, and the sky wrapping around itself when at the lowest layer? - Early on there were interactions between the Arms, but that was quickly dropped. Something like optional questlines for each Arm to develop their abilities and learn about their pasts would have been neat! - Nearly impossible to avoid taking damage from most enemies, since they had attacks with zero signaling or build up. - The combat became trivialized too quickly, with the lack of any real i-frames or stamina system making rushing in and spamming fast attacks the most efficient way to tackle 90% of combat scenarios.  - Conversely, the hacking system felt underpowered and hardly worth investing upgrade points into. For instance, the (expensive) ALLEGIANCE hack would have the enemy stay at their current route rather than follow you around, which made it next to worthless.  Nitpicks - In the back half of the game, opening up shortcuts was entirely pointless.  - The wall climb ability was way too sticky. In tight situations, it was too easy to get stuck on the wall when you wanted to do combat or platforming. - The slow walk mechanic was pointless, and was sometimes detrimental when you tilted the controller stick in just the wrong way to trigger it when you wanted to actually run. - Similarly, even the slightest upwards angle to the stick would have you climb stairs when you actually wanted to walk horizontally. - The unskippable save point "fountain" animation became very old very quickly. Please allow us to skip or speed it up!
