Ключевое: это НЕ продолжение Axiom Verge, это просто игра от того же автора, вдобавок, поливающая вас буквально с первого экрана СЖВшной парашей про гениальных женщин и нацменьшинства

Seven years ago Axiom Verge got released and while I had a fair share of criticisms towards it, it really was a fantastic metroidvania. Without being too novel, it had several extremely clever additions to the formula of the genre and apart from that was a solid game with unforgettable soundtrack and cool visual style. A year ago Axiom Verge 2 got released on several platforms and had far less excited reviews overall, most emphasizing the change in focus of the game from action to exploration. Which excited me, because I love exploration in metroidvania games and the combat in the first title was my least favorite thing about it. Now that I’ve finally had a chance to play the sequel myself, I have to say that I love it even more than the original. Yet, I can see how it’s not going to be for everyone.  What haven’t changed is how cool the visual style of the game is, with creepy biomechanical Giger-esque elements (though this time this is somewhat de-emphasized), and how incredible the soundtrack is. As with the first game – I urge you to check out the music even if you have not interest in the game, it’s great. Maybe less consistently awesome, but still fantastic. The metroidvania basics also remain, with a map that you gradually fill, while getting new abilities and finding optional upgrades. Though, this time there’s a simple skill tree added on top, that is unlocked via one of the types of optional items.  But the way the game plays and feels is vastly different. Where Axiom Verge was mostly about tighter corridors, following the classic Metroid structure, and also heavily emphasized action, a crazy amount of different guns and many boss battles, Axiom Verge 2 is all about exploration. You still have combat, but it’s mainly in the melee range and isn’t really necessary, apart from clearing the way. There are boss enemies, but all apart from 2 boss encounters are entirely optional. And the world is extremely open at almost all times, giving you plenty of possibilities to explore and find cool stuff. There are clear goals, but you can often ignore them and try to explore as much as possible without going for the main story. Though, main goals is what often gives you upgrades that open up your possibilities.  This world is what, I fear, may overwhelm a lot of people. There are only a couple of moments where it’s very unclear on how you should get to where you need to, but that could be enough for some to get frustrated. But there at the same time, when you accept that it’s all about exploring and looking for interesting discoveries and possibilities, the game is amazing. The idea of a parallel world here is very reminiscent of the Spectral Realm from Legacy of Kain titles, meaning that after a certain point you start thinking of the entire huge world map in parallel versions (which is helpfully visualized by the game) and a lot of navigation puzzles are solved in genuinely inventive and fun ways due to that.  I guess that’s what Axiom Verge 2 is in the first place – inventive. Where apart from corruption and “save the progress of the map and items even when dying” the original title was a pretty conventional Super Metroid-like game, this sequel does everything to be something else. And apart from the La-Mulana titles, I think the last time I’ve played a metroidvania that was so wildly different and exciting was Toki Tori 2+ from 9 years ago. Another title that didn’t click with many.  And I can see that coming from the action filled original to the sequel is going to be a shock. Especially since I also feel that the enemies can be get really obnoxious at the beginning as you will undoubtedly be taking damage and dying as part of the experience. But, once you accept that this is part of the game, that avoiding the combat where possible is a better choice, that it’s all about the exploration and the navigation, the game gets very enjoyable… if you’re into this sort of thing in the first place, of course.  I loved Axiom Verge 2 and at one moment, when I realized how a new ability suddenly gave me so many crazy possibilities to explore, was unlike anything I’ve felt from games in a long time. Because more often than not metroidvanias are predictable, as great as they get despite this. But Axiom Verge 2 is anything but predictable. It’s a bit rough, it’s not for people who like action adventure platformers for the “action” part, not the “adventure platforming”. But it’s fresh, inventive and absolutely amazing if you love exploring these metroidvania worlds.

I really enjoyed this game and loved all the little nods to the first one. The world building is great and I appreciate how different it looks from AV 1. Overall the game is very fun and has much better exploration mechanics. The combat feels much more like Castlevania since it is primarily melee and it works very well. Before the proper review, let me preface this with something more related to the general identity of this series rather than the qualities of this game specifically: Axiom Verge is and always was supposed to be a series of games about individual stories that can stand out on their own while being a part of something bigger, each game expanding on a monumental amount of lore by making you rethink about any certainties you had about the rules of the extended multiverse this series takes place in. It might take a long while before we find out about what happens after the events of Axiom Verge 1; nevertheless, the events and notes in this game greatly expand on what Axiom Verge 1 had done, while being seemingly unrelated at first. If you only care about Doctor Trace's Silly Adventures 2, look elsewhere, but if the writing of Axiom Verge 1 entranced you into wanting to know more about the inner mechanisms behind all that is Axiom Verge, this game is for you.   Now onto a proper review of the game: I'm with the majority of the people who prefers Axiom Verge 1 to this game, as it sits in my top 10 favorite games, but don't let anyone tell you that this game is anything less than phenomenal.  The combat isn't exactly its strong point, but if you care enough to put an ounce of strategy into what you do rather than slamming your face onto each enemy's attacks, you may realize that there's often many, many different ways you could possibly go about every encounter in the game. More variety in weapons would have been greatly appreciated, but as it stands, fighting in this game is fun and by no means bad. That being said, all boss fights, except maybe the final one, are very underwhelming.  The world is vast and connected in an innumerable amount of ways, with shortcuts appearing very often all the way to the end of the game, and while each room isn't as memorable as perhaps the ones in Axiom Verge 1 would be, the organic way Kiengir is built makes it always a pleasurable experience to explore its various locations - and while the Breach follows different design philosophies, its quality is no less. To wrap it all up is a beautiful map menu that makes sure you get a good feeling of how each different biome is shaped and connected to the others, making it hard to forget where each thing is.  The pixel art in this game is some of the best I've ever seen in indie games, and because of it multiple times have I had to stop for a second and remind myself that only one person is responsible for the entirety of this game. The soundtrack is no less, and while it doesn't reach peaks as high as Axiom Verge 1's collection of absolute bangers, you'll find that this game's different approach to its OSTs has plenty to offer.  When it comes to length, this game is admittedly not very long, so whether it's worth its value to you is very subjective to what you want out of a game. If you only care about beating the final boss and then never open it again, I strongly recommend against buying it, as it will last you very little, and you will miss out on most of the things that make this game as good as it is. If you care for a single 100% completion rate playthrough, keep in mind that it will take more or less 10 hours to complete, so whether you should buy it or not depends on just how much you're interested about what this game's has to offer, and if it's worth it for you to pretty much spend 2$ for each hour you play. If you're interested in getting all achievements (which realistically will take you at least 2 playthroughs, one 100% and another quick one), or if you simply care to play this game multiple times, whether to speedrun it, or just to replay it and doing stuff in a different order to have a different experience with it, then I fully believe that buying this game is more than worth it. And if you're interested in this kind of stuff, the community is working on all sorts of mods, and may one day create a randomizer just like they did for Axiom Verge 1! But that might take a long while, so you're free to not let that change your judgement for the time being.  All in all, while I do agree that this game is imperfect, I fully believe that it is an incredible videogame experience. Is it one of the best metroidvanias ever made? That's hard to say - especially because the genre is full of earthshattering games that will always keep you wishing for more! But it's a hell of a good one, and no matter how long it takes until we get another game that takes place in such a complicated multiverse, I'm very excited to keep following this series, because if there's something I know, is that Thomas Happ always Axioms a good Verge.  9/10

It's great, and just as interestingly experimental and cerebral as the first one.  Plenty to explore, find, do and contemplate, and I'm happy to see more storytelling in this particular genre.  Don't let the whiners fool you, they set themselves up with their own poor expectations and when they disappointed themselves they're taking it out on this game.

I've played this game twice over before on other platforms! And I have to say, I absolutely *love* this game. It's such an amazing world to walk around in and just drink the atmosphere in, with so much to learn and piece together about the world you're in. It's one of the few ever pieces of media that I started right back up from the start after finishing it, and one of so few games I've put the effort in to try and 100% myself. The visuals, music and writing all leave such a mark on me: playing this game changes my dreams.  The melee combat is absolutely brillant. You really learn how to use movement to close distance, throw yourself into the danger and take high risks to end the battle quickly! Don't hold yourself back -- there's so much to master with how you move and place your hits, so much to learn about how enemies behave and react to you, about how movement *is* combat here. One of my absolute favorite moments was starting a new game and realizing just how differently I fought compared to my first time through in those early areas!  Maybe it doesn't capture everybody from the Axiom Verge 1 audience, but this game is something complete and special in its own right. I really connected with it, and it's okay if not everybody does.

Its a great Metroidvania.  The art is pretty and the songs are pretty catchy. Item progression is constant so things keep interesting for the most part. Unfortunately combat is mostly melee, with a few ranged options but they're not very good.  Fans from the first game however will feel a bit sad that the "Giger-esque" feel to the game is mostly gone.   Still a great game and I can't wait for more from Mr. Happ.

It isn't what you'd expect after Axiom Verge 1, but it's not bad either.  The second installment of Axiom Verge takes place seemingly in a different time and location, and operates on different styles of action.  So if you were expecting a continuation of Axiom Verge 1, shed that thought.  It is different in terms of it's pacing, and expect to go back and forth a lot between areas as you progress (typical of a Metroidvania style game).  However, the graphics still feel worthy of the Axiom Verge title when it comes to it.  The music is another set of bangers like AV1, the graphics are very similar in nature (old school pixel style), and the combat system definitely provides a slight challenge above run-and-gun as AV1 presented.  A lot of people who were expecting a "proper sequel" may be disappointed here, but if you shed the expectation and dig in, you will find this possibly adds to the universe in such a way where you play and study both, it's probably leading to a culmination in a future title.  I do highly suggest this title to anyone who loved the first entry.  I first bought on EGS, and finally have it on my preferred platform, and still worth it.

This is a really fun metroidvania that has the feel of AV1 without playing exactly like the original. While I would have liked a continuation of AV1's story, it's still a fun prequel.  The music in many areas and the sounds makes it really feel like an axiom verge sequel/prequel. The weapon system revamp and ability points are a nice addition, as well as the larger mini bot focus (I played as it a lot once I got a few certain upgrades). I didn't even know it was an EG exclusive until I read some reviews, thought it just came out recently (marketing clearly wasn't the best) If you are looking for a metroidvania with a focus on exploration and secrets this will definitely scratch that itch. The only downside I really have is that there are basically no bosses.  There are large enemies that seem like mini-bosses, but they go down as quick as some of the regular enemies.  Even the final boss doesn't really feel like one (think steamworld dig 1 last boss).

I was hesitant to try Axiom Verge 2 due to its mixed reception, but grabbing it in the bundle with the first game (mostly played on Switch) I was pleasantly surprised with the game. Admittedly - I didn't enjoy the first hour or so due to how radically different it was from the first game, some decisions annoyed me (Skill trees - not a fan of them in Vanias.) Background rooms to explore, harder to see/navigate mini map.    Eventually the game clicked and I found myself enjoying it a lot and beat the rest of the game in a single sitting, I have some gripes - Drone usage is a bit much, less interesting abilities in comparison to the first game, less weapons - Whilst for some this could be a good thing, I do prefer how many you had access to in AV1 and how unique some of them were - hacker gun, ion beam, flamethrower, SMG that shoots ice, richocet -focused gun, etc. They were neat, meanwhile AV2 is just 'Axe, Axe2 and Axe 3' with Axe 2 being the best out of the 3 due to its faster and more lethal attacks.  Overall, yeah I'd say AV2's worth a buy - just hope that AV3 is a sequel to both games or something, I don't want another prequel I want to see what happened to Trace and Indra not a third entirely new protagonist.
Yeah, of course I'm going to recommend it. I bought it for Switch and Steam. Played it on Switch last year.
If you're a fan of the original Axiom Verge, you're going to want to play this one.

I really wanted to like it but this "sequel" is not even close to AV1 in terms of gameplay. The biggest source of disappointment for me is the fighting and enemy placement.  There are a lot of cheap shots at the player, ranging from hidden enemies in tiles that just bop out of them and track you down which are unavoidable, drones that shoot at you and mimic your vertical velocity, meaning if you move up so do they. You will be barely able to catch them with the forced melee approach this game has. Every other grounded enemy forces you to trade blows, you can not dodge, you cant block or even spot dodge, you have to trade the blow. Every other option is not viable because they are just so freaking fast. A lot of enemies will flail at you (spiders in a jungle) or shoot at you from off screen. The projectiles will even have an aoe area. The mines that track you down also have a big aoe which means if you attack them you will take a blow too and they take off a considerable amount of health.   The fighting which happens in every room is just frustrating, its not even hard as most of the time you can just run past the enemies but I dont think that is what the developer wanted.  The game also has unnecessary game mechanics. Why can the main character crouch-walk but not move into any vent unless he transforms into the drone? On top of that you will actually bug out and get out of bounds if you try to crouch into crevices that seem like you can get into but you can not. The deadzone for the crouch-walk is also too big. Most of the time you hold the stick to the left or right and only mildly up and down and you end up crouch-walking. Absolutely unnecessary.  The Breach sections with the drone are also laggy probably due to the camera not updating properly. The tileset seems to lag behind, I do not know if this is what the developer wanted but it does not feel good if everything is jittering. Get your integer movement speeds right :p  The overall story is kinda neat but the protagonist is somewhat unlikeable.  5/10, I waited a year to get disappointed
Axiom Verge 2 is an absolutely brilliant sequel. Mr. Happ, you mad genius, thank you for creating this experience.
I like it. reminds me of the old days. Very playable. Some very nice mechanics.

I bought Axiom Verge 2 the day it came out on Steam. At the time it had mixed reviews. I was a little worried at the time, but I still bought it out of faith in the developer. And I'm happy to say that it exceeded my expectations.  It's a wonderful expansion of the original, in terms of both mechanics and setting. The breach is like an awesome, scaled up version of Axiom Verge's hidden worlds. It's totally mind-blowing, and I don't want to spoil anything by explaining how it works. The Worldstream is also further explained and explored and it's gotta be one of the most compelling sci-fi settings that I've seen.  I was very satisfied with Axiom Verge 2, but much like its predecessor, it's left me hungry for more. The mystery runs deep, and I can't wait to discover more in the next installment.
I 100% this game with all achievements on Epic and im going to 100% this game again on Steam! Cant wait for another sequel!!!

Already played through it several times on the EGS version, but I bought it here to support Tom Happ. I love both AV1 and AV2, but they are undeniably different games - if you go into the sequel expecting more of the exact same gameplay as the first, you may be disappointed. However, if you enjoyed the first game at all, I strongly encourage you to give Axiom Verge 2 a try with an open mind - it iterates on the gameplay and world design in some fascinating ways. There is more focus on exploration and navigation instead of boss fights, which I personally prefer. Though, I do rather miss the large variety of weapons that Trace had at his disposal.  In many ways, what captures my attention about Axiom Verge and its sequel the most is the story and worldbuilding. I've always felt that there's a qualitative difference between a story built on a comprehensive foundation and one with just the appearance of one. Even if the player never learns all or even most of it, the fact that the fictional world is built on a common truth, rather than a cool-looking empty box, makes things feel more real. When the game is throwing out fictional terms, references to places and events, they feel like more than just set dressing. They feel like glimpses at a larger world. Enough to tantalize and inspire the imagination without simply infodumping. Into The Breach and Dark Souls, for example, do an excellent job of this. So do both Axiom Verge games.  Like the first game, the plot may seem rather bare-bones and straightforward if you don't pay much attention. However, diligence and attention to detail is rewarded - especially if the first game is fresh in your mind, the timeline of Kiengir and how it links to the plot of Axiom Verge will come into focus, and AV2 fleshes out the series universe in some new and fascinating ways. I love digging into game lore like this, and if you enjoy constructing fictional timelines and looking up Akkadian definitions, I think you will too. Personally, I'm crossing my fingers to see Axiom Verge 3 a few years down the line. Very few fictional worlds capture my imagination like Axiom Verge's does, and I can't wait to dive back in.

Pros: - Controls are great. - The game is rock solid (aside from some issues on Steam release which were fixed the same day). - Exploration and "puzzles" are enjoyable. - More world-building with some cross-over with the first game. There's some Weird S**t going on! It makes me want another sequel just for more story.  Neutral: - Bosses don't gate progression. You can ignore them if you want. - Hacking doesn't feel as important as you would think it should be, and straight up doesn't apply to about half the game.  Cons: - Almost all of the secret items are just multiple copies of the same exact item, which stops being exciting quickly.  The first game had a TON of weapons in the secret areas that made looking for secrets a lot more fun (even if a lot of those weapons weren't particularly useful, they were different). - Dark areas aren't fun except in horror games. This isn't a horror game. - Remember that one song with the "singing" (annoying wailing) in the first game that didn't fit with the other songs?  This game inverted that pattern; it's mostly (slightly better but still annoying) singing and only a few chiptune songs.

Axiom Verge 2 is an enjoyable game, it was relatively quick to complete with about 10 hours to get to the ending. In order to better convey my opinions about this game, I'll explain my thoughts through answering these questions:   1) How does Axiom Verge 2 compare to Axiom Verge 1?  AV2 has the same hallmarks as AV1 when it comes to theme and genre; its a metroidvania with a focus on unconventional power-ups. Much like its predecessor, Axiom Verge 2 also lightly explores the ideas of what it is to be human and uses its gameplay to convey that narrative.  Graphically, AV2 has more elaborate sprite art and beautiful animation for the especially large creatures and bosses that roam the world. The Background vistas are also very well done, but pale in comparison to the abstract and otherworldly vibe that Axiom Verge 1 had set up as its alien world.  The music also has shifted in tone, its more optimistic than eerie and has more use of instruments that add to the more naturalistic setting, though there are places where chip-tune music is present and a few melodies and remixes are used at key moments in the plot.   2) What new mechanics are there compared to the original?  AV2 seems to have taken inspiration from the Castlevania games in comparison to the originals homage to Metroid. Instead of relying on a plethora of ranged weapons like AV1, AV2 has a focus on melee weapons and later on uses a more conventional fast travel system that was absent in the first game.  There is also inspiration taken from Guacamelee, where the protagonist gets to explore Two large and interconnected maps that interlink between themselves and the player can use this dimension hopping to reach more areas along the critical path.  Beyond that, one of the most considerate things added to this game are the accessibility features; you can decrease or increase the difficulty in a manner of ways, varying from weapon damage to damage negation. Alongside this, the bosses you encounter across the game are optional - you do not have to fight them at all and can walk right past them and carry onwards.   3) Can you Play AV2 without previously playing AV1?  Yes. While the plot of AV2 does have a few minor ties to the previous game, the plot and world are largely self-contained, making a good game to enjoy if you're curious about trying it out. The plot feels more like a back seat focus, but that isn't really a problem in my opinion as the gameplay is enjoyable.  Overall I'd recommend getting this, whether you're a fan of the first game in this series or wanting to find a Metroidvania to play that breaks the staple of traditional power ups.

Пройдя игру до конца почти на одном дыхании я искренне не понимаю негативных комментариев в её сторону. Я переиграл во множество метроидваний, и axiom verge 2 нашлось что предложить мне: а именно атмосферу исследования, интересный лор, порой неожиданный сюжет. Боёвка не может похвастаться изысканностью, но в отличие от первой части на ней нет акцента - сюжетные боссы прощают всё, но от этого (как ни удивительно) скучно играть не становится. Я однозначно рекомендую игру, но имейте ввиду, что это идейный продолжатель первой части, а не длц к ней.
If your significant other loved Axiom Verge 1, get them the second one for christmas or the sorts. They will be the happiest person alive and I am living proof of it.

当成一个新游戏，不要和初代比，那就是一个非常值得推荐的游戏。西卡西！！！！这是不可能的（笑）……  整体游玩下来，剧情方面和初代[spoiler]基本没有什么关系，看完初代的结尾，满怀希望，理所应当的认为2代是初代的后续，结果根本不是。但是虽然文字剧情中，好像和初代没有任何关系，但是个人感觉2代更像初代的前传，或者说初代主角去到那个世界之前的样子，因为游戏中有大量的，怎么说呢，初代怪物的幼体，而且隙间怎么看都是初代的东西，地图感觉更像是初代的低分辨率版本，我们碰到的除了自己以外的人类，等探索到一定地步再回来找他们，会发现他们都会变成初代的第一个boss的幼体，无理猜测人类来到这边到了一定时间会变异，而2代主角因为被换了身所以逃过一劫，或者他们被谁抓住改造了……后边还有蛋状复活器和小型的2代主角可用的蛋状复活器，这个想不出来太好的解释……2代主角最后留在了这个世界，通过ED看到了全貌，一头的大波浪，看起来特别像初代里嗝屁的那个，以上。 [/spoiler]  [h1]说完剧情了，说说优点：[/h1] 1---和初代比，有中文！！！而且本地化的很棒，对话很有那味！！！ 2---boss战很容易； 3---可以到处传送简直开心的一批，再也不用和初代一样因为突然想起来哪里有个什么东西跑半天的图； 4---有可以带在身上的血包，配合可以随时传送，再也不用担心手滑死掉没存档，只能跑图回来的痛苦； 5---地图的显示更加直观，不用担心玩的时间太久忘了哪里是哪里； 6---无人机的操作更加多样化，能飞真的超酷的好吗； 7---双地图的解密还是很有意思的，尤其是主副地图互相对应，提供了新的解密思路。  [h1]至于缺点，还是那句话，不和初代比，很多都不是缺点：[/h1] 1---boss战太简单了[strike] （我就是这么矛盾） [/strike]，基本没有走位，直接硬刚能解决80%的敌人，剩下的19%靠背包里的血瓶奶自己一下可以硬刚解决，剩下的1%动脑子地形杀[strike]（这1%好像得放到优点啊） [/strike]； 2---敌人和boss的种类和初代比太少了，翻来覆去就那么几个； 3---整体的升级系统很鸡肋，除了血上限会肉眼可见的涨，剩下的感觉升级的强度不大，一开始需要打好几下的小兵，升级了还是得打好几下，侵入系统更鸡肋，升级基本只是为了开几个门，侵入敌人真是可有可无，小怪侵入用处不大，变成队友仿佛突然弱智，大怪侵入也没啥用，也就减速啥的，我不侵入硬刚都打死了，更重要的，侵入还有数值条，并不能大手一挥，都变成自己的队友，也不能和初代一样，看见个敌人就想着感染一下看看会有什么意想不到的效果； 4---武器和道具系统，种类太少了，一共就几样武器，但是基本全程就是镐子和回力标，尤其是最后升级后镐子还能蓄力远程，其他的几个更没啥用处了，纯粹是填充背包拿成就，物品也不如初代的有多又好用，能用的地方很多，有用的地方太少； 5---双地图的解密也很坑，当你获得了拉传送门的技能后，会发现并不是所有的地方都有副地图，如果运气不好，或者说没想到这里也能进入，非常容易卡关（也可能是有提示，我没认真看）； 6---和初代比，隐藏的东西太少了，基本没有什么探宝的乐趣，基本就是从头到尾走一遍就能拿个七七八八，缺少初代那种挖石头突然发现一条新路，拿到新道具发现可以穿过之前根本过不去的地方的的欣喜； 7---节奏慢，习惯了初代再体验这一代，初上手会不适应，不光是武器的改变，还有攻击手段的单调，boss战也没有太多激情，毕竟初代是可以当魂斗罗打的； 8---音乐差了点，没有初代那种能记住的（个人感觉）；  [h1] 以上，如果没玩过的话，还是推荐玩一玩的，看了我的评测还在犹豫的话可以等打折，但是还是值得一玩的 [/h1]

[h1]一句话评测：为寻女儿入南极，悬疑解密加穿越。敌人遍布攻击强，及时存档死又生。[/h1] 《公理边缘》这游戏，玩过一代的应该都知晓，更接近于老版本的密特罗德。  当然如果你玩过最新的NS版本的密特罗德的话，除了3d效果的与时俱进，第一人称与第三人称的流畅切换外，基本上也是2D的底子。 [h1][quote=] [table]     [tr]         [th]　欢迎关注 [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/38287879]网易UU鉴赏家培养计划[/url][/th]     [/tr]     [tr]         [th]　来这里找优质游戏、开黑日友、白嫖UU时长卡　[/th]     [/tr]     [tr]         [th]　加速Steam社区、游戏记得用UU哦_(:з」∠)_　[/th]     [/tr] [/table] [/quote][/h1] 而这一次《公理边缘2》的虽然在氛围上与1代的相同，但是有许多地方进行了某种不是太密特罗德的改变。 说不上好还是不好，大概就跟大家一起来梳理一下。  [h1]节奏缓慢[/h1] 游戏里的角色偏多，不论是NPC，还是你获得技能后与灵魂的对话，都让整游戏的节凑开始变得停顿下来。 这一点不论是跟1比，还是跟最新的NS的密特罗德比，其实节奏都变得十分缓慢，本来是一个快进快出，不停战斗，然后迅速切换下一场战斗的游戏。  由于你看到了人，必然会停下来对话，当然你可以不停下来，这是由我们玩家决定的。 但制作者的设定，是有NPC在这里，是为了要交代剧情呀。你要是不对话，那不就是遗失了内容了。  这么做有一个极大的问题，就是，我们在快速奔跑、迅速战斗的时候，本来大脑在快速运转，接下来的敌人遇到后，如何杀死，然后迅速清图。  但《公理边缘2》的机制设定造成了，很多的时候，你都被迫停止了，比如说角色的对话，再比如说不停地 进入到新的屋子，以及遇到的收集的元素，你必须停下来按一个新的按键，才可以读取。  总的来说一旦这种打破迅速移动与战斗的元素出现了，就开始不停地叠加了。  接下来我们就会遇到，各种技能说明的复杂化，比如密特罗德的技能说明，基本上就一个弹出框，再加上进紧接的一个教程地图，你不会用这个技能，就去不了下一个地图。  而在《公理边缘2》里，这里你遇到的新技能，是由人的灵魂改变的，所以你们之间产生了很多必要的情感交流，而交流的过程就拉满了速度。  同样的当速度不快的时候，敌人的速杀也不是太可能，所以比起传统的一枪一个或者两枪一个，这个游戏里的时候，你基本要打好几下才能杀死敌人，想要速通地图，你只能不停地逃跑，而不是击杀。  游戏的战斗虽然有近战与远程两个部分，但是远程的伤害并不足够高，往往需要近战的配合。这种配合就要求，你需要有准确地距离判断，所以停顿、蹲下来攻击，甚至跳跃躲避，都成为了必须的判断选择。  所以你看对话、收集、进入房间以及战斗都让我们的快速清图发生了改变，必须要慢慢地一点一点地清理里面的所有敌人。   [h1]战斗方式的改变[/h1]  单独脱离了密特罗德的一些公式一般的信息外，其实这个游戏，我觉得这个游戏做的并不差，只是与1代有着似乎不太相同的制作思路。  游戏的剧情在这里占据了很重要的一个部分，与其说这是一个战斗驱动，不如说这合适一个剧情与战斗同等重要的驱动游戏。  比如我们角色是一个来自于大公司的总裁，来南极寻找自己的女儿，然后在冒险的过程里，发现了另外的一个世界。  在这个游戏里，你很大的谜题都在解决这个难题，你如何通过找到自己女儿，如何在另一个世界里活下去的，如何发现另一个世界的历史，以及这个世界是怎样的。  从游戏的剧情里，得知，新世界的历史，很有可能是从米索不达米亚文明延续下来的另一个文明，毕竟是平行世界，有无数的可能性。  由于游戏里有一部分是服务于剧情的，所以这一次的演出也还是可以的。比如最开始，你下飞机，寻找对应的发电机，然后打开灯，再听到最后的别人用声音来引导你，让你如何战斗，如何找到被围困的瓶子里的另一个世界的人。   所以你在整个游戏里，解密这个世界是一个十分重要的内容。 甚至连整个游戏的战斗方式，都有一定的解密元素。  比如说，游戏里你也会使用一种武器，这种武器与本身分离，投出一个生物，进行转移的解密方式。  这种行为虽然可以理解，但是很容易拖垮战斗的节奏，要等待，一个心思两份使用。 同时战斗的方式也采用了一种入侵的方式，通过按键侵入到敌人的机制里，你可以选择让其与你一体，也可以选择让其爆炸等等。  但这两个操作，依旧是让整个战斗特别缓慢。  [h1]总体上是一个还算可以，但是玩起来与1代颇有点不同的游戏。感兴趣的话，大家可以玩玩看。[/h1]

[h1] 媚びてないから、華がない [/h1] [h1] でもね、とびっきりピュアなんだ [/h1] [h1] 例えるなら、メトロイドヴァニアの純粋種 [/h1] [h1] アクシオムバージってさ、これでいいと思う [/h1] [h1] 【クリア所感：17時間プレイで達成率97％くらい】 [/h1] 　このゲームは、製作者からの挑戦状「マップ踏破&アイテム収集１００％できるかな？」です。２は、１と比べてより探索色が濃くなってます。前作同様、人類・あちらの世界・さらなる上位世界のなんやかんやというストーリーはあります。全てがパワーアップした続編というよりも、[b]前作の味付けを変えた [/b]って感じです。前作は派手な作り、今作は地味だけどアイテム収集の謎解きがストイックなつくりです。 　 「マップ探索難しかった！」です。明らかに不自然な場所にあるアイテム、バリアや格子での行き止まり、頭上に広がる未探索マップ。メロトイドヴァイニアンを刺激するマップデザインが素晴らしい。前作のように「こんなところにすり抜けできる壁が！」という解法もありますが、もう少し広域で解かせる感じです。マップを彷徨いまくり。探索欲が刺激されて大満足です。 　 　一方で、前作と大きく異なる部分もあるわけで。ボス戦があまりにもあっさり過ぎる。白熱のボスバトルを演出することはなく、ふつうのマップに普通にいて、倒したいならどーぞご自由に、みたいな感じですｗ え？　「いちおうボスは用意したけど、今作は探索特化なんで」って開発者の心の声が聞こえました。前作のボスバトル好きだったんだけどな～。倒すメリットは、キャラの育成ポイントだけです。主人公やボスのリアクションとかありませんｗ残念ですｗ  　クリア後に印象に残ったことは「ひたすらアイテム収集の謎かけを解いていく＆ひたすらマップを彷徨う」「ストーリーは前作よりあっさり。特に演出がすっごくあっさり」「アクション性は完璧、２Dプラットフォームの鑑」「攻撃が棒で殴るのとブーメランだけでちょっと派手さがない。派手に炸裂する銃も欲しかった」。こんなところです ^^) 総じて満足です。  最後に、 [b] シリーズ初見さんには、１作目をおススメします [/b]

I want to recommend this game because of how good the first one was. I really want to. But this just doesn't carry the same punch as a sequel. I knew going in that there wouldn't be guns. Sure, fine, whatever. The exploration is fun and kind of interesting. The fast travel was a welcome addition. The spritework is fantastic. The music is good.  Maybe this game set itself up for failure by calling itself a sequel and then failing to deliver on that. It's in the same world, which is cool and all, though I'm not much of a lore guy in general, so it raised the expectation on the gameplay.  This game is sooo close to earning a positive review from me, except that it suffers from a few critical flaws:  - No bosses- or at least not any real ones that block your way. Just some big robots in certain rooms that don't respawn and drop some upgrades when they die. - I got totally stuck because I think the game spawned the wrong upgrade somewhere. Or at least that's what a guide I found seems to indicate. - Stick sensitivity. The up/down deadzone is far too sensitive, causing you to run up stairs when you least expect it and drop through platforms when you mean to jump. This is inexcusable for a game that has already been out for a year and leaves me even more baffled when noting that the character has a walking animation.  This game just doesn't have the same oomph and wonder in it as the first game. The whole Epic store situation is irrelevant as far as I'm concerned and you should ignore all the review bombing related to it.  It was a good effort from Thomas Happ, but it fell flat IMO. Maybe I'll finish it one day, but I think I'm ready to put it down without finishing it. I am thoroughly disappointed because I liked the first one enough to beat it twice (or maybe three times, IDK)

People who criticize this title are really not fair with this game. They were hoping for a sequel being more like a copy paste of the first and if this what they'd got, they would have complained it was the same all over again.  Anyway, the game is good, it's a well thought metroidvania, there is no real boss fight as in classic metroidvania but instead axed it on exploration and there is a new light "rpg element" where you can collect sort of skill points and increase stats like health, attack, ... how you see fit.  I heard Thomas Happ wanted to do some kind fantasy metroidvania but stayed in a science-fiction world. As a result, the  game is mainly melee fighting, it works pretty well, it's different than guns but it isn't bad.  Gameplay wise, it works, nothing to say, just like the first one, controls are responsive and exploring is pretty fun. You can set reminders on the map and mark the area where you should return to inspect something once you get the correct power and it's welcomed. I used that feature a bit late and I ended with 92% items collected as a result when I was hoping to 100% it.   Length wise, As you can see, I took 18h to complete it but I explored a lot and turned in circle to find how to get to some places a bunch of times. The game isn't really long but this is correct for a metroidvania. I felt like you were getting new powers crazy fast which isn't a bad thing and I could appreciate having almost no boss.  Graphics are pretty cool, same quality as the first game, at one point you also go back to 8 bit graphics instead of 16.  The music is equivalent to the first game, it's pretty entertaining. Some tracks are really cool some other I don't like that much and I had the same feeling during the first game.   Some people say the first game had better graphics and better soundtrack, I don't think so, the game very much succeeds at what it's trying to be.  The story is somewhat linked to first game but you do not have to play it to understand what's happening. It's rather complex but easier to understand than in the first game. To sum it up without spoilers, you're a woman going after her daughter, you use a mysterious portal and you find yourself in a new world invaded by drones and machines.  Doesn't sound great? Well, there's more to it and it's hard to talk about the details without spoiling it and ruining the pleasure to discover each and every events.  And while the game, the story and the music can feel familiar and like you've seen/heard it somewhere already (especially if you played axiom verge 1), it always succeeds to add something new.  But all in all, it's a really fun and great game. Highly recommend, especially if you liked the first.  ***Now, a bit of spoiler warning about powers***  At one point, you will be able to turn yourself into a drone and that drone will get super useful skills to explore the landscape. As a result, I spent most of my time as a drone rather than a human. This can be a bit weird / disappointing but it's still enjoyable.  You also get the power to hack enemies and occasionally some doors, it's not explained very well but you need to invest points in your skill tree to hack higher level doors (the skill, at the moment, only mention hacking enemies) This was a bit confusing. Also, I saw some people playing like crazy with hacking, for my part, I rarely used it because it did cost too much energy and was only temporary + it was faster to just attack them. There are some pretty cool stuff you can do with hacking though and you need to use it two or three times to access hidden objects.  *** Spoiler Alert on secrets which you might not notice ***  During the game, you will see some stones with an eye drawn on it. (mark a reminder on the map each time you see one) You should use your "sight" skill next to it which usually reveal hidden portals. The stone is always an indication there is a secret nearby but for some stones, in red, you will see 8 big arrows (4 top, 4 bottom) appearing. Just input the "code" near the stone (by reading the arrows left to right, top to bottom) and it will modify the landscape to allow you to reach a secret item.   The game contains many secrets, they're fun to track and some are really hard to find. I had to google some of them (luckily the game has coordinates so it's not too hard to ask about which secrets you're looking for)
For get all these low and mixed reviews. This game is amazing. Gameplay, plot, exploration.
Interesting use of abilities as story tool.

I connected with the world of Axiom Verge 2 quite a bit more than Axiom Verge 1.  My favorite aspect of the metroidvania genre is exploration, and I just really love the vibe of this world.  The soundtrack perfectly fits the mood, as well, and elevates the experience of just moving around in this environment.  With the addition of the breach world, a sort of low-res alternate universe that connects with the main world in certain points, I was favorably reminded of Knytt Underground, one of my favorite games of all time.  For what its worth re: crashes, which some other reviews are mentioning, I bought this game at launch, and experienced frequent crashes for the first few hours after release, but a patch appears to have been quickly pushed through that same day which resolved all of the issues.  I find the  game to be perfectly stable since this patch, and it runs with no problems.
挺失望的，1代剧情挺有悬疑而且气氛环境设计很有意思。这代就保留了部分设计和1代环境，剧情和初代什么关系也没有。我原本非常期待初代故事的后续。 操作上角色和无人机模式更加分裂了，所以解谜程度增加，爽快感大幅下降。

Thoughful experience in a well crafted Grand World.  Really tries to innovate from axiom verge 1 and other metroidvania. Not always sucessfully. The game is not that easy and requires your attention. However the game suffers from a lack of true boss fight. (There is another systeme in it's stead) The biggest problem is the lack of a climax at the end of the game, gameplay wise and story wise.  It really feels like the studio run out of budget and could not tell the story at the scope they wanted.  They they still made the best of it. The simple version is this:  Axiom Verge 1 was better.  Axiom Verge 2 is still very good, but Axiom Verge 1 was better.  Get both, but maybe wait for a sale or a bundle for Axiom Verge 2 if you must (I mean its only $20 smackers.)  But yeah. I like what I've seen so far.

It's a good game. I think it suffers from Chrono Cross syndrome, though, what with it having big shoes to fill and deviating quite a bit from how the first game played.  You will not be getting any wacky guns in this game, and I'm sure that'll be a turn off for a lot of people. This game does, however, find other ways to make up for that. It has a new core mechanic that I find very interesting, and one of the things I wanted most in the first game but didn't get: Fast Travel. There's a certain something you spend part of the game getting back, and by the time you do, I find that it feels mostly useless.  100%ing this game feels a lot more fair than the first game, though I can still think of a specific collectible that I did not feel was fairly placed. The music is also a lot catchier than it was in the first game, and the story feels significantly more coherent. The graphics are very pretty, and the world feels quite diverse. I did feel like mobility was lacking at times, but overall it's a very fun game, and I'm glad I played it. I'd probably give it a B. Worth playing, but not something I plan to replay unless something new gets added.

Esse jogo é uma experiência fantástica em questão de metroidvania, pode não ser o mais bonito, mas com certeza é muito bem feito. Ele é completamente diferente do primeiro jogo Axiom Verge 2, mas isso não faz dele ruim, praticamente tudo é novo e instigante, mecânicas novas, gameplay nova, estlio de música, tudo mesmo. A exploração é bem legal também fazendo você fuçar todo canto possível pra ir atrás de segredos e afins. A trilha sonora também é incrível, pessoalmente é uma das melhores que eu já escutei num jogo desse estilo em muito tempo, é tudo tão diferente só que com uma vibe mais caricata, nem eu sei explicar exatamente. A história também é bem interessante e os eventos que acontecem são dignos do primeiro jogo também. Independente de problemas externos, esse jogo sem dúvida é um ótimo metroidvania, recomendo muitíssimo todo mundo jogar ele.

For starters, this is not a shooter like the previous game. The gameplay is melee based. Closer to Hollow Knight than Metroid.  In the beginning I was skeptical, because this game is a lot different than the first one. New character, new themes and visuals, new gameplay style.  But hear me out, it's a great game. If you enjoy metroidvanias, can't miss this one. It only keeps getting better as you progress and unlock new powers to traverse the map. The new powers are fun and creative. And the map design is really good.  The game starts kinda slow, but it grows on you as you go on.  The soundtrack is 10 out of 10. And why is the sound when you charge an attack so damn loud? I rarely use charged attacks because of this.  Personally I think I like AV1 the most, but nonetheless, AV2 is definitely worth playing.
While it isn't quite as good as the first game, it's still a pretty decent game overall. A solid 3.5 out of 5. Hoping for DLC or something similar to improve it.

I feel like a lot of people are being emotional in judging this game. I will try to be as pragmatic as I can to give a clear view of what I experienced. I will also mention that I did finish the game as well as got 100% completion (items/map).  [b]Bugs/crashes:[/b] I did not experience a single crash and I don't recall encountering any bugs at all in my 14 hours of play.  [b]Exploration/Map:[/b] The game world is rather big. As in it takes up a lot of space. There are two main "worlds" you traverse. The Overworld and The Breach. The Overworld is a huge natural landscape with different biomes. As such, a lot of screens could feel same-y because of the natural/ruin/abandoned nature of the world. It still is a lot of space and there's a lot to explore and there is a fair bit of variety. Both in level design and also gameplay of each biome. The Breach on the other hand feels like a more retro version of the world, both in design and gameplay. The screens are smaller. The map is not as big and expansive as the Overworld and I would say the main use of it is traversal during the main story. There really isn't much use to going to the Breach once you have most of the game explored other than for hunting secrets. I should also mention that the Breach is a lot more dangerous than the Overworld with more enemies and tight spaces.  [b]Gameplay:[/b] The main gameplay of the game is exploration. The abilities you find throughout the game are almost all used for traversal. There are two types of enemies in the game. Normal enemies you encounter all over the world, and stronger, unique enemies you may call "bosses". Combat in the game I would say does work but it is lackluster. Up until near the end of the game, almost all normal enemies are an annoyance mainly because of the melee combat. That being said combat is not your only option. There's also hacking that you can invest some skill points into and use against most enemies to ease the fight. But again it's a simple system and doesn't really make you feel smart or skilled for using it. The "bosses" I'm sad to say are all pushovers, every single one. There's no contact damage so you can just go into the boss' body and spam melee and there's a good chance you won't die. On top of that, spending some time upgrading your damage skills and finding good weapons makes combat trivial, and fast.  [b]Comparison To First Game:[/b] I see so many reviews mentioning how the game is not as good as the first one. I personally don't agree with this sentiment. Reason being Axiom Verge 2 has evolved and built upon what Axiom Verge did best. The map is so much bigger. The unique abilities used for traversal and combat are still there and I would say they've become more integrated and fit together very well to enhance your experience. Another major complaint mentioned in other reviews is the overall theme and feel of the world not being alien. That is both true and false. It's true that the game does not at all feel like the first game but that's not a bad thing in itself. I think what has disappointed some is just the bland/abandoned feel the new world. It's just a different theme altogether, and I personally didn't mind it. That being said, the world is not all nature and landscapes. There are structures and environments that have the same alien feel the first game had, but again they are all mostly abandoned or in ruins, as is the theme of the world.  [b]Conclusion:[/b] Axiom Verge 2 does have its shortcomings, mainly in the lackluster combat and to an extent having a mostly bland world. It really is a completely different feeling from the first game. However, in my experience, as I explored about half of the game, due to the abilities and upgrades I had acquired, and the variety in biomes and so many different areas to explore, I did start to enjoy the game more and more and in the end I did really enjoy moving around and exploring the world to the point that I decided to find every item and explore the entire map. It's understandable that the game did not meet many players' expectations coming from the first game however the game itself is not a bad game.

Axiom Verge 2 is a litte bit shorter than Axiom Verge 1 but I still think I got my money's worth. Axiom Verge 2 is the right game for you if you enjoy Metroidvanias (2D platformer with a focus on exploration and finding pieces of equipment / abilities so you can cross into new territories... and if you don't mind a bit of running into (temporary) dead ends and having to do a bit of backtracking).  There are references to AV1 but you don't need to have played it to enjoy AV2. Combat has more focus on melee and is easier than in AV1.  Some said they liked AV1 better, but I think that can mostly be explained with different expectations. Coming into AV1 nobody knew what hey could expect, coming into AV2... well, some things are similar but some are different and so is the general vibe, but it's still great.  Thanks, Tom Happ, I really enjoyed it.

I didn't like this on release. Coming back to it a few years later I really despise it.  Bad platforming where the combat is unfair and you take so much damage that the main goal of the game seems to be to avoid enemies, except you can run past most enemies without losing health or just use your disposable drone so it becomes tedious gameplay with no real consequence for loss. The weapons are rubbish, even upgraded they aren't enjoyable or precise or powerful to play with. You constantly feel like you're fighting bad enemy movement and placement to get a hit in.  The world feels disconnected and unrelated, one second you're in snow and the next you're swimming through water to a desert. There is no joy of exploration here, it's just tedium to get to the next part to get a small part of a meaningless message. The Sumerian theme and story is unrelateable and full of unexplained rubbish. There is no compelling reason to move the story forward.  Overall this really feels like the creator was just experimenting with disjointed game ideas, or intentionally going against metroidvania design for no good reason, ignoring the fact that none of those ideas work well, then quickly slapped it together into a release to make a quick buck. Similar to how Iga had no idea what made SotN good so HoD is unbalanced and awful.  I consider the first Axiom Verge to be a masterpiece and one of the best games ever made. I really wanted to like this too but it's just unplayable drek. Huge disappointment.

1と違って近接攻撃がメインになっていますし、明確な上位互換の武器に更新していく形になります。 自分は1のショット自体の威力はさほど変わらずとも、ショット毎の個性を使い分けて閉所や複数の敵を倒しながら進む戦略性を非常に好んでいたので、その点では残念に感じました。  ストーリーは前作とのつながりは殆どありませんが、良質なストーリーと言っていいと思います。風味というか、AxiomVergeらしい雰囲気を保っています。自らの正気を疑うような考察要素に関しては少なくなっていますが、新しい主人公もタフで好ましい性格だと思います。  音楽は神秘的で孤独感を感じる曲が多く場面にも合っていて非常に良いと思います。  どうやら全てのボスを倒す必要はなく、攻略上の自由度はかなり高いであろう印象があります。スピードランモードが最初から解放されているため、ルート構築を考えるプレイヤーには非常にお薦めできると思います。 進行ルートに関係ないボスを倒した際の報酬がスキルポイントになっていて、スキル自体も基礎能力の向上だけですので、達成感という点では劣るかもしれません。  序盤中盤までは能力に制限のかかるブリーチ内の探索がつまらなく感じましたが、2層になっているMAPを見比べながらルートを試行錯誤できる能力を手に入れてからは楽しめた。  前作と同じく操作性は完璧に良くて、プラットフォーマーとして素晴らしい出来です。  前作ファンでしたらプレイしても損はないと思います。 ただし、前作を未プレイでしたら、シナリオの繋がりが等といった意味ではなく、純粋にゲームとして1の方が面白いと思いますので、そちらをプレイしてみることをお勧めします。  海外のレビューでクラッシュが多発するということが多く書かれていましたが、自分の環境ではそのような事はありませんでした。 windows10 64bit/corei7 7700k/GTX1080

It's OK  It is missing a lot of stuff I was expecting from Axiom Verge 1.  - It's a prequel / side story so no answers on what happened to Athetos / Trace - No arsenal of guns, You've got a boomerang, melee weapon, and that's it. Late game Update: The game comes together by the end. The cosmic body horror starts to come into play. And most importantly I finally figured out how the game actually functions and in that regard it is actually very well made: [spoiler] The main focus of the game is your drone. Its movement abilities are far superior to your human body. It gains a grabling hook and flight.   Also the game is twice as big as you thought since the breach this time is basically a second world. What you thought were small pocket dimensions is actually 1 connected map. You can later enter and exit this dimension at will and reemerge at the same spot in the other world. Which makes it a huge exploration dream land.[/spoiler]  Also after even 10 hours of play I had not a single crash, which is probably since I still have Win 7 on my PC. So my guess is that Win 10 or 11 just f***ed something up in the recent updates or similar.    Original Text:   If you were to play Axiom Verge 2 before 1 you would be forgiven for thinking that AV2 was the first game developed by a single person and AV1 was the more refined 2nd game he made after.   Its still an ok game, I do not regret purchasing... buuut   The tonal shift is weird. AV1 had a very dark tone to it that fitted the eldrich horrors you face. I mean the line "I think I am beyond fear already" stuck with me for the longest time. Here the opening sequence is very clunky and feels very off beat. We casually drown and get revived. And it doesn't seem to faze the protagonist one bit. And all that normal green nature doesn't really give you the same feeling like the Metroid inspired tile set.   Gameplay wise, you don't get a gun but only an awful boomerang (which doesn't mean that the enemies in the game aren't still best disposed of by ranged attacks with most being flying enemies) So there isn't actually a melee focused stealth gameplay here even though the vision cones and stealth bushes might suggest that this was the intention.   And then there are the bugs. My screenshot folder can be renamed bug report at this point. My drone has 2 hacking abilities that block me from correctly assigning the abilities correctly as the bugged one cant be reassigned or removed. Restarting doesn't fix it and I already have overwritten my savefile. Once hacking glitched out and was stuck in an endless animation. Hacked enemies turned friendly will have their AI bug out. I personally didn't experience crashes but looking at the other reviews that might be just a matter of time.   In the end its still an ok game to play. The exploration is still good and some of the new hacking interactions are interesting. But this is a far worse experience than AV1.

***EDIT1: Huh,lots of negative reviews for some reason, so i decided to give some feedback on common complaints:  "constant crashes" -  Idk what their problem might be, but in 12h of play the game didnt crash even once for me. "not like the Axiom Verge 1" - imo its not a good idea for a game to repeat itself and use the same tricks over and over again. So i for once,glad that AV2 is not copy-pasted AV1 with different locations and characters "characters are humans,not interesting" - guys,this game was made literally by ONE person,not a big team with writers, scenarists and whatnot. Do not expect AAA-lvl stuff from an indie game. For its price its quite good ***  This is a metroidvania in a sci-fi style with all the usual stuff: non-linear exploration, different biomes, abilities that allow to access new parts of the game world, upgrades for main character, tonns of collectibles, etc. Combat is fast-paced, real time beat em up with a decent selection of melee and ranged weapons. Later in game you will be able to hack enemies,which affects their behaviour (stop using certain abilities, fight for player, move fast/slow,etc).   PROS  +interesting exloration abilities, not your usual double jump,dash,etc +good biom variety, no boring forests,sea,volcanoes,castle (and other typical biomes) levels +nice variety of enemies, many have unique abilities/movesets +decent artstyle, sprites are well-done +good plot and lore. not a masterpiece, but got me interested enough +fair amount of content for its price - ive finished the game with ~95% completion in 12h +custom markers on the world map! (so you will have easier time getting back for previously inaccessible stuff). Thats actually important, but few game devs bother to do it  CONS  -bosses are too few and very weak, they really could use some balancing or new weapons/tactics. you will kill most (if not all) of them on the first (mb second) try, if you are somewhat familiar with genre -some weapons are clearly inferior to others and make no sense to use. For example, the remote controlled boomerang is used exactly once in the entire game  TL;DR  Good game for a couple of days. If you like metroidvanias - you wont regret playing it

Ok, I started giving a negative review due to crashes, preventing me playing the game after waiting to play it for so long. Now that I have finished it, time to make it right.  [h1]Gameplay[/h1] • Overall time to finish : if you are used to the genre, count around 8h. 10-11h if you aim 100%. • Like Axiom Verge, the game is a Metroidvania, with an emphasis on exploration, powering up and backtracking. Your loadout change compared to the first game to where you have a melee weapon as the primary way to deal damage, and secondary weapon to deal or poke damage from afar.  • The game is incredibly tough when you start, due to your lack of range and power, and you quickly face enemies that can obliterate you, forcing you to use stealth, or just flee. This change as soon as you find the Infection urn, that allows you to hack enemies and get the upper hand. However, once you get past a game-changing point in the game, you can just start to "rofl into the enemies' faces" melee attacks and take damage without much of consequences, that is aggravated now since you can now store health in advance. • Controls are nice. Indra feels a bit clunky due to the fact she has more inertia than Trace, but I never had any issue when I wanted to go exactly to a specific spot. • You have less "weapon junk" compared to the first game, which is great. But you have still some : why would I play with a "late-found" dagger/sword when my axes have special/powerful moves for a while ? • Infection is the Address Disruptor of the game, but better. • The Breach is nice : it's a world itself this time and is interconnected to the overworld. You have to think in both dimensions to open new areas, or find secrets, which is really neat. But it doesn't feel anymore like a place where you shouldn't be, and lost what made the secret areas so dangerous and exciting in the first game.  • Level design is excellent, with lots of shortcuts you can unlock, with or without upgrades [spoiler]Teleportation, finally![/spoiler]. Moving through levels never felt like a chore, on the contrary of the first game where sometime, moving through Indi was the only way to go faster from one area to another. • Bosses are "non-existent", which is good and bad at the same time. You can also entirely skip them, which is a rather innovative way to progress in a Metroidvania, especially for people who struggle in this kind of fights. However, kudos to MUS A M20, which was really interesting to defeat. I wish all bosses had something similar to defeat them, instead of just hack/spam attacks.  • Secrets and 100% hunting : the game is not as generous as the first one, and we even lost some "think outside the box" puzzles (those who needed cypher translation) from Axiom Verge, in exchange for some "Konami codes". But it's still enjoyable nonetheless, and for people like me who cannot play a Metroidvania without 100% the s*** out of it, the game will still combles you very well. Especially with the extra juicy addition of optional upgrades that will help you to track anything you potentially missed, which is something I really like to have integrated in the game, instead of just going into a wiki or solution on the net.  [h1]Art[/h1] • Color are less vibrant/flashy (except in the Breach), and all graphical elements have finer details. It lost the charm of the first game, but at the same time, it has another kind of charm to it. Ultimately, I like the pixel art we have in Axiom Verge 2 : you have so much details compared to the first game. • Still feels a bit off compared to the first game : you are in another world, but it's not as Alien as Sudra. It makes highly odd to see such advanced technology in general in a world where vestiges of civilization show more "Indian/Egyptian" ruins. However, this is not necessarily a bad point, since it serves perfectly the story, and you will still have some surprises, that will counter this very argument. • The Breach (inside) is less "glitchy" and more structured with "clean" pixel art. It was needed to make a new interconnected map that would not be a mess, but it lost what made this area special in Axiom Verge.  [h1]OST[/h1] • In Axiom Verge 2, you will have lots of "Indian tunes", which may not be to the liking of everyone. But it serves the gameplay/story : when you will find the Earth campement, you will have a music that is more like the first game, but once you go further, you will be immerged in Kiengir's culture that you know nothing of... Well, with the exception of specific places, where memories will instantly flow once you have reached them. • Like the first game, there is some tracks that I didn't like, others that are good, and specific ones that are outstanding (Ex: "[url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Vk02QLMdilA&list=OLAK5uy_nkjZkHxRGWNPCgh5aGNYyPTUmy4yhnMuI&index=7]Procession of the Anunna[/url]"). • I couldn't help but stupidly smile when I heard remix of the first game tracks. Best part ? It's intentional and has a meaning lore-wise when you "read between the lines", when you consider everything you read/see in those places while hearing those tracks. • I couldn't help but be stupidly exalted when I heard the final boss music. I would need some severe amnesia to forget such a track !   [h1]Story[/h1] • You can play the game without playing the first one, but it will be better if you do. • The ambiance lost a part of the "glitch" feeling, that you "hack" through reality. It's still somewhat present thanks to the Breach, but it felt less impactful. • Like the first game, if you like to think about it, it will make you question about our relationship with certain themes, like machines, religion and evolution. • Right after starting the game, you will realize this is a prequel to Axiom Verge. So sadly, no. We will not know what happened to Trace we incarnate in the first game. • Story is not exposed and will be mediocre at best (like the first game) if you rush, don't care about notes, or don't pause a bit to think about what it implies.  [h2]Related to Axiom Verge story[/h2] • If you paid attention to details, the story in Axiom Verge 2 will not quench your thirst on what will happen after the first game, but [b]will[/b] gives you informations that will make several pieces of the first game fall into place, and will allow you to speculate on what will happen, in both games. • If you remember parts of Axiom Verge' story, you will get that Dr. Hammond is the link between this game and the first one, and that Trace's epiphany has triggered indirectly the events in Axiom Verge 2. • If you greatly remember Axiom Verge' story (or took an express wakeup call on the wiki, works fine too), you will realize, at some part or at the end of the game, that you are witnessing events that will lead to the birth of Axiom Verge story [spoiler]The literal birth of Rusalki, and by an obvious extension, Ophelia[/spoiler], and clues to what will likely happen if there is an Axiom Verge 3 [spoiler]Accessing the Mother world with a PatternMind, the last one I know conveniently put into a coma.[/spoiler].  [h1]TL;DR : Conclusion[/h1] Axiom Verge 2 lost parts of the first game that made/felt it special, but enhanced lots of points that were defaults in my eyes. You will not get answers if you expected some for Axiom Verge's end, but you will get new keys to understand what happened, and what will probably happen next. The game is exactly like the first one : a solid Metroidvania that will reward exploration, and even more, since combat is not the main focus of the game, something that can be loved or hated, depending on what your preferences are. However, there is no reasons to not buy it if you like the genre, and if you didn't played the first one, you have now two incredible games to play instead of one.

Now that I've done everything the game has to offer I can give a full review.  1. Atmosphere The world doesn't feel alien except some small segments. This may be a draw for some, but for me it feels off-putting since I was a huge fan of the aesthetics of the original. The seemingly Indian inspired music really never sat well with me personally. Didn't like it in Axiom verge 1, don't like it here. A bad experience with a stalker puts me off of it. May not be an issue for some.  2. Characters No Trace. Not a fan of the new protagonist. She's a major global corporate CEO buying out people who are actually talented, and she seems to hate people in general. What's there to love? [spoiler] She becomes somewhat more tolerable at the VERY end of the game, but it's arguably not Indra.  You will find early on that there are other humans around in the area you explore. It really removes the alien feel the original did so well. Not to mention that most of the NPCs become minibosses after a while if you interact with them using the reformed humanoid form/drone [/spoiler]   3. Skill tree  May not be something which is well liked by many who play metroidvanias. I like to explore so I can unlock new things to explore more (creating a nice and simple feedback loop), such as the original or hollow knight. Much less a fan of unlocking a base ability and upgrading them like in this game and Ori and the blind forest (the latter of which is more forgivable). The only things that are substantial for upgrading with skill points are the hacking strength and max health.  4. Exploration  Level design feels bland simply because there are so few tools to explore with for a huge portion of the game. The mobility in this game is interesting for a metroidvania, but it isn't great or terrible. The early game exploration options are not good, but they expand and become somewhat satisfying in the late midgame and on. The drone is the most entertaining tool to explore with and hacking is not particularly interesting, other than the fact you can't hack things as much as you want as it takes ammunition to perform certain hacks which is probably off-putting to many.  5. Bugs  Lots of crashing. (Edit: patch fixed as of 8/12/22)  6. The weaponry and combat.  There are only two bosses you need to fight and only one of those you fight traditionally. The combat in this game leaves a LOT to be desired. Few weapons, fewer weapon upgrades, and almost everything requires you to get in the face of enemies which can sometimes literally snipe you from across the room. Boomerang, while mid-ranged, is not a great weapon even using its nanite abilities. Your character has very little health naturally and until you get most of the upgrades or backtrack the whole map every time you get a major exploration upgrade you will be weak until near endgame.  7. Story The story is pretty unsatisfactory. The main character swears in practically every other statement she makes and she gives off the whole "I want to be a man with breasts" vibe for most of the game rather than leaning into ANY feminine traits. [spoiler] She tries to find her daughter and a way to escape the whole game, but it never feels like a real goal. When she loses her body trying to split her consciousness and ends up stuck in the drone while her body is possessed by the first apocalypse arm. She finds something to upgrade the drone to be able to take a humanoid form. Then she finds her daughter... sort of, Then she speaks to the computer which has been trying to kill her with machines the whole game and agrees to work with it to stop the antagonist, while it's still trying to kill her... stops the antagonist and then gives up on her goal for escaping, and seemingly also reuniting with her daughter... [/spoiler] The only decent parts of the story are how it relates to the first game, and those references aren't particularly prevalent.  8. MAJOR GRIPE Accessibility options. the only thing in there which I can see as being not made to detract from the game is upping contrast for platforms to make them easier to see. If that was the only thing I wouldn't care. That said, there are three other options. Alter damage your character takes, the damage your character deals, and the speed of the game.  You can turn off taking damage. You can make most enemies die without effort. You can slow down the game to the point reflexes of a sloth can still let you play perfectly. AND YOU CAN EARN EVERY ACHIEVEMENT WITH THEM ON. In fact there's an achievement for actually USING one of these options. It kills any sense of achievement if a literal two year old with no effort and a lot of time on their hands would be able to get every achievement... Speaking of which, the speedrun achievement Overclocked gives you 6 HOURS to beat the game when even playing casually you can beat it in two.  Final judgement.
